_metadata.description: Philosophia complete data completion — ALL concepts from the 365-concept library with full enrichment.
_metadata.instruction: For each object in completion[], find the matching concept in concepts.json by 'name' (exact match, case-sensitive). Add or overwrite: thinkerBio, quote, tags, related, books. All other fields (description, thinker, category, era, difficulty) remain unchanged.
_metadata.affiliate_note: books[].url uses format https://bookshop.org/a/philosophia/ISBN — replace ISBN with real ISBN-13 after joining the Bookshop.org affiliate program (bookshop.org/affiliates, instant approval, 10% commission).

schema.name: string — must exact-match the concept's name in concepts.json
schema.thinkerBio: string — 1-2 sentences on the primary thinker(s) in historical context
schema.quote: string — a notable quote illuminating the concept
schema.tags: array of 3-5 lowercase strings for Obsidian YAML frontmatter
schema.books: array of 3 objects: {title, author, url, why}

completion.name: A Priori / A Posteriori
completion.thinkerBio: Immanuel Kant (1724–1804) gave the distinction its modern philosophical form in the Critique of Pure Reason (1781); Saul Kripke's Naming and Necessity (1972) showed that necessity and a priority come apart — one of the most important results in twentieth-century analytic philosophy.
completion.quote: Thoughts without content are empty, intuitions without concepts are blind.

related.name: Analytic / Synthetic Distinction
related.note: Kant's a priori/a posteriori distinction intersects with the analytic/synthetic — his central question is how synthetic a priori knowledge is possible.

related.name: Essentialism
related.note: Kripke showed essential truths are necessary but a posteriori — dissolving Kant's equation of the necessary with the a priori.

related.name: Naturalized Epistemology
related.note: Quine's dissolution of the analytic-synthetic distinction was simultaneously an attack on the a priori as a philosophically significant category.

related.name: Cogito Ergo Sum
related.note: The cogito is Descartes's paradigmatic a priori knowledge — self-evident without any empirical verification.

books.title: Critique of Pure Reason
books.author: Immanuel Kant
books.why: The foundational text — Kant's full argument for synthetic a priori knowledge.

books.title: Naming and Necessity
books.author: Saul Kripke
books.why: Kripke shows that necessity and a priority come apart — the discovery that transforms the framework.

books.title: The Empiricists
books.author: R.S. Woolhouse
books.why: Essential context — the empiricist challenge to a priori knowledge that Kant's Critique answered.

completion.name: Abstract Objects
completion.thinkerBio: Gottlob Frege (1848-1925) defended mathematical Platonism; W.V.O. Quine (1908-2000) developed the indispensability criterion for ontological commitment to abstracta.
completion.quote: We are committed to the existence of whatever our best theories must quantify over.

related.name: The Problem of Universals
related.note: Platonism about abstract objects is the mathematical special case of the broader universals debate.

related.name: Philosophy of Mathematics
related.note: Whether numbers exist as abstract objects is the central ontological question in philosophy of mathematics.

related.name: Occam's Razor
related.note: Nominalism about abstract objects is motivated by parsimony against Platonist multiplication of entities.

books.title: Word and Object
books.author: W.V.O. Quine
books.why: Quine's indispensability criterion for ontological commitment.

books.title: Science Without Numbers
books.author: Hartry Field
books.why: The most ambitious nominalist response, reformulating physics without numbers.

books.title: Introduction to Mathematical Philosophy
books.author: Bertrand Russell
books.why: Classic accessible treatment of mathematical ontology.

completion.name: Absurdism and Revolt
completion.thinkerBio: Albert Camus (1913-1960) developed absurdism in The Myth of Sisyphus (1942) and extended its political dimension in The Rebel (1951); his sustained public dispute with Sartre over the latter's communism stemmed partly from this political development.
completion.quote: There is no fate that cannot be surmounted by scorn -- one must imagine Sisyphus happy.

related.name: The Absurd
related.note: The Myth of Sisyphus establishes the absurd as the fundamental confrontation between human need for meaning and the universe's silence.

related.name: Nihilism
related.note: Camus's revolt is explicitly an alternative to both nihilistic resignation and the false consolations of religious or political absolutism.

related.name: Civil Disobedience
related.note: Camus's political revolt and civil disobedience traditions both ground resistance to injustice in individual moral conviction rather than historical inevitability.

related.name: Existence Precedes Essence
related.note: Both Camus and Sartre reject pre-given meaning, but Camus rejects Sartre's claim that radical freedom can fully resolve the absurd.

books.title: The Myth of Sisyphus
books.author: Albert Camus
books.why: The foundational text of absurdism -- Camus's argument that Sisyphus must be imagined happy.

books.title: The Rebel
books.author: Albert Camus
books.why: Camus's political extension of absurdism into a philosophy of revolt and resistance to totalitarianism.

books.title: Camus: A Critical Examination
books.author: David Sprintzen
books.why: A thorough philosophical examination of Camus's development from the absurd to revolt.

completion.name: Adaptive Preferences
completion.thinkerBio: Jon Elster (b. 1940) developed the concept of adaptive preferences, drawing on Aesop's fable of sour grapes; Martha Nussbaum and Amartya Sen incorporated the phenomenon into capabilities-based critiques of pure preference-satisfaction accounts of wellbeing.
completion.quote: People living under oppressive conditions often adapt their desires downward to match what is achievable, making actual preference satisfaction an unreliable measure of genuine wellbeing.

related.name: Desire-Satisfaction Theory
related.note: Adaptive preferences pose a serious challenge to using actual desire satisfaction as a measure of genuine wellbeing, since desires themselves can be products of oppression.

related.name: The Capabilities Approach
related.note: Sen and Nussbaum's capabilities approach was developed partly in response to the adaptive preferences problem, insisting some capabilities matter regardless of whether they are desired.

related.name: Feminist Political Philosophy
related.note: Adaptive preferences are central to feminist political philosophy's analysis of how women's apparent consent to subordination may reflect manufactured rather than autonomous preference.

related.name: Epistemic Injustice
related.note: Both adaptive preferences and hermeneutical injustice examine how oppressive conditions distort the very capacity to recognize and articulate one's own interests.

books.title: Sour Grapes
books.author: Jon Elster
books.why: Elster's foundational philosophical development of adaptive preferences and their implications for rational choice theory.

books.title: Women and Human Development
books.author: Martha Nussbaum
books.why: Nussbaum's application of the capabilities approach directly addressing the adaptive preferences problem for women's wellbeing.

books.title: Inequality Reexamined
books.author: Amartya Sen
books.why: Sen's broader philosophical treatment connecting adaptive preferences to debates about equality and capability.

completion.name: Advaita Vedanta
completion.thinkerBio: Adi Shankaracharya (c. 788–820 CE) was an Indian philosopher and theologian who systematized Advaita Vedanta in commentaries on the Upanishads, the Bhagavad Gita, and the Brahma Sutras; he revitalized Hinduism and established four monastic centers across India.
completion.quote: Brahman alone is real, the world is appearance, and the individual self is non-different from Brahman.

related.name: Brahman and Atman
related.note: Advaita Vedanta is the systematic philosophical development of the Upanishadic identity of Atman and Brahman.

related.name: Idealism
related.note: Both Advaita Vedanta and Western idealism hold that mind or consciousness is fundamental to reality — though Advaita's non-dual Brahman differs structurally.

related.name: Madhyamaka Philosophy
related.note: Both Advaita and Madhyamaka are non-dualist traditions that challenge conventional distinctions as ultimately unreal, by different arguments.

related.name: Panpsychism
related.note: Both make consciousness fundamental to all reality — Advaita identifies ultimate reality as pure consciousness; panpsychism distributes proto-consciousness through matter.

books.title: The Crest-Jewel of Discrimination
books.author: Shankaracharya (trans. Swami Prabhavananda)
books.why: Shankara's most accessible summary of Advaita Vedanta — the best introduction to his philosophy.

books.title: Advaita Vedanta: A Philosophical Reconstruction
books.author: Eliot Deutsch
books.why: Rigorous philosophical analysis of Advaita in dialogue with Western metaphysics.

books.title: A History of Indian Philosophy Vol. 1
books.author: Surendranath Dasgupta
books.why: The definitive scholarly history covering Advaita Vedanta in relation to other Indian philosophical schools.

completion.name: Aesthetic Disinterestedness
completion.thinkerBio: Immanuel Kant (1724–1804) formulated aesthetic disinterestedness in the Critique of Judgment (1790) as the defining feature of genuinely aesthetic pleasure; Arthur Schopenhauer (1788–1860) radicalized the concept in The World as Will and Representation (1818).
completion.quote: Taste is the faculty of estimating an object or a mode of representation by means of a delight or aversion apart from any interest.

related.name: The Sublime
related.note: The sublime also requires disinterested attention — the person threatened by the storm cannot experience its sublimity.

related.name: Formalism in Art
related.note: Aesthetic formalism inherits Kantian disinterestedness — pure formal appreciation requires suspension of all practical interests.

related.name: Aesthetic Experience
related.note: Schopenhauer's account of aesthetic experience as liberation from the will radicalizes disinterestedness.

related.name: Wu Wei
related.note: Taoist wu wei and Kantian disinterestedness both describe a mode of pure engagement freed from self-interested willing.

books.title: Critique of Judgment
books.author: Immanuel Kant
books.why: The primary source — Kant's analysis of disinterested pleasure as the foundation of aesthetic judgment.

books.title: The World as Will and Representation Vol. 1
books.author: Arthur Schopenhauer
books.why: Schopenhauer's radicalization of disinterestedness as liberation from the will.

books.title: Aesthetic Theory
books.author: Theodor Adorno
books.why: Adorno's critical development of aesthetic disinterestedness as social resistance.

completion.name: Aesthetic Experience and Flow
completion.thinkerBio: John Dewey (1859-1952) developed pragmatist aesthetics treating experience as continuous with ordinary life; Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi (1934-2021) developed the empirical psychological concept of flow as a complementary scientific account of peak engagement.
completion.quote: Peak experiences of total absorption, loss of self-consciousness, and effortless control occur across activities from rock climbing to musical performance to surgery.

related.name: The Aesthetic Experience
related.note: Csikszentmihalyi's flow provides an empirical psychological complement to Dewey's philosophical account of consummatory aesthetic engagement.

related.name: Wu Wei
related.note: Both flow states and Taoist wu wei describe a mode of skilled, effortless action in which self-conscious deliberation drops away.

related.name: Embodied Cognition
related.note: Flow states exemplify embodied cognition's claim that skilled action involves a tight, non-deliberative coupling between perception and bodily response.

related.name: Pragmatism
related.note: Dewey's aesthetics, which flow theory empirically complements, is the application of his broader pragmatist philosophy of experience to the specific domain of art.

books.title: Flow: The Psychology of Optimal Experience
books.author: Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi
books.why: Csikszentmihalyi's foundational empirical psychological account of flow states across diverse activities.

books.title: Art as Experience
books.author: John Dewey
books.why: Dewey's philosophical groundwork that flow theory provides an empirical complement to.

books.title: Finding Flow
books.author: Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi
books.why: A more accessible later development connecting flow theory to everyday wellbeing and meaning.

completion.name: Aesthetic Judgment
completion.thinkerBio: Immanuel Kant (1724-1804) developed the theory of aesthetic judgment in the Critique of Judgment; David Hume (1711-1776) developed the standard of taste.
completion.quote: A judgment of beauty claims universal validity -- 'this is beautiful' means more than 'I like this.'

related.name: Aesthetic Disinterestedness
related.note: Kant grounds aesthetic judgment's claim to universality in disinterested pleasure arising from free play of imagination.

related.name: Aesthetic Value
related.note: Aesthetic judgment is the act of attributing the aesthetic value that this entry examines the nature of.

related.name: Interpretation
related.note: Both aesthetic judgment and interpretation theory address how subjective response and objective claim interact in art appreciation.

books.title: Critique of Judgment
books.author: Immanuel Kant
books.why: The most rigorous philosophical treatment of aesthetic judgment's claim to universal validity.

books.title: Of the Standard of Taste
books.author: David Hume, in Essays Moral, Political, and Literary
books.why: Hume's foundational treatment of taste and the ideal critic.

books.title: Kant's Critique of the Power of Judgment
books.author: Paul Guyer (ed.)
books.why: Contemporary scholarly essays on Kant's aesthetic theory.

completion.name: Aesthetic Value
completion.thinkerBio: David Hume (1711-1776) developed the standard of taste; Frank Sibley (1923-1996) distinguished aesthetic from non-aesthetic concepts requiring perceptual sensitivity.
completion.quote: Aesthetic terms require taste for their application -- gracefulness emerges from non-aesthetic properties but cannot be derived from them by algorithm.

related.name: Aesthetic Judgment
related.note: Aesthetic value is the property that aesthetic judgment attributes to objects and works.

related.name: Interpretation
related.note: Both debates ask whether aesthetic response tracks objective properties or is purely projected.

related.name: Formalism in Art
related.note: Formalism locates aesthetic value specifically in formal arrangement independent of representational content.

books.title: Of the Standard of Taste
books.author: David Hume
books.why: Hume's foundational account of taste and the ideal critic.

books.title: Approach to Aesthetics
books.author: Frank Sibley
books.why: Sibley's foundational essays distinguishing aesthetic concepts.

books.title: Aesthetics and the Philosophy of Art
books.author: Noel Carroll
books.why: A comprehensive contemporary survey of aesthetic value theory.

completion.name: African Communalism
completion.thinkerBio: Kwame Gyekye (1939–2019) was a Ghanaian philosopher who developed moderate communitarianism in Tradition and Modernity (1997); Kwasi Wiredu (1931–2022) at the University of South Florida developed consensus democracy and conceptual decolonization throughout his career.
completion.quote: The good person is one who has a friendly, generous attitude toward others — who is constituted by and through community.

related.name: Ubuntu
related.note: Ubuntu is the most widely cited expression of African communalism — the philosophical grounding of community as constitutive of personhood.

related.name: Communitarianism
related.note: Western communitarianism and African communalism both challenge liberal individualism, though African communalism has a deeper ontological claim.

related.name: Liberalism
related.note: African communalism rejects liberal atomism — the idea of a self prior to and independent of its community.

related.name: Ubuntu as Political Philosophy
related.note: Ubuntu as political philosophy systematically develops African communalism into political theory, particularly consensus democracy.

books.title: Tradition and Modernity
books.author: Kwame Gyekye
books.why: Gyekye's account of moderate communalism — the alternative to both liberal individualism and strong collectivism.

books.title: Cultural Universals and Particulars
books.author: Kwasi Wiredu
books.why: Wiredu's cross-cultural philosophy including his development of African communalism and consensus democracy.

books.title: The Moral Foundation of Communitarian Africa
books.author: Thaddeus Metz
books.why: A rigorous philosophical defence of communitarian African ethics in dialogue with Western moral philosophy.

completion.name: African Environmental Philosophy
completion.thinkerBio: Kai Horsthemke (b. 1965) and Philip Higgs developed African eco-philosophy as a systematic alternative to Western environmental ethics, drawing on Ubuntu and indigenous African cosmologies of relational existence.
completion.quote: Nature is a participant in the community of existence rather than an inert background for human exploitation.

related.name: Environmental Ethics
related.note: African eco-philosophy and Western deep ecology both ground environmental obligations in community membership extended beyond humans, though through different cosmological frameworks.

related.name: Ubuntu
related.note: African environmental philosophy applies Ubuntu's relational ontology to human-nature relations, extending community obligations to non-human members of the ecosystem.

related.name: Taoism and Nature
related.note: Both African eco-philosophy and Taoist philosophy of nature critique the domination model of human-nature relations central to much Western environmental thought.

related.name: African Metaphysics
related.note: Vital force metaphysics, in which spiritual forces inhabit nature, provides the cosmological foundation for African environmental philosophy's relational approach.

books.title: Animals and African Ethics
books.author: Kai Horsthemke
books.why: Horsthemke's systematic development of African environmental and animal ethics.

books.title: African Philosophy of Education
books.author: Yusef Waghid
books.why: Connects African philosophical traditions to broader questions of ecological and ethical education.

books.title: Ubuntu: Curating the Archive
books.author: Leonhard Praeg and Siphokazi Magadla (eds.)
books.why: Essays exploring Ubuntu's applications across ethics, politics, and environmental relations.

completion.name: African Humanism
completion.thinkerBio: Leopold Sedar Senghor (1906-2001) developed the concept of civilisation de l'universel; Kwame Nkrumah (1909-1972) developed consciencism as a systematic philosophical synthesis for African decolonization.
completion.quote: African values of community, artistic expression, and participatory engagement with reality complement the European heritage of rationality in a genuine cultural synthesis.

related.name: Negritude
related.note: Senghor's African humanism develops Negritude's cultural assertions into a broader philosophical synthesis aimed at universal civilization.

related.name: Ubuntu
related.note: African humanism and Ubuntu both place human dignity, community, and relational flourishing at the center of African philosophical and political thought.

related.name: Pan-Africanism
related.note: Nkrumah's consciencism provided philosophical grounding for Pan-African political solidarity and the broader project of African unity.

related.name: Decolonizing Knowledge
related.note: Contemporary African humanism has moved beyond the nationalist phase toward more philosophically rigorous engagement with decolonizing knowledge projects.

books.title: On African Socialism
books.author: Leopold Sedar Senghor
books.why: Senghor's philosophical development of African humanism and its place within a universal civilization.

books.title: Consciencism
books.author: Kwame Nkrumah
books.why: Nkrumah's systematic philosophical synthesis aimed at grounding African decolonization and unity.

books.title: An African Ethics of Personhood
books.author: Thaddeus Metz
books.why: Metz's rigorous contemporary development connecting African humanism to systematic ethical theory.

completion.name: African Logic and Reasoning
completion.thinkerBio: Kwasi Wiredu (1931-2022) analyzed Akan epistemological vocabulary; J.O. Sodipo and Barry Hallen documented sophisticated Yoruba epistemological practice through interviews with traditional healers in Knowledge, Belief and Witchcraft (1986).
completion.quote: The Akan language embeds an epistemological distinction between modes of knowledge that English collapses into a single concept of belief.

related.name: Sage Philosophy
related.note: Both sage philosophy and African logic demonstrate that rigorous individual philosophical reasoning exists in African traditions outside formal academic philosophy.

related.name: Epistemic Injustice
related.note: Documenting sophisticated African epistemological vocabulary directly challenges colonial epistemic injustice's denial of philosophical sophistication to African knowers.

related.name: Language Games
related.note: Wiredu's analysis of how Akan linguistic structure embeds distinct epistemological categories parallels Wittgenstein's insight that meaning is use within particular practices.

related.name: Decolonizing Knowledge
related.note: Documenting indigenous African logical and epistemological frameworks is itself a central method of decolonizing knowledge.

books.title: Cultural Universals and Particulars
books.author: Kwasi Wiredu
books.why: Wiredu's analysis of Akan language and its implications for epistemology and conceptual decolonization.

books.title: Knowledge, Belief and Witchcraft
books.author: Barry Hallen and J.O. Sodipo
books.why: The foundational philosophical interviews documenting sophisticated Yoruba epistemological vocabulary and practice.

books.title: A Companion to African Philosophy
books.author: Kwasi Wiredu (ed.)
books.why: A comprehensive anthology covering African logic, epistemology, and reasoning across multiple traditions.

completion.name: African Metaphysics
completion.thinkerBio: Placide Tempels (1906-1977) introduced the concept of vital force as central to Bantu metaphysics in Bantu Philosophy (1945); John Mbiti (1931-2019) developed an influential account of African conceptions of time in African Religions and Philosophy (1969).
completion.quote: Everything that exists possesses vital force, and the fundamental relations between things are relations of the strengthening or weakening of that force.

related.name: Ubuntu
related.note: Vital force metaphysics and Ubuntu's relational personhood share the insight that beings are constituted through dynamic relations rather than as isolated substances.

related.name: Process Philosophy
related.note: Tempels's vital force metaphysics, treating relations and strengthening or weakening rather than static substance, has affinities with Whiteheadian process thought.

related.name: Time and Existence
related.note: Mbiti's account of African temporality as past-oriented contrasts sharply with Western linear, future-oriented conceptions of historical time central to existentialist temporality.

related.name: Sage Philosophy
related.note: Oruka's sage philosophy explicitly challenges the ethnophilosophical generalization that Tempels and Mbiti's accounts represent, insisting on individual critical reflection.

books.title: Bantu Philosophy
books.author: Placide Tempels
books.why: The foundational (if controversial) text introducing vital force metaphysics as a systematic African worldview.

books.title: African Religions and Philosophy
books.author: John Mbiti
books.why: Mbiti's influential and widely discussed account of African time, religion, and metaphysics.

books.title: African Philosophy: Myth and Reality
books.author: Paulin Hountondji
books.why: Hountondji's critical response challenging the generalizing assumptions of ethnophilosophical approaches like Tempels's.

completion.name: African Political Philosophy
completion.thinkerBio: Kwasi Wiredu (1931-2022) developed consensus democracy drawing on Ashanti political traditions; Emmanuel Eze (1950-2007) engaged critically with the racist legacy of canonical European philosophers including Hume, Kant, and Hegel.
completion.quote: Consensus democracy aims not at majority victory but at genuine agreement through dialogue that actively engages minority concerns.

related.name: Democracy
related.note: Wiredu's consensus democracy offers a genuine alternative to the adversarial, majoritarian model of democracy inherited from European colonial governance.

related.name: African Communalism
related.note: Wiredu's political philosophy systematically develops African communalist principles into a concrete model of governance and decision-making.

related.name: Race and Political Philosophy
related.note: Eze's critique of canonical European philosophers' racism connects to Mills's racial contract theory and the broader project of examining race in political philosophy.

related.name: Decolonizing Knowledge
related.note: Eze's engagement with the racist legacy of European philosophy is itself part of the broader project of decolonizing the philosophical canon.

books.title: Cultural Universals and Particulars
books.author: Kwasi Wiredu
books.why: Wiredu's foundational development of consensus democracy and conceptual decolonization.

books.title: Race and the Enlightenment
books.author: Emmanuel Eze (ed.)
books.why: Eze's essential anthology documenting the racist views embedded in canonical Enlightenment philosophy.

books.title: Postcolonial African Philosophy
books.author: Emmanuel Eze (ed.)
books.why: A comprehensive anthology of contemporary African political philosophy across multiple traditions and approaches.

completion.name: Agent-Relative vs Agent-Neutral
completion.thinkerBio: Thomas Nagel (b. 1937) developed the systematic distinction between agent-relative and agent-neutral reasons in The View from Nowhere (1986); Samuel Scheffler (b. 1951) developed the agent-centered prerogative as a hybrid accommodation within an otherwise consequentialist framework.
completion.quote: Agent-relative reasons essentially refer to the agent whose reasons they are; agent-neutral reasons are reasons for anyone regardless of their particular relation to the situation.

related.name: Consequentialism and Its Limits
related.note: Scheffler's agent-centered prerogative is the most influential attempt to accommodate Williams's integrity-based critique within an otherwise consequentialist framework.

related.name: Utilitarianism
related.note: Pure utilitarianism is agent-neutral, requiring impartial maximization; agent-relative reasons preserve the legitimacy of partiality toward one's own projects and relationships.

related.name: Categorical Imperative
related.note: Kant's categorical imperative is agent-neutral in its universalizability requirement, though it generates agent-relative duties through the specific application of universal principles.

related.name: Care Ethics
related.note: Care ethics' insistence on the moral legitimacy of partiality toward particular relationships is a strong, non-consequentialist defence of agent-relative reasons.

books.title: The View from Nowhere
books.author: Thomas Nagel
books.why: Nagel's foundational systematic development of the agent-relative versus agent-neutral distinction within a broader philosophy of objectivity.

books.title: The Rejection of Consequentialism
books.author: Samuel Scheffler
books.why: Scheffler's foundational development of the agent-centered prerogative as a hybrid response to the agent-relative challenge.

books.title: Equality and Partiality
books.author: Thomas Nagel
books.why: Nagel's later attempt to reconcile impartial equality with the legitimacy of agent-relative personal partiality.

completion.name: Alienation
completion.thinkerBio: G.W.F. Hegel (1770–1831) introduced alienation as a stage in Spirit's self-development; Karl Marx (1818–1883) transformed it into a critique of capitalism in the Economic and Philosophic Manuscripts (1844) and implicitly throughout Capital.
completion.quote: The worker becomes poorer the more wealth he produces.

related.name: Dialectics
related.note: Alienation is produced by the dialectical contradictions of capitalist production — private appropriation of socially produced value.

related.name: Alienation and Reification
related.note: Lukács extended Marx's alienation through reification — how social relations take on the appearance of relations between things under capitalism.

related.name: The Spectacle
related.note: Debord's spectacle is the fully developed alienation of modern capitalism — authentic life replaced by its representation.

related.name: Ubuntu
related.note: Ubuntu's relational personhood constituted through community is the philosophical antithesis of the alienated individual of capitalist society.

books.title: Economic and Philosophic Manuscripts of 1844
books.author: Karl Marx
books.why: The source — Marx's account of the four forms of alienation in capitalist production.

books.title: The Phenomenology of Spirit
books.author: G.W.F. Hegel
books.why: Hegel's account of alienation as a necessary stage in Spirit's self-development — the idealist original.

books.title: Alienation
books.author: Bertell Ollman
books.why: The most systematic scholarly analysis of Marx's concept of alienation and its place in his mature theory.

completion.name: Alienation and Reification
completion.thinkerBio: Georg Lukacs (1885-1971) extended Marx's alienation through reification in History and Class Consciousness (1923); Axel Honneth (b. 1949) has recently reconstructed reification theory through a phenomenology of prior recognition.
completion.quote: Under capitalism, social relations between persons take on the appearance of relations between things.

related.name: Alienation
related.note: Reification extends Marx's alienation concept -- it describes how the worker's estrangement makes social relations appear as relations between mere things.

related.name: Critical Theory
related.note: Reification became central to Frankfurt School critical theory's analysis of instrumental reason's colonization of social and personal life.

related.name: The Spectacle
related.note: Debord's spectacle is a fully developed extension of reification theory -- authentic life replaced entirely by its mediated representation.

related.name: Power-Knowledge
related.note: Both reification and Foucauldian power-knowledge examine how modern systems transform persons into objects of management and instrumental calculation.

books.title: History and Class Consciousness
books.author: Georg Lukacs
books.why: Lukacs's foundational development of reification from Marx's alienation, one of the most influential Marxist philosophical texts.

books.title: Reification: A New Look at an Old Idea
books.author: Axel Honneth
books.why: Honneth's contemporary reconstruction grounding reification in a phenomenology of prior recognition.

books.title: The Sane Society
books.author: Erich Fromm
books.why: Fromm's psychoanalytic and humanistic application of alienation and reification to consumer society.

completion.name: Analytic / Synthetic Distinction
completion.thinkerBio: Immanuel Kant (1724-1804) introduced the distinction; W.V.O. Quine (1908-2000) attacked it as untenable in 'Two Dogmas of Empiricism' (1951).
completion.quote: There is no sharp boundary between truths that hold come what may and truths revisable in light of experience.

related.name: A Priori / A Posteriori
related.note: Kant's a priori/a posteriori distinction intersects with the analytic/synthetic distinction.

related.name: Underdetermination
related.note: Quine's attack on the distinction is the companion thesis to his holism and underdetermination.

related.name: Naturalized Epistemology
related.note: Quine's dissolution of the distinction motivates his broader naturalistic epistemology.

books.title: Critique of Pure Reason
books.author: Immanuel Kant
books.why: Kant's original formulation of the distinction.

books.title: From a Logical Point of View
books.author: W.V.O. Quine
books.why: Contains 'Two Dogmas,' Quine's decisive attack on the distinction.

books.title: Word and Object
books.author: W.V.O. Quine
books.why: Quine's broader holistic philosophy developed from this critique.

completion.name: Anarchism
completion.thinkerBio: Pierre-Joseph Proudhon (1809-1865) coined the term in What Is Property? (1840); Mikhail Bakunin (1814-1876) and Peter Kropotkin (1842-1921) developed collectivist and communist anarchism respectively, grounding political order in voluntary cooperation rather than state coercion.
completion.quote: Property is theft -- and the state, which protects property, is the instrument of that theft.

related.name: Social Contract
related.note: Anarchism denies that any social contract could legitimately authorize a coercive state -- voluntary agreement grounds only voluntary cooperation.

related.name: Civil Disobedience
related.note: Anarchism and civil disobedience share the rejection of unjust positive law, though they differ on whether reform or abolition of the state is the ultimate goal.

related.name: Hegemony
related.note: Anarchism's vision of voluntary cooperation rejects Gramsci's analysis of how hegemonic culture naturalizes the necessity of state and class authority.

related.name: Power and Domination
related.note: Anarchist political theory provides the most radical critique of domination, extending the analysis to state authority itself, not merely particular unjust laws.

books.title: What Is Property?
books.author: Pierre-Joseph Proudhon
books.why: The foundational anarchist text -- Proudhon's argument that property is theft and the critique of coercive authority.

books.title: Mutual Aid: A Factor of Evolution
books.author: Peter Kropotkin
books.why: Kropotkin's natural-historical argument for cooperation rather than competition as the primary evolutionary and social mechanism.

books.title: Statism and Anarchy
books.author: Mikhail Bakunin
books.why: Bakunin's collectivist anarchism and his famous dispute with Marx over the role of the state in revolutionary transition.

completion.name: Anatta
completion.thinkerBio: The doctrine of no-self (anatta/anatman) was first taught by the historical Buddha (c. 480–400 BCE) in the Anattalakkhana Sutta; it was developed philosophically by the Abhidharma schools and most rigorously by Nagarjuna in the Madhyamaka tradition.
completion.quote: When searching for the self, you find only processes — there is no separate, permanent self to be found.

related.name: Dependent Origination
related.note: Anatta is explained by dependent origination — there is no self because everything arises dependently, including what we call 'I.'

related.name: Personal Identity
related.note: Parfit's no-self conclusion in personal identity arrives at structurally similar results to anatta through analytic philosophical argument.

related.name: Dukkha
related.note: The illusion of a permanent self is the root cause of dukkha — clinging to what cannot be grasped because no fixed self exists.

related.name: Madhyamaka Philosophy
related.note: Nagarjuna radicalized anatta into a metaphysics of universal emptiness — not only the self but all phenomena are empty of intrinsic existence.

books.title: The Heart of the Buddha's Teaching
books.author: Thich Nhat Hanh
books.why: An accessible account of anatta in the context of Buddhist practice and the three marks of existence.

books.title: The Selfless Mind
books.author: Peter Harvey
books.why: A scholarly philosophical analysis of anatta and its implications for consciousness and liberation.

books.title: Buddhism Without Beliefs
books.author: Stephen Batchelor
books.why: A secular philosophical engagement with anatta freed from metaphysical commitments about rebirth.

completion.name: Angst
completion.thinkerBio: Søren Kierkegaard (1813–1855) analyzed anxiety as the dizziness of freedom in The Concept of Anxiety (1844); Martin Heidegger (1889–1976) transformed it into an ontological concept in Being and Time (1927) — both texts are foundational for the existentialist tradition.
completion.quote: Anxiety is the dizziness of freedom.

related.name: Existence Precedes Essence
related.note: Existential angst is the felt experience of radical freedom — the vertiginous awareness that nothing outside you determines what you will be.

related.name: Bad Faith
related.note: Bad faith is the typical flight from angst — the comforting fiction of fixed nature that protects us from confronting radical freedom.

related.name: Being-toward-death
related.note: Heidegger's anxiety before being-toward-death individualizes Dasein and makes authentic existence possible.

related.name: Despair
related.note: Kierkegaard's despair is the existential illness that angst threatens to produce when freedom is denied rather than acknowledged.

books.title: The Concept of Anxiety
books.author: Søren Kierkegaard
books.why: Kierkegaard's foundational analysis of anxiety as the dizziness of freedom — the source of the existentialist concept.

books.title: Being and Time
books.author: Martin Heidegger
books.why: Heidegger's ontological analysis of anxiety as the mood that reveals Dasein's fundamental groundlessness.

books.title: The Courage to Be
books.author: Paul Tillich
books.why: Tillich's theological-existentialist response to anxiety — courage as the affirmation of being in the face of non-being.

completion.name: Animal Consciousness
completion.thinkerBio: Donald Griffin (1915-2003) founded cognitive ethology, documenting sophisticated animal cognition; the Cambridge Declaration on Consciousness (2012), signed by leading neuroscientists, formally recognized non-human consciousness as scientifically established.
completion.quote: The neurological substrates of consciousness are not unique to humans -- non-human animals also possess these neurological substrates.

related.name: Qualia
related.note: Nagel's question of what it is like to be a bat directly raises the issue of animal consciousness and its irreducibly subjective character.

related.name: Theory of Mind
related.note: Whether non-human animals have theory of mind is closely connected to debates about animal consciousness and cognitive sophistication.

related.name: Environmental Ethics
related.note: Animal consciousness has direct ethical implications -- if animals have genuine experience, their welfare must figure in moral calculations.

related.name: Buddhist Ethics
related.note: Buddhist ethics already extends moral concern to all sentient beings, anticipating the contemporary scientific recognition of animal consciousness.

books.title: Animal Minds
books.author: Donald Griffin
books.why: Griffin's foundational work establishing cognitive ethology and the scientific study of animal minds.

books.title: Are We Smart Enough to Know How Smart Animals Are?
books.author: Frans de Waal
books.why: De Waal's accessible survey of contemporary research on animal cognition and consciousness.

books.title: Animal Liberation
books.author: Peter Singer
books.why: Singer's foundational ethical argument that animal sentience grounds direct moral obligations.

completion.name: Animal Language
completion.thinkerBio: Noam Chomsky (b. 1928) argued human language is uniquely characterized by discrete infinity and recursion; the Kanzi bonobo experiments conducted by Sue Savage-Rumbaugh challenged sharp species boundaries by demonstrating sophisticated novel sentence comprehension.
completion.quote: Human language is uniquely characterized by the capacity to generate infinitely many sentences from finite means through recursive embedding.

related.name: Animal Consciousness
related.note: Both animal language research and animal consciousness studies investigate the cognitive sophistication of non-human animals and the boundaries of distinctively human capacities.

related.name: Theory of Mind
related.note: Whether animals capable of sophisticated communication also possess theory of mind is closely connected to debates about the nature and uniqueness of animal language abilities.

related.name: Concepts and Categorization
related.note: Animal language research bears on whether non-human animals possess genuine conceptual categorization or merely associative pattern recognition.

related.name: Multiple Realizability
related.note: If different species achieve communicative function through different cognitive architectures, this connects to multiple realizability's broader claim about functional kinds across substrates.

books.title: Kanzi: The Ape at the Brink of the Human Mind
books.author: Sue Savage-Rumbaugh and Roger Lewin
books.why: The primary account of the Kanzi experiments challenging sharp boundaries between human and animal linguistic capacity.

books.title: The Language Instinct
books.author: Steven Pinker
books.why: Pinker's accessible defence of the Chomskyan view that human language is a unique biological adaptation distinct from animal communication.

books.title: Syntactic Structures
books.author: Noam Chomsky
books.why: Chomsky's foundational linguistic text establishing the formal study of recursive grammatical structure central to his account of human language.

completion.name: Architecture as Art
completion.thinkerBio: Roger Scruton (1944-2020) developed the aesthetics of architecture as the humanization of space in his 1979 book of the same name.
completion.quote: Architecture is the humanization of space -- the transformation of raw spatial extension into a place fit for human dwelling.

related.name: Environmental Aesthetics
related.note: Both architectural aesthetics and environmental aesthetics investigate how spatial and environmental design shapes aesthetic experience.

related.name: Formalism in Art
related.note: Functionalist architectural aesthetics, which grounds beauty in fitness to purpose, contrasts with pure formalist appreciation.

related.name: The Aesthetic Experience
related.note: Scruton's spatial imagination connects to Dewey's broader account of aesthetic experience as engaged, embodied activity.

books.title: The Aesthetics of Architecture
books.author: Roger Scruton
books.why: Scruton's foundational philosophy of architecture as humanized space.

books.title: Towards a New Architecture
books.author: Le Corbusier
books.why: The classic functionalist architectural manifesto contrasting with Scruton's approach.

books.title: Experiencing Architecture
books.author: Steen Eiler Rasmussen
books.why: A classic accessible treatment of architectural aesthetic experience.

completion.name: Art and Morality
completion.thinkerBio: Plato (428–348 BCE) made the debate unavoidable by expelling poets from the Republic; Aristotle responded with the Poetics; the contemporary debate is shaped by Berys Gaut's ethicism, Noël Carroll's moderate moralism, and Daniel Jacobson's autonomism.
completion.quote: We should not allow the soul to become angry and sorrowful — the arts must be censored to protect virtue.

related.name: Mimesis
related.note: The debate is partly about mimesis — whether representing evil inflames or educates the audience.

related.name: Katharsis
related.note: Katharsis is Aristotle's response to Plato's moral concern — tragedy does not corrupt emotion but purifies or clarifies it.

related.name: Aesthetic Disinterestedness
related.note: Aesthetic autonomism derives from Kantian disinterestedness — if aesthetic judgment is independent of all interest, moral assessment cannot be aesthetically relevant.

related.name: Narrative and Fiction
related.note: Whether fiction can teach moral truths is the specific form that art-morality debates take for literary aesthetics.

books.title: The Republic
books.author: Plato (trans. G.M.A. Grube)
books.why: Plato's foundational moral critique of art — the case that mimesis inflames passion and must be censored.

books.title: Art and Moral Evaluation
books.author: Berys Gaut
books.why: The most sustained defence of ethicism — moral flaws in artworks are aesthetic flaws.

books.title: A Philosophy of Mass Art
books.author: Noël Carroll
books.why: Carroll's moderate moralism — a nuanced account of when moral evaluation is relevant to aesthetic judgment.

completion.name: Art as Expression
completion.thinkerBio: Benedetto Croce (1866–1952) identified art with intuitive expression in Aesthetic (1902); R.G. Collingwood (1889–1943) refined this in The Principles of Art (1938), arguing that genuine art is the process of clarifying emotional experience rather than producing a finished artifact.
completion.quote: Art is the expression of emotion, and expression is a process of clarification — not the discharge of feeling but its transformation into something understood.

related.name: Aesthetic Experience
related.note: Expression theory locates art's value in the experience of expression and reception — the artist clarifying emotion, the audience re-experiencing this clarification.

related.name: Mimesis
related.note: Mimesis locates art's value in representing the world; expression theory locates it in externalizing inner experience.

related.name: The Philosophy of Music
related.note: Expression theory is most naturally applied to music — the paradigm art of emotional expression with apparently no representational content.

related.name: Imagination
related.note: Expression requires imagination — both the artist's imaginative clarification and the audience's imaginative reception of the work.

books.title: The Principles of Art
books.author: R.G. Collingwood
books.why: The most philosophically developed expression theory — Collingwood's account of art as the clarification of emotional experience.

books.title: Aesthetic
books.author: Benedetto Croce
books.why: Croce's founding text of expression theory — the identification of art with intuitive-expressive activity.

books.title: Art and Its Objects
books.author: Richard Wollheim
books.why: Wollheim's philosophical analysis of art including a critique and refinement of expression theory.

completion.name: Artificial Intelligence and Mind
completion.thinkerBio: Alan Turing (1912-1954) proposed the imitation game as an operational test for machine intelligence in 'Computing Machinery and Intelligence' (1950); John Searle's Chinese Room argument (1980) remains the most influential philosophical challenge to claims of genuine machine understanding.
completion.quote: Can machines think? The question is, I believe, too meaningless to deserve discussion -- but we can ask whether machines can pass the imitation game.

related.name: Chinese Room
related.note: The Chinese Room remains the central philosophical challenge to claims that computational systems genuinely understand rather than merely simulate understanding.

related.name: Functionalism
related.note: Functionalism is the philosophical foundation for strong AI -- if minds are functional organizations, they can in principle be implemented in silicon.

related.name: The Turing Test
related.note: Turing's behavioral test for machine intelligence remains the most famous, if contested, operational criterion in the field.

related.name: Hard Problem of Consciousness
related.note: Whether AI systems could be genuinely conscious, not merely behaviorally sophisticated, connects directly to the hard problem of consciousness.

books.title: The Essential Turing
books.author: Alan Turing (ed. B. Jack Copeland)
books.why: Contains Turing's foundational paper proposing the imitation game as a test for machine intelligence.

books.title: Minds, Brains and Science
books.author: John Searle
books.why: Searle's accessible development of the Chinese Room argument and its implications for strong AI.

books.title: Superintelligence
books.author: Nick Bostrom
books.why: Bostrom's analysis of the philosophical and existential stakes of advanced artificial intelligence.

completion.name: Attention and Consciousness
completion.thinkerBio: Anne Treisman (1935-2018) developed feature integration theory connecting attention to perceptual binding; Stanislas Dehaene's Global Workspace Theory makes attention the key mechanism determining what becomes conscious.
completion.quote: Attention is the mechanism that determines what gets broadcast to the global workspace and thereby becomes available to consciousness.

related.name: Consciousness and the Brain
related.note: Global Workspace Theory closely connects attention to consciousness -- attention determines what gets broadcast to the global workspace.

related.name: The Binding Problem
related.note: Treisman's feature integration theory proposes that attention is the mechanism that binds separately processed features into unified perceptual objects.

related.name: Hard Problem of Consciousness
related.note: Even a complete account of attention's role in consciousness leaves the hard problem of why attended information is accompanied by subjective experience.

related.name: Functionalism
related.note: Attention-based theories of consciousness are broadly functionalist, defining consciousness by attention's causal and informational role.

books.title: Consciousness and the Brain
books.author: Stanislas Dehaene
books.why: Dehaene's rigorous account of how attention determines global workspace access and conscious awareness.

books.title: The Attentive Brain
books.author: Raja Parasuraman (ed.)
books.why: A comprehensive scientific survey of attention research including Treisman's feature integration theory.

books.title: Supersizing the Mind
books.author: Andy Clark
books.why: Clark connects attention and consciousness to broader questions about embodied and extended cognition.

completion.name: Authenticity
completion.thinkerBio: Martin Heidegger (1889–1976) developed authenticity (Eigentlichkeit) as an existential structural concept in Being and Time (1927); Jean-Paul Sartre (1905–1980) gave it more concrete ethical content through the contrast with bad faith and the demands of radical freedom.
completion.quote: Authenticity means taking over one's own existence as genuinely one's own — no longer living at the pace of what one does.

related.name: Bad Faith
related.note: Bad faith is the systematic denial of authenticity — the flight from radical freedom into the comfortable anonymity of fixed nature or social role.

related.name: Inauthenticity and the They-Self
related.note: The they-self and inauthenticity are the default conditions from which authentic existence must be won through confrontation with one's finitude.

related.name: Existence Precedes Essence
related.note: Authenticity is what existence-preceding-essence demands — genuinely taking up one's own authorship of oneself.

related.name: Resoluteness
related.note: Resoluteness is Heidegger's specific existential structure of authentic existence — owning thrownness from the standpoint of being-toward-death.

books.title: Being and Time
books.author: Martin Heidegger
books.why: The source — Division Two develops authenticity, resoluteness, and being-toward-death as the structure of genuine existence.

books.title: Existentialism Is a Humanism
books.author: Jean-Paul Sartre
books.why: Sartre's account of authenticity as the affirmation of radical freedom and responsibility.

books.title: The Ethics of Authenticity
books.author: Charles Taylor
books.why: Taylor's critique of trivial authenticity cultures and defence of the genuine moral ideal.

completion.name: Autonomy and Paternalism
completion.thinkerBio: John Stuart Mill (1806-1873) articulated the harm principle; Joel Feinberg (1926-2004) developed the sophisticated analysis of paternalism's limits.
completion.quote: The only purpose for which power can be rightfully exercised over any member of a civilised community, against his will, is to prevent harm to others.

related.name: Categorical Imperative
related.note: The humanity formula grounds the wrongness of hard paternalism.

related.name: Liberalism
related.note: The harm principle is the classical liberal argument limiting state power.

related.name: Bioethics
related.note: Informed consent and patient autonomy apply anti-paternalist principles to healthcare.

books.title: On Liberty
books.author: John Stuart Mill
books.why: The foundational anti-paternalist liberal argument.

books.title: Harm to Self
books.author: Joel Feinberg
books.why: Feinberg's comprehensive analysis of soft vs hard paternalism.

books.title: Nudge
books.author: Thaler and Sunstein
books.why: The libertarian paternalism argument, raising new philosophical questions about consent.

completion.name: Bad Faith
completion.thinkerBio: Jean-Paul Sartre (1905–1980) developed bad faith (mauvaise foi) as the central concept of existentialist moral psychology in Being and Nothingness (1943) — the self-deception by which consciousness denies its own radical freedom and responsibility.
completion.quote: Man is always more than what he is said to be — bad faith is the attempt to fix oneself as a thing.

related.name: Existence Precedes Essence
related.note: Bad faith is the flight from the radical freedom that existence-precedes-essence entails.

related.name: Authenticity
related.note: Authenticity is the opposite of bad faith — the lucid acknowledgment of radical freedom rather than its denial.

related.name: Facticity
related.note: Bad faith often consists in treating facticity as if it were the whole of one's being — denying transcendence by identifying with one's past and roles.

related.name: The Gaze
related.note: The Other's gaze connects to bad faith — we often adopt the Other's objectifying view of us as our fixed nature.

books.title: Being and Nothingness
books.author: Jean-Paul Sartre
books.why: The source — the waiter example and Sartre's full analysis of bad faith in Being and Nothingness.

books.title: Anti-Semite and Jew
books.author: Jean-Paul Sartre
books.why: Sartre's analysis of bad faith in antisemitism and certain responses to it — existentialism applied to social identity.

books.title: The Words
books.author: Jean-Paul Sartre
books.why: Sartre's autobiography — self-examination of his own bad faith as a writer and public intellectual.

completion.name: Bayesian Epistemology
completion.thinkerBio: Bruno de Finetti (1906-1985) and Frank Ramsey (1903-1930) grounded subjective probability in coherence conditions on degrees of belief.
completion.quote: Rational credence should be updated by conditionalizing on new evidence according to Bayes's theorem.

related.name: Calibration and Epistemic Rationality
related.note: Accuracy-first epistemology provides a foundational justification for Bayesian norms.

related.name: Inference to Best Explanation
related.note: Bayesian confirmation theory provides a formal framework quantifying IBE's intuitive judgments.

related.name: Belief Revision
related.note: Bayesian conditionalization and AGM belief revision both address rational belief updating, with different scopes.

books.title: Theory of Probability
books.author: Bruno de Finetti
books.why: De Finetti's foundational subjectivist account of probability.

books.title: Foundations
books.author: Frank Ramsey
books.why: Ramsey's foundational essays grounding subjective probability in coherent betting behavior.

books.title: Accuracy and the Laws of Credence
books.author: Richard Pettigrew
books.why: A contemporary accuracy-based justification for Bayesian norms.

completion.name: Being-toward-death
completion.thinkerBio: Martin Heidegger (1889–1976) was a German philosopher whose Being and Time (1927) is one of the most influential and difficult texts in twentieth-century philosophy; his analysis of being-toward-death transformed how philosophy thinks about finitude and authentic existence.
completion.quote: Death is Dasein's ownmost possibility — non-relational, certain, and indefinite as to its When.

related.name: Authenticity
related.note: Authentic existence requires confronting being-toward-death — running ahead to one's finitude dissolves the comfortable anonymity of the they-self.

related.name: Thrownness
related.note: Thrownness and being-toward-death together constitute the structure of finite Dasein — thrown into existence toward death.

related.name: Death and Dying
related.note: Heidegger's being-toward-death is the philosophical analysis that makes Epicurus's consolation seem inadequate — death matters because it is my ownmost possibility.

related.name: Angst
related.note: Angst individualizes Dasein by confronting it with its groundlessness — and being-toward-death is the ultimate ground of this groundlessness.

books.title: Being and Time
books.author: Martin Heidegger
books.why: The source — Division Two contains the full analysis of being-toward-death and authentic temporality.

books.title: Heidegger: A Very Short Introduction
books.author: Michael Inwood
books.why: The most accessible entry point to Being and Time including the analysis of death.

books.title: Staring at the Sun
books.author: Irvin Yalom
books.why: Yalom's existential therapeutic application of being-toward-death — how confronting mortality can liberate rather than paralyze.

completion.name: Belief Revision
completion.thinkerBio: Carlos Alchourron, Peter Gardenfors, and David Makinson developed AGM belief revision theory in the 1980s.
completion.quote: When new information conflicts with existing beliefs, rational revision requires retracting just enough to maintain consistency.

related.name: Coherentism
related.note: AGM theory formalizes coherentist intuitions about how a web of beliefs should be updated.

related.name: Bayesian Epistemology
related.note: Both AGM and Bayesian updating address rational belief change, though AGM handles a broader range of revision scenarios.

related.name: Underdetermination
related.note: Both examine how theory or belief choice responds to new, potentially conflicting evidence.

books.title: Knowledge in Flux
books.author: Peter Gardenfors
books.why: Gardenfors's foundational systematic development of AGM belief revision theory.

books.title: On the Logic of Theory Change
books.author: Alchourron, Gardenfors, Makinson, in Journal of Symbolic Logic
books.why: The original foundational paper establishing AGM theory.

books.title: Belief Revision
books.author: Peter Gardenfors (ed.)
books.why: An essential anthology extending AGM theory across applications.

completion.name: Bioethics
completion.thinkerBio: Tom Beauchamp (b. 1939) and James Childress (b. 1940) developed the four-principle framework in Principles of Biomedical Ethics (1979).
completion.quote: The goal of medicine is not merely to cure disease but to serve the whole person -- body, mind, and relationships.

related.name: Autonomy and Paternalism
related.note: Patient autonomy is the first bioethical principle and grounds informed consent.

related.name: Utilitarianism
related.note: Utilitarian reasoning grounds resource allocation debates like QALYs.

related.name: Rights
related.note: Rights-based bioethics grounds autonomy in fundamental rights against bodily invasion.

books.title: Principles of Biomedical Ethics
books.author: Beauchamp and Childress
books.why: The foundational four-principle framework.

books.title: Practical Ethics
books.author: Peter Singer
books.why: A systematic utilitarian treatment of major bioethical issues.

books.title: A Short History of Medical Ethics
books.author: Albert Jonsen
books.why: Historical context for the development of modern bioethics.

completion.name: Biopolitics and Bare Life
completion.thinkerBio: Giorgio Agamben (b. 1942) developed bare life and the state of exception in Homo Sacer (1995), extending Foucault's biopower through Carl Schmitt's theory of sovereignty and Hannah Arendt's analysis of statelessness.
completion.quote: The camp is the paradigm of modern sovereignty -- a space where the distinction between living and dying becomes a matter of sovereign decision.

related.name: Biopower
related.note: Agamben extends Foucault's biopower through bare life -- the form of life stripped of all political qualification that sovereignty produces.

related.name: Sovereignty
related.note: Agamben draws directly on Schmitt's theory of sovereignty as the decision on the exception to analyze how modern states produce bare life.

related.name: Panopticon
related.note: Agamben's bare life represents what is left when disciplinary control is applied at its absolute limit -- the stripped, controlled life of the camp.

related.name: Race and Political Philosophy
related.note: Mills's racial contract and Agamben's bare life both analyze how political orders systematically exclude certain populations from full political and moral standing.

books.title: Homo Sacer
books.author: Giorgio Agamben
books.why: Agamben's foundational and most widely discussed development of bare life and sovereign power.

books.title: State of Exception
books.author: Giorgio Agamben
books.why: Agamben's extension of the analysis to contemporary emergency powers and detention practices.

books.title: The Origins of Totalitarianism
books.author: Hannah Arendt
books.why: Arendt's analysis of statelessness and the loss of political standing that directly informs Agamben's concept of bare life.

completion.name: Biopower
completion.thinkerBio: Michel Foucault (1926–1984) was a French philosopher and historian of ideas whose Discipline and Punish (1975) and The History of Sexuality Vol. 1 (1976) developed the concept of biopower; his Collège de France lectures elaborated its biopolitical dimensions.
completion.quote: The old power of death now gives way to the administration of bodies and the calculated management of life.

related.name: Power-Knowledge
related.note: Biopower is one of the most important forms of Foucauldian power-knowledge — it produces subjects and populations through medical, administrative, and statistical knowledge.

related.name: Biopolitics and Bare Life
related.note: Agamben extends Foucault's biopower through bare life — sovereignty operates by deciding which lives can be exposed to death.

related.name: Hegemony
related.note: Both Gramsci's hegemony and Foucault's biopower explain how modern power operates through internalization and consent rather than direct coercion.

related.name: Sovereignty
related.note: Foucault explicitly contrasted biopower with sovereign power — sovereignty 'lets live or makes die'; biopower 'makes live and lets die.'

books.title: Discipline and Punish
books.author: Michel Foucault
books.why: Foucault's analysis of disciplinary power — the panopticon and the production of docile bodies.

books.title: The History of Sexuality, Vol. 1
books.author: Michel Foucault
books.why: The explicit introduction of biopower — how the administration of sexuality became a site of political power.

books.title: Biopolitics: An Advanced Introduction
books.author: Thomas Lemke
books.why: The clearest contemporary overview of biopower and biopolitics across Foucault, Agamben, and contemporary theory.

completion.name: Brahman and Atman
completion.thinkerBio: The doctrine of Brahman and Atman is developed across the Upanishads (c. 800-200 BCE) and given its most systematic philosophical articulation by Adi Shankaracharya (c. 788-820 CE) in his Advaita Vedanta commentaries.
completion.quote: Tat tvam asi -- That thou art.

related.name: Advaita Vedanta
related.note: Advaita Vedanta is the systematic philosophical development of the Upanishadic identity of Atman and Brahman into a complete metaphysical system.

related.name: Moksha
related.note: The direct realization of the identity of Atman and Brahman constitutes moksha -- liberation from the cycle of rebirth.

related.name: Panpsychism
related.note: Both the Brahman-Atman doctrine and panpsychism make consciousness fundamental to all reality, though through very different metaphysical frameworks.

related.name: Idealism
related.note: The non-dual identity of Atman and Brahman is structurally similar to certain forms of Western idealism that identify ultimate reality with mind or consciousness.

books.title: The Upanishads
books.author: trans. Eknath Easwaran
books.why: An accessible and philosophically attentive translation of the primary source texts on Brahman and Atman.

books.title: The Crest-Jewel of Discrimination
books.author: Shankaracharya (trans. Swami Prabhavananda)
books.why: Shankara's systematic philosophical treatment of the identity between Atman and Brahman.

books.title: Indian Philosophy: A Very Short Introduction
books.author: Sue Hamilton
books.why: A clear introduction situating the Brahman-Atman doctrine within the broader landscape of Indian philosophy.

completion.name: Buddhism in China
completion.thinkerBio: Huineng (638-713 CE) is the legendary sixth patriarch of Chan Buddhism whose Platform Sutra established sudden enlightenment as the central teaching of Southern Chan; the Tiantai school, developed under Zhiyi (538-597 CE), synthesized all Buddhist teachings into a comprehensive doctrinal system.
completion.quote: The mind is fundamentally pure -- there is nothing to be gradually purified, only the illusion that one lacks what one already has.

related.name: Zen Koans
related.note: The Chan tradition founded partly through Huineng's teaching of sudden enlightenment is the direct ancestor of the Japanese Zen koan tradition.

related.name: Madhyamaka Philosophy
related.note: The Tiantai school's three truths doctrine represents a sophisticated Chinese development of Nagarjuna's original two-truths framework.

related.name: Anatta
related.note: Huineng's teaching that the mind is fundamentally pure builds on but transforms the earlier Buddhist doctrine of no-self and Buddha-nature.

related.name: Wang Yangming's Mind-Only Philosophy
related.note: Chan Buddhism's emphasis on innate Buddha-nature and Wang Yangming's innate moral knowledge both locate ultimate truth as already present within the mind, requiring only its uncovering.

books.title: The Platform Sutra of the Sixth Patriarch
books.author: Huineng (trans. Philip Yampolsky)
books.why: The foundational text of Southern Chan Buddhism and the doctrine of sudden enlightenment.

books.title: The Essentials of the Eight Traditions
books.author: Gyonen (trans. Leo Pruden)
books.why: A classical overview of Chinese Buddhist schools including Tiantai's systematic doctrinal synthesis.

books.title: Zen Buddhism: A History
books.author: Heinrich Dumoulin
books.why: A comprehensive scholarly history tracing the development of Chan/Zen from its Chinese origins through Japan.

completion.name: Buddhist Epistemology
completion.thinkerBio: Dignaga (fifth century CE) and Dharmakirti (seventh century CE) developed the most rigorous Buddhist epistemological system, engaging in extended debate with Nyaya logicians.
completion.quote: Valid knowledge is restricted to perception and inference -- genuine perception grasps only the bare particular, free from conceptual construction.

related.name: Nyaya Logic
related.note: Dignaga and Dharmakirti engaged in extended technical debate with Nyaya logicians over perception and inference.

related.name: Buddhist Meditation and Epistemology
related.note: Dharmakirti's account of yogic direct perception is the meditative extension of the broader Buddhist epistemological system.

related.name: Anatta
related.note: Buddhist epistemology's account of bare particulars connects to the broader doctrine of no-self and the deconstruction of conceptual overlay.

books.title: Dharmakirti's Philosophy and Its Tibetan Interpretations
books.author: John Dunne
books.why: The definitive scholarly study of Buddhist epistemology's most sophisticated system.

books.title: Dignaga on the Perception of Reality
books.author: Masaaki Hattori
books.why: A scholarly translation and study of Dignaga's foundational epistemological text.

books.title: Buddhist Philosophy as a Way of Life
books.author: Harris, Heim, Tzohar (eds.)
books.why: Contemporary essays connecting Buddhist epistemology to its practice dimensions.

completion.name: Buddhist Ethics
completion.thinkerBio: Buddhist ethics is grounded in the Pali Canon's teachings attributed to the historical Buddha; the Mahayana bodhisattva ideal, developed by figures including Shantideva (eighth century CE), extends ethical concern to the liberation of all sentient beings.
completion.quote: Hatred is never appeased by hatred in this world. By non-hatred alone is hatred appeased.

related.name: Karma
related.note: Karma is the causal mechanism through which ethical action bears fruit -- understanding it is fundamental to Buddhist ethical motivation.

related.name: Dukkha
related.note: Buddhist ethics is organized around the goal of liberation from dukkha, cultivating wisdom and compassion as means to that liberation.

related.name: The Mahayana Bodhisattva Ideal
related.note: The bodhisattva ideal extends Buddhist ethical concern from personal liberation to universal compassion for all sentient beings.

related.name: Virtue Ethics
related.note: Both Buddhist ethics and Aristotelian virtue ethics center moral life on the cultivation of stable character dispositions rather than rule-following alone.

books.title: The Dhammapada
books.author: trans. Eknath Easwaran
books.why: The most accessible canonical source for the core ethical teachings of Buddhism.

books.title: A Guide to the Bodhisattva's Way of Life
books.author: Shantideva (trans. Vesna Wallace and Alan Wallace)
books.why: Shantideva's philosophically sophisticated development of universal compassion and the bodhisattva ideal.

books.title: Buddhist Ethics: A Very Short Introduction
books.author: Damien Keown
books.why: A clear, comprehensive introduction to the structure and content of Buddhist ethical thought.

completion.name: Buddhist Meditation and Epistemology
completion.thinkerBio: Dharmakirti (seventh century CE) developed the theory of yogic direct perception within the broader Buddhist epistemological tradition founded by Dignaga, holding that sustained meditation can produce a form of direct cognition unavailable to ordinary consciousness.
completion.quote: Through sustained meditation on impermanence, no-self, and emptiness, what was initially an inferential conclusion becomes directly experienced.

related.name: Zen Koans
related.note: Koan practice is the most radical form of Buddhist epistemology -- direct yogic perception bypassing all conceptual mediation, paralleling Dharmakirti's account.

related.name: Virtue Epistemology
related.note: Both Buddhist meditative epistemology and virtue epistemology hold that epistemic excellence requires cultivated capacities, not merely passive reception of evidence.

related.name: Anatta
related.note: Yogic direct perception is held to provide direct, non-inferential access to truths like no-self and impermanence that ordinary cognition can only approach through inference.

related.name: Embodied Cognition
related.note: Both meditative epistemology and embodied cognition challenge the idea that knowledge is purely a matter of abstract propositional representation.

books.title: Dharmakirti's Philosophy and Its Tibetan Interpretations
books.author: John Dunne
books.why: The definitive scholarly study of Dharmakirti's epistemology including yogic direct perception.

books.title: Buddhist Philosophy as a Way of Life
books.author: Stephen Harris, Maria Heim, and Roy Tzohar (eds.)
books.why: Essays connecting Buddhist epistemology to its transformative, practice-oriented dimensions.

books.title: Knowing Illusion
books.author: The Yakherds (trans.)
books.why: A rigorous translation and study of Tibetan debates on Dharmakirti's epistemology and yogic perception.

completion.name: Bundle Theory
completion.thinkerBio: David Hume (1711-1776) developed bundle theory of the self in A Treatise of Human Nature; contemporary bundle theorists of objects more generally include Russell and, in a qualified form, Rudolf Carnap.
completion.quote: I never can catch myself at any time without a perception, and never can observe anything but the perception.

related.name: Substance and Accident
related.note: Bundle theory denies that objects are substances bearing properties -- they are nothing but collections of co-located properties or perceptions.

related.name: Anatta
related.note: Hume's bundle theory of the self and Buddhist anatta both deny a persistent subject underlying the flow of experience.

related.name: Personal Identity
related.note: Hume's bundle theory denies any underlying self that could ground personal identity -- there is only a constantly changing bundle of perceptions.

related.name: The Problem of Universals
related.note: Bundle theory of objects treats things as bundles of universals or tropes co-located in space, without a substratum to bear them.

books.title: A Treatise of Human Nature
books.author: David Hume
books.why: Book I, Part IV contains Hume's bundle theory of the self -- one of philosophy's most radical claims about personal identity.

books.title: Metaphysics: A Contemporary Introduction
books.author: Michael Loux
books.why: Loux's textbook gives the clearest contemporary statement of bundle theory and its problems.

books.title: Hume's Philosophy of the Self
books.author: Terence Penelhum
books.why: A scholarly study focused specifically on Hume's bundle theory and its puzzles.

completion.name: Calibration and Epistemic Rationality
completion.thinkerBio: James Joyce (b. 1962) developed accuracy-first epistemology grounding probabilistic coherence in the goal of accuracy; Richard Pettigrew (b. 1980) developed a comprehensive epistemic decision theory deriving all epistemic norms from this single source.
completion.quote: A well-calibrated agent is one whose confidence in beliefs matches the actual rate at which such beliefs are true.

related.name: Bayesian Epistemology
related.note: Accuracy-first epistemology provides a foundational justification for Bayesian norms.

related.name: Epistemic Humility
related.note: Calibration is the formal expression of epistemic humility.

related.name: Reliabilism
related.note: Both ground epistemic norms in producing accurate or true beliefs.

related.name: Inference to Best Explanation
related.note: Both address how rational belief-formation should be evaluated.

books.title: Accuracy and the Laws of Credence
books.author: Richard Pettigrew
books.why: Pettigrew's comprehensive development of accuracy-first epistemology.

books.title: The Foundations of Causal Decision Theory
books.author: James Joyce
books.why: Joyce's foundational decision-theoretic framework.

books.title: Epistemic Utility Theory
books.author: Richard Pettigrew and Ben Levinstein (eds.)
books.why: Examines grounding epistemic norms in accuracy.

completion.name: Care Ethics
completion.thinkerBio: Carol Gilligan (b. 1936) challenged Kohlberg's male-biased moral development theory in In a Different Voice (1982); Nel Noddings (b. 1929) and Virginia Held (b. 1929) developed care ethics into a systematic philosophical framework over subsequent decades.
completion.quote: The ideal of care is an activity of relationship, of seeing and responding to need, taking care of the world by sustaining the web of connection.

related.name: Feminist Ethics of Care
related.note: Care ethics is developed into a full political philosophy by Held and Tronto, connecting it to the structural devaluation of care work.

related.name: Virtue Ethics
related.note: Both focus on character and relationship over rules; care ethics reads as virtue ethics with attentive caring as the paradigmatic virtue.

related.name: Utilitarianism
related.note: Utilitarianism demands strict impartiality; care ethics holds that partiality toward particular relationships is morally required, not a failing.

related.name: Ubuntu
related.note: Both ground ethics in constitutive relationships and community rather than in abstract individual rights or impartial obligations.

books.title: In a Different Voice
books.author: Carol Gilligan
books.why: Gilligan's foundational critique of male-biased moral development and introduction of the care orientation.

books.title: Caring: A Feminine Approach to Ethics
books.author: Nel Noddings
books.why: The first systematic philosophical development of care ethics as a complete moral theory.

books.title: The Ethics of Care
books.author: Virginia Held
books.why: Held's mature synthesis connecting care ethics to political philosophy, justice, and feminist theory.

completion.name: Carvaka Materialism
completion.thinkerBio: Brihaspati is the legendary founder of the Carvaka (Lokayata) school of materialist philosophy in ancient India, whose original texts are lost but are known through extensive quotation and refutation in opposing philosophical texts.
completion.quote: Only direct perception is a valid source of knowledge -- consciousness arises from the combination of material elements and ceases when they decompose at death.

related.name: Skepticism
related.note: Carvaka's restriction of valid knowledge to direct perception alone parallels Western empiricist skepticism about inference and testimony as reliable knowledge sources.

related.name: Functionalism
related.note: Carvaka's eliminativist view that consciousness simply ceases at the decomposition of material elements stands in contrast to functionalist accounts of mind as substrate-independent.

related.name: Hedonism
related.note: Carvaka's practical conclusion -- enjoy life since nothing follows death -- parallels certain hedonist conclusions reached through entirely different philosophical premises.

related.name: Karma
related.note: Carvaka explicitly rejected the doctrine of karma and rebirth as resting on unreliable inference, making it the most radical heterodox position in classical Indian philosophy.

books.title: A History of Indian Philosophy Vol. 1
books.author: Surendranath Dasgupta
books.why: Contains the most accessible scholarly treatment of Carvaka materialism within its historical context.

books.title: Materialism and Immaterialism in India and the West
books.author: Pradeep Gokhale
books.why: A comparative study placing Carvaka materialism in dialogue with Western materialist traditions.

books.title: Classical Indian Philosophy
books.author: Bina Gupta
books.why: A comprehensive scholarly survey including the heterodox materialist and skeptical traditions of classical India.

completion.name: Categorical Imperative
completion.thinkerBio: Immanuel Kant (1724–1804) was a German philosopher who spent his career at Königsberg; his Groundwork for the Metaphysics of Morals established deontological ethics as a systematic alternative to both consequentialism and divine command theory.
completion.quote: Act only according to that maxim whereby you can at the same time will that it should become a universal law.

related.name: Utilitarianism
related.note: Kant grounds morality in universalizable duty regardless of outcomes; utilitarianism grounds it entirely in aggregate consequences.

related.name: Contractualism
