completion.thinkerBio: The Buddha (c. 563–483 BCE) declared dukkha the first of the Four Noble Truths in his first sermon; the entire Buddhist philosophical and soteriological project flows from this diagnosis — understanding dukkha is the beginning of the path to liberation.
completion.quote: Birth is dukkha, aging is dukkha, sickness is dukkha, death is dukkha — the five aggregates of clinging are dukkha.

related.name: Anatta
related.note: Dukkha arises from the illusion of a permanent self — clinging to what cannot be grasped because no fixed self exists.

related.name: Dependent Origination
related.note: The twelve links of dependent origination describe precisely how dukkha perpetuates itself through craving, clinging, and becoming.

related.name: Nirvana
related.note: Nirvana is the complete cessation of dukkha — the third noble truth establishes that liberation from suffering is achievable.

related.name: Stoic Ethics
related.note: Both Stoicism and Buddhism identify attachment to externals as the source of suffering, though their prescriptions and metaphysics differ significantly.

books.title: In the Buddha's Words
books.author: Bhikkhu Bodhi (ed.)
books.why: An anthology of the Buddha's teachings organized thematically — includes the primary suttas on dukkha and the Four Noble Truths.

books.title: The Miracle of Mindfulness
books.author: Thich Nhat Hanh
books.why: A practical introduction to working with dukkha through mindfulness practice.

books.title: Why Buddhism is True
books.author: Robert Wright
books.why: A secular philosophical defence of Buddhist insights about dukkha using evolutionary psychology.

completion.name: Embodied Cognition
completion.thinkerBio: Maurice Merleau-Ponty (1908–1961) provided the phenomenological foundation in Phenomenology of Perception (1945); Francisco Varela (1946–2001), Evan Thompson, and Eleanor Rosch developed enactivist cognitive science in The Embodied Mind (1991).
completion.quote: The body is not an object the mind uses — it is the very medium through which we have a world.

related.name: Phenomenology of Perception
related.note: The phenomenological foundation — Merleau-Ponty's analysis of the body as the pre-reflective vehicle of all experience.

related.name: Functionalism
related.note: Functionalism abstracts from the biological substrate; embodied cognition insists the specific body partly constitutes the character of cognition.

related.name: Extended Mind
related.note: Embodied cognition and the extended mind thesis are parts of the 4E approach — cognition is embodied, embedded, enacted, and extended.

related.name: Dependent Origination
related.note: Enactivist embodied cognition and Buddhist dependent origination both hold that cognition is constituted through interdependent relations rather than internal representations.

books.title: Phenomenology of Perception
books.author: Maurice Merleau-Ponty
books.why: The philosophical foundation of embodied cognition — the body as the subject of perception.

books.title: The Embodied Mind
books.author: Varela, Thompson, and Rosch
books.why: The foundational scientific-philosophical text of enactivist cognitive science.

books.title: Being There
books.author: Andy Clark
books.why: Clark's accessible account of embodied, embedded cognition — minds extended into their environment.

completion.name: Embodied Freedom
completion.thinkerBio: Simone de Beauvoir (1908-1986) introduced embodied freedom in The Second Sex and The Ethics of Ambiguity; Iris Marion Young (1949-2006) developed its phenomenological implications in 'Throwing Like a Girl' (1980).
completion.quote: Women often hold their bodies back in space, experiencing them as encumbrances rather than vehicles for engagement with the world -- a result of social training, not biology.

related.name: Situation
related.note: Embodied freedom develops de Beauvoir's concept of situation specifically through the lens of bodily comportment and its social shaping.

related.name: Freedom and Responsibility
related.note: Embodied freedom complicates pure Sartrean freedom by showing how social practices shape bodily capacities for action from early childhood onward.

related.name: Embodiment and Feminist Philosophy
related.note: Young's phenomenological account of constrained female bodily comportment directly develops the broader feminist philosophy of embodiment.

related.name: Phenomenology of Perception
related.note: Young's analysis applies Merleau-Ponty's phenomenology of the lived body specifically to the gendered structuring of bodily comportment and movement.

books.title: The Second Sex
books.author: Simone de Beauvoir
books.why: De Beauvoir's foundational analysis of women's embodied condition under patriarchy.

books.title: Throwing Like a Girl
books.author: Iris Marion Young
books.why: Young's foundational phenomenological essay on constrained bodily comportment as a social rather than natural phenomenon.

books.title: The Ethics of Ambiguity
books.author: Simone de Beauvoir
books.why: De Beauvoir's broader philosophical framework of situated freedom that grounds her analysis of embodiment.

completion.name: Embodiment and Feminist Philosophy
completion.thinkerBio: Luce Irigaray (b. 1930) argued Western philosophy has been constituted by the suppression of sexual difference; Elizabeth Grosz (b. 1952) developed an account of bodies as the active material site of subjectivity in Volatile Bodies (1994).
completion.quote: The body is neither nature nor culture but the crossing point of both -- marked by gender, race, and sexuality in ways that constitute rather than merely express the subject.

related.name: The Second Sex
related.note: De Beauvoir's analysis of women's lived embodiment under patriarchy is the foundational text that Irigaray and Grosz further develop and complicate.

related.name: Phenomenology of Perception
related.note: Both feminist embodiment philosophy and Merleau-Ponty's phenomenology treat the body as the active site of subjectivity rather than a passive object.

related.name: Gender Performativity
related.note: Irigaray's sexual difference asserts a positive feminine specificity, in tension with Butler's view that all gender, including femininity, is purely performative construction.

related.name: Throwing Like a Girl
related.note: Young's phenomenological account of constrained female bodily comportment directly develops the feminist philosophy of embodiment that Irigaray and Grosz established.

books.title: This Sex Which Is Not One
books.author: Luce Irigaray
books.why: Irigaray's foundational and challenging text developing the philosophy of sexual difference.

books.title: Volatile Bodies
books.author: Elizabeth Grosz
books.why: Grosz's systematic account of the body as the active material site of feminist philosophical inquiry.

books.title: Throwing Like a Girl
books.author: Iris Marion Young
books.why: Young's phenomenological essays applying feminist embodiment theory to lived bodily experience under patriarchy.

completion.name: Emergence
completion.thinkerBio: John Stuart Mill anticipated emergence in his account of chemical combination; contemporary philosophers including Philip Clayton develop strong versus weak emergence.
completion.quote: Water is wet; neither hydrogen nor oxygen alone is wet.

related.name: Reductionism
related.note: Strong emergence directly denies that all higher-level facts are determined by physical-level facts.

related.name: Hard Problem of Consciousness
related.note: Chalmers argues consciousness is a case of strong emergence resisting physicalist explanation.

related.name: Complexity and Self-Organization
related.note: Self-organization is a primary mechanism through which emergent order arises in complex systems.

books.title: Mind and Emergence
books.author: Philip Clayton
books.why: Comprehensive treatment of weak versus strong emergence.

books.title: The Re-Emergence of Emergence
books.author: Clayton and Davies (eds.)
books.why: An anthology on emergence across physics, biology, and mind.

books.title: Consciousness and the Brain
books.author: Stanislas Dehaene
books.why: Relevant context for emergence debates about consciousness.

completion.name: Emergence and Supervenience
completion.thinkerBio: Jaegwon Kim (1934-2019) developed the exclusion problem challenging non-reductive physicalism about mental causation.
completion.quote: If mental events are not reducible to physical events, they cannot play any genuine causal role given the causal closure of the physical.

related.name: Emergence
related.note: This entry develops emergence specifically as it bears on the mind-body problem and mental causation.

related.name: Multiple Realizability
related.note: Kim's exclusion problem threatens the causal efficacy that multiple realizability was meant to preserve for mental kinds.

related.name: The Hard Problem Revisited
related.note: Strong emergence would need to solve Kim's exclusion problem to give consciousness genuine causal powers.

books.title: Mind in a Physical World
books.author: Jaegwon Kim
books.why: Kim's foundational statement of the exclusion problem.

books.title: Supervenience and Mind
books.author: Jaegwon Kim
books.why: Kim's collected essays developing supervenience across his career.

books.title: The Re-Emergence of Emergence
books.author: Clayton and Davies (eds.)
books.why: Broader context on emergence debates that bear on the exclusion problem.

completion.name: Emotions and Cognition
completion.thinkerBio: Richard Lazarus (1922-2002) developed cognitive appraisal theory in psychology; Martha Nussbaum (b. 1947) developed the most influential philosophical cognitive theory of emotions, treating them as evaluative judgments rather than mere bodily feelings.
completion.quote: Emotions are not just feelings but evaluative judgments about what matters to us -- grief is the judgment that something of great value has been lost.

related.name: Moral Motivation
related.note: Nussbaum's cognitive theory of emotion supports a Humean-adjacent view that emotional appraisal, not pure reason, is central to practical and moral motivation.

related.name: Katharsis
related.note: Nussbaum's cognitive theory grounds the clarification interpretation of katharsis -- tragedy educates emotional intelligence rather than merely discharging feeling.

related.name: Moral Psychology
related.note: Cognitive appraisal theory connects to the broader empirical study of how emotion and reasoning interact in producing moral judgment.

related.name: Care Ethics
related.note: Both cognitive emotion theory and care ethics treat emotional responsiveness as epistemically and morally significant rather than as mere irrational noise.

books.title: Upheavals of Thought
books.author: Martha Nussbaum
books.why: Nussbaum's comprehensive philosophical development of the cognitive theory of emotions.

books.title: Emotion and Adaptation
books.author: Richard Lazarus
books.why: Lazarus's foundational psychological theory of cognitive appraisal underlying emotional response.

books.title: The Fragility of Goodness
books.author: Martha Nussbaum
books.why: Nussbaum's earlier work connecting emotion, vulnerability, and ethical luck in Greek philosophy and tragedy.

completion.name: Emotions as Appraisals
completion.thinkerBio: Richard Lazarus (1922-2002) developed cognitive appraisal theory; Martha Nussbaum (b. 1947) developed the philosophical eudaimonistic judgment account of emotion.
completion.quote: Grief involves the judgment that something of great value has been lost -- emotions are evaluative judgments, not mere feelings.

related.name: Emotions and Cognition
related.note: This entry introduces the general appraisal framework the fuller emotions-and-cognition entry develops.

related.name: Katharsis
related.note: The cognitive theory of emotion grounds the clarification interpretation of katharsis as emotional education.

related.name: Moral Motivation
related.note: Cognitive appraisal theory supports treating emotional response as central to, not opposed to, moral reasoning.

books.title: Upheavals of Thought
books.author: Martha Nussbaum
books.why: Nussbaum's comprehensive philosophical cognitive theory of emotions.

books.title: Emotion and Adaptation
books.author: Richard Lazarus
books.why: Lazarus's foundational psychological appraisal theory.

books.title: The Fragility of Goodness
books.author: Martha Nussbaum
books.why: Nussbaum's earlier work connecting emotion to ethical vulnerability.

completion.name: Emptiness and the Two Truths
completion.thinkerBio: Nagarjuna (c. 150-250 CE) developed the two-truths doctrine; Tsongkhapa (1357-1419 CE) clarified the relationship between conventional and ultimate truth in Tibetan philosophy.
completion.quote: Emptiness is itself empty -- the ultimate truth does not have intrinsic existence any more than conventional entities do.

related.name: Madhyamaka Philosophy
related.note: The two truths doctrine is the central philosophical structure of Madhyamaka's account of emptiness.

related.name: Dependent Origination
related.note: Conventional reality exists precisely because of dependent origination, while lacking ultimate intrinsic existence.

related.name: Buddhism in China
related.note: The Tiantai school's three truths doctrine is a sophisticated Chinese development of Nagarjuna's original framework.

books.title: Nagarjuna's Middle Way
books.author: trans. Siderits and Katsura
books.why: The definitive translation and commentary on the two truths doctrine's source text.

books.title: Ocean of Reasoning
books.author: Tsongkhapa (trans. Garfield and Samten)
books.why: Tsongkhapa's rigorous clarification of the two truths relationship.

books.title: Buddhism as Philosophy
books.author: Mark Siderits
books.why: A clear analytic introduction to emptiness and the two truths.

completion.name: Environmental Aesthetics
completion.thinkerBio: Allen Carlson (b. 1943) developed the natural environmental model requiring scientific knowledge for appropriate appreciation of nature; Arnold Berleant (b. 1932) developed aesthetic engagement emphasizing multi-sensory immersion over detached observation.
completion.quote: Appropriate aesthetic appreciation of nature must be informed by scientific knowledge -- to appreciate a wolf correctly requires understanding it as an apex predator in a complex ecosystem.

related.name: The Sublime
related.note: The sublime is the central concept in natural aesthetic experience, and environmental aesthetics develops a more systematic theory of how we appreciate vast, overwhelming landscapes.

related.name: Environmental Ethics
related.note: If natural environments have genuine aesthetic value, this provides one dimension of the argument for their protection beyond purely instrumental or biocentric grounds.

related.name: Aesthetic Disinterestedness
related.note: Berleant's aesthetic engagement challenges Kantian disinterested contemplation in favor of total multi-sensory immersion in environments.

related.name: Philosophy of Biology
related.note: Carlson's cognitively-informed model requires that proper aesthetic appreciation of nature draw on accurate ecological and biological understanding.

books.title: Aesthetics and the Environment
books.author: Allen Carlson
books.why: Carlson's foundational development of the natural environmental model and cognitively-informed nature appreciation.

books.title: The Aesthetics of Environment
books.author: Arnold Berleant
books.why: Berleant's foundational development of aesthetic engagement as multi-sensory immersion rather than detached contemplation.

books.title: Environment and the Arts
books.author: Arnold Berleant (ed.)
books.why: A comprehensive anthology covering the major positions in contemporary environmental aesthetics.

completion.name: Environmental Ethics
completion.thinkerBio: Aldo Leopold (1887-1948) developed the land ethic; Arne Naess (1912-2009) introduced deep ecology in 1973.
completion.quote: A thing is right when it tends to preserve the integrity, stability, and beauty of the biotic community.

related.name: African Environmental Philosophy
related.note: Both ground obligations in community membership extended beyond humans.

related.name: Taoism and Nature
related.note: Both critique domination-based approaches to human-nature relations.

related.name: Jain Philosophy of Ahimsa
related.note: Ahimsa extended to all beings is the most radical non-Western parallel.

books.title: A Sand County Almanac
books.author: Aldo Leopold
books.why: The foundational land ethic text.

books.title: Ecology, Ethics and the Environment
books.author: Arne Naess
books.why: Naess's deep ecology foundation.

books.title: Animal Liberation
books.author: Peter Singer
books.why: Connects to the scope of moral consideration debate.

completion.name: Epistemic Akrasia
completion.thinkerBio: Jonathan Adler argued epistemic akrasia is impossible because belief is directly responsive to evidence; John Greco challenged this, arguing that motivated reasoning and self-deception make epistemic akrasia both possible and common.
completion.quote: Someone is epistemically akratic when they believe P while simultaneously believing they have better evidence for not-P.

related.name: Free Will and Moral Responsibility
related.note: Practical and epistemic akrasia both raise the puzzle of acting or believing against one's own best judgment.

related.name: Epistemic Humility
related.note: Epistemic akrasia represents a failure of epistemic self-governance that humility should help prevent.

related.name: Wang Yangming's Mind-Only Philosophy
related.note: Wang Yangming's unity of knowing and acting offers a structurally similar resolution to akrasia.

related.name: Moral Motivation
related.note: Both investigate the gap between judgment and motivation, in believing or acting.

books.title: Belief's Own Ethics
books.author: Jonathan Adler
books.why: Adler's foundational argument that epistemic akrasia is impossible.

books.title: Achieving Knowledge
books.author: John Greco
books.why: Greco's virtue epistemology including his challenge to Adler.

books.title: Self-Deception and Autonomy
books.author: Mike Martin (ed.)
books.why: An anthology on self-deception's relationship to epistemic akrasia.

completion.name: Epistemic Autonomy
completion.thinkerBio: Immanuel Kant (1724-1804) articulated the Enlightenment ideal of epistemic autonomy in 'What Is Enlightenment?' (1784); Miranda Fricker (b. 1966) showed how social power structures can undermine epistemic autonomy through epistemic injustice.
completion.quote: Sapere aude -- dare to know. Enlightenment consists in emerging from self-imposed intellectual immaturity.

related.name: Epistemic Injustice
related.note: Epistemic injustice shows how social power undermines epistemic autonomy by denying marginalized people conceptual resources.

related.name: Testimony
related.note: Full epistemic autonomy is in tension with the necessity of epistemic dependence on testimony for most beliefs.

related.name: Categorical Imperative
related.note: Kant's epistemic autonomy parallels his moral autonomy -- both require self-legislation through reason.

related.name: Decolonizing Knowledge
related.note: Restoring epistemic autonomy to colonized peoples is a central goal of decolonizing knowledge.

books.title: An Answer to the Question: What Is Enlightenment?
books.author: Immanuel Kant
books.why: Kant's foundational essay on epistemic autonomy.

books.title: Epistemic Injustice
books.author: Miranda Fricker
books.why: Fricker's account of how social power undermines epistemic autonomy.

books.title: Autonomy and the Self
books.author: Michael Kuhler and Nadja Jelinek (eds.)
books.why: Examines autonomy across ethical and epistemic dimensions.

completion.name: Epistemic Humility
completion.thinkerBio: Socrates (c. 470-399 BCE) embodied epistemic humility through the examined life; John Stuart Mill (1806-1873) grounded it politically in On Liberty.
completion.quote: I know that I know nothing.

related.name: Skepticism
related.note: Both begin from recognition of the limits of knowledge, though humility leads to calibrated confidence rather than suspension.

related.name: Calibration and Epistemic Rationality
related.note: Calibration is the formal expression of epistemic humility.

related.name: The Gettier Problem
related.note: Gettier cases show even well-justified beliefs can fail to be knowledge, supporting humility.

books.title: Apology
books.author: Plato
books.why: The primary source for Socratic epistemic humility.

books.title: On Liberty
books.author: John Stuart Mill
books.why: Mill's political grounding of epistemic humility in fallibilism.

books.title: Intellectual Virtues
books.author: Jason Baehr
books.why: Contemporary treatment connecting humility to democratic discourse.

completion.name: Epistemic Injustice
completion.thinkerBio: Miranda Fricker (b. 1966) is a British philosopher at CUNY Graduate Center whose Epistemic Injustice: Power and the Ethics of Knowing (2007) created a new subfield at the intersection of epistemology, feminist philosophy, and political philosophy.
completion.quote: Epistemic injustice is a wrong done to someone specifically in their capacity as a knower.

related.name: Testimony
related.note: Testimonial injustice is the primary form of epistemic injustice — systematic deflation of a speaker's credibility based on their social identity.

related.name: Standpoint Epistemology
related.note: Both show how social position shapes epistemic access and credibility in ways that track power structures.

related.name: Decolonizing Knowledge
related.note: Colonial epistemic injustice is the paradigm case of hermeneutical injustice — colonized peoples lacking conceptual resources to articulate their own situation.

related.name: Social Epistemology
related.note: Epistemic injustice extends social epistemology to questions about the fair distribution of epistemic credibility and conceptual resources.

books.title: Epistemic Injustice
books.author: Miranda Fricker
books.why: The foundational text — introduces testimonial and hermeneutical injustice grounded in a theory of epistemic virtue.

books.title: The Routledge Handbook of Epistemic Injustice
books.author: Kidd, Medina, Pohlhaus (eds.)
books.why: The comprehensive academic overview of epistemic injustice research.

books.title: Epistemic Cowardice
books.author: Leigh Johnson
books.why: Extension of epistemic injustice to the vice of avoiding difficult truths — a practically grounded application.

completion.name: Error Theory
completion.thinkerBio: J.L. Mackie (1917-1981) presented the definitive error theory argument in Ethics: Inventing Right and Wrong (1977).
completion.quote: Moral values are not part of the fabric of the world.

related.name: Moral Realism
related.note: Error theory denies any moral claims are true while realism holds many are.

related.name: Expressivism
related.note: Both deny objective moral truth though via different semantic routes.

related.name: Nihilism
related.note: Both identify the absence of objective values as the fundamental situation.

books.title: Ethics: Inventing Right and Wrong
books.author: J.L. Mackie
books.why: The foundational error theory text with the queerness and relativity arguments.

books.title: Morality
books.author: Richard Joyce
books.why: The most systematic contemporary defence extending Mackie's framework.

books.title: The Moral Problem
books.author: Michael Smith
books.why: A cognitivist response clarifying error theory's stakes.

completion.name: Essentialism
completion.thinkerBio: Aristotle (384–322 BCE) developed essentialism in the Metaphysics and biological works; Saul Kripke (b. 1940) revived it through possible worlds semantics in Naming and Necessity (1972/1980), demonstrating that essential truths are necessary but discovered empirically.
completion.quote: Water is necessarily H₂O — this was not known a priori, but once discovered, it is necessarily true.

related.name: Possible Worlds
related.note: Kripke's essentialism is grounded in possible worlds — essential properties are those a thing has in every possible world in which it exists.

related.name: Reference and Rigid Designation
related.note: Rigid designation and essentialism are two sides of the same view — names rigidly designate their bearers' essential properties.

related.name: A Priori / A Posteriori
related.note: Kripke shows that essential truths are necessary but a posteriori — dissolving Kant's equation of the necessary with the a priori.

related.name: Philosophy of Biology
related.note: Biological essentialism — that species have essential properties — is the target of much philosophy of biology; most philosophers of biology now reject it.

books.title: Naming and Necessity
books.author: Saul Kripke
books.why: The foundational text for contemporary essentialism — rigid designation and necessary a posteriori truths.

books.title: Essence and Existence
books.author: Kathrin Koslicki
books.why: Rigorous contemporary treatment of essence and what it is for a property to be essential.

books.title: Metaphysics: The Fundamentals
books.author: Robert Koons and Timothy Pickavance
books.why: Clear introduction covering essentialism, possible worlds, and natural kinds.

completion.name: Eternal Recurrence
completion.thinkerBio: Friedrich Nietzsche (1844–1900) was a German philosopher and philologist who published prolifically from 1872 until his mental collapse in 1889; eternal recurrence appears first in The Gay Science (1882) and is developed most fully in Thus Spoke Zarathustra.
completion.quote: What, if some day or night a demon were to steal after you into your loneliest loneliness and say to you: 'This life as you now live it, you will have to live once more and innumerable times more'?

related.name: Nihilism
related.note: Eternal recurrence is Nietzsche's test for whether one can affirm life without cosmic backing — the proposed answer to nihilism.

related.name: The Eternal Return as Ethics
related.note: The metaphysical thought experiment becomes an ethical criterion — can you will that you have lived as you have lived, forever?

related.name: Authenticity
related.note: Only a life lived authentically — from one's own values without self-deception — can pass the test of eternal recurrence.

related.name: Time and Existence
related.note: Both eternal recurrence and Heidegger's being-toward-death use the structure of time to reveal what matters in how one lives.

books.title: The Gay Science
books.author: Friedrich Nietzsche
books.why: Eternal recurrence's first appearance — section 341, 'The Greatest Weight,' is the most important passage.

books.title: Thus Spoke Zarathustra
books.author: Friedrich Nietzsche
books.why: The fullest development of eternal recurrence as the test of amor fati and the will to power.

books.title: Nietzsche on Truth and Philosophy
books.author: Maudemarie Clark
books.why: Clark's rigorous analysis of eternal recurrence as an ethical doctrine rather than a cosmological hypothesis.

completion.name: Eudaimonia
completion.thinkerBio: Aristotle (384–322 BCE) developed eudaimonia in the Nicomachean Ethics as the ultimate human good; Martha Nussbaum (b. 1947) at the University of Chicago produced the most influential contemporary development through the capabilities approach.
completion.quote: Happiness is the meaning and the purpose of life, the whole aim and end of human existence.

related.name: Virtue Ethics
related.note: Virtue ethics aims at eudaimonia — virtuous character exercised over a complete life just is what flourishing consists in.

related.name: Utilitarianism
related.note: Eudaimonia is an objective condition requiring activity and external goods; utilitarian happiness is a subjective mental state of pleasure or preference satisfaction.

related.name: Dharma
related.note: Both ground human flourishing in acting in accordance with one's nature and proper function within a larger order.

related.name: Buddhist Ethics
related.note: The Eightfold Path and eudaimonia both describe a path to genuine flourishing through right character and action, though Buddhism denies the persistent self Aristotle's account requires.

books.title: Nicomachean Ethics
books.author: Aristotle
books.why: The foundational text — Aristotle's full account of eudaimonia, virtue, friendship, and the contemplative life.

books.title: Creating Capabilities
books.author: Martha Nussbaum
books.why: Nussbaum's accessible statement of her capabilities approach — eudaimonia operationalized for development policy and global justice.

books.title: The Fragility of Goodness
books.author: Martha Nussbaum
books.why: Nussbaum's philosophical account of luck, vulnerability, and the conditions for genuine flourishing in Greek tragedy and philosophy.

completion.name: Existence Precedes Essence
completion.thinkerBio: Jean-Paul Sartre (1905–1980) was a French philosopher, novelist, and playwright who became the leading figure of existentialism; Being and Nothingness (1943) developed the full philosophical account of radical freedom that the slogan 'existence precedes essence' encapsulates.
completion.quote: Man is condemned to be free.

related.name: Bad Faith
related.note: Bad faith is the flight from radical freedom — the denial of existence-preceding-essence through self-deception about having a fixed nature.

related.name: Authenticity
related.note: Authenticity is the positive ideal that existence-precedes-essence demands — genuinely taking up one's radical freedom.

related.name: Nihilism
related.note: Both begin from the absence of pre-given human essence or cosmic meaning, but Sartre sees this as liberation.

related.name: Thrownness
related.note: Heidegger's thrownness limits Sartrean radical freedom — we exist within a situation we did not choose that partly constitutes what we are.

books.title: Existentialism Is a Humanism
books.author: Jean-Paul Sartre
books.why: Sartre's most accessible statement of existence-precedes-essence and its ethical implications.

books.title: Being and Nothingness
books.author: Jean-Paul Sartre
books.why: The full ontological account — consciousness as nothingness, radical freedom, bad faith, and the Other.

books.title: The Roads to Freedom
books.author: Jean-Paul Sartre
books.why: Sartre's trilogy — the lived experience of radical freedom in occupied France.

completion.name: Existence and Nothingness
completion.thinkerBio: Jean-Paul Sartre (1905-1980) developed the foundational ontological distinction between being-in-itself and being-for-itself in Being and Nothingness (1943), structuring his entire philosophical system around consciousness as nothingness.
completion.quote: Consciousness is not a thing but a nothingness that perpetually distances itself from what it is -- this nihilation is the source of human freedom.

related.name: Existence Precedes Essence
related.note: Sartre's ontology of consciousness as nothingness directly generates the slogan that existence precedes essence -- there is no fixed nature because consciousness is not a thing.

related.name: Bad Faith
related.note: Bad faith is the attempt to deny one's own nothingness by pretending to be a fixed thing -- a being-in-itself rather than being-for-itself.

related.name: Facticity
related.note: Facticity and nothingness are the two poles of human reality for Sartre -- the given, fixed dimension and the negating freedom that transcends it.

related.name: Idealism
related.note: Sartre's being-in-itself, dense and self-identical matter independent of consciousness, resists idealist reduction of all reality to mind-dependent content.

books.title: Being and Nothingness
books.author: Jean-Paul Sartre
books.why: The primary source -- Sartre's complete ontological system distinguishing being-in-itself from being-for-itself.

books.title: Sartre: A Philosophical Biography
books.author: Thomas Flynn
books.why: Traces the development of Sartre's ontology through his entire philosophical career.

books.title: The Cambridge Companion to Sartre
books.author: Christina Howells (ed.)
books.why: Comprehensive scholarly essays on Sartre's philosophy including the ontology of Being and Nothingness.

completion.name: Existential Communication
completion.thinkerBio: Soren Kierkegaard (1813-1855) developed indirect communication through pseudonymous authorship across his major works, holding that existential truths cannot be conveyed directly without being falsified by the act of telling.
completion.quote: Direct communication conveys objective information; indirect communication creates conditions for the reader's own existential self-discovery.

related.name: Authenticity
related.note: Kierkegaard's indirect communication aims to awaken the reader to authentic existence, which requires their own leap rather than receiving truth as information.

related.name: Zen Koans
related.note: Both indirect communication and the koan tradition hold that existential truths cannot be conveyed directly but must be catalyzed in the student's own experience.

related.name: Despair
related.note: Kierkegaard's pseudonymous works on despair are themselves exercises in indirect communication, compelling readers toward self-recognition rather than offering direct diagnosis.

related.name: The Leap of Faith
related.note: The leap of faith cannot be communicated directly; Kierkegaard's literary strategy of indirect communication is designed to provoke rather than describe this movement.

books.title: Concluding Unscientific Postscript
books.author: Soren Kierkegaard
books.why: Kierkegaard's most extended theoretical reflection on indirect communication and pseudonymous authorship.

books.title: Fear and Trembling
books.author: Soren Kierkegaard
books.why: A primary example of indirect communication in practice, using the Abraham story to provoke rather than argue.

books.title: Kierkegaard's Writings
books.author: Howard V. Hong and Edna H. Hong (trans.)
books.why: The definitive scholarly translation series providing access to the full range of Kierkegaard's pseudonymous strategy.

completion.name: Existential Phenomenology
completion.thinkerBio: Maurice Merleau-Ponty (1908-1961) and Jean-Paul Sartre (1905-1980) jointly founded existential phenomenology by applying Husserl's phenomenological method to questions of embodied existence, freedom, and concrete situatedness.
completion.quote: Existence precedes essence, but existence is always embodied, situated, and lived from the inside.

related.name: Phenomenology of Perception
related.note: Merleau-Ponty's Phenomenology of Perception is the foundational text applying phenomenological method to embodied existence.

related.name: Existence Precedes Essence
related.note: Sartre's existentialist slogan is itself a product of applying phenomenological method to the structures of human freedom.

related.name: Embodied Cognition
related.note: Contemporary embodied cognition science draws directly on Merleau-Ponty's existential phenomenological account of the lived body.

related.name: Intersubjectivity
related.note: Existential phenomenology grounds intersubjectivity in embodied encounter rather than abstract theoretical inference about other minds.

books.title: Phenomenology of Perception
books.author: Maurice Merleau-Ponty
books.why: The foundational text of existential phenomenology -- the body as the lived, situated subject of perception and action.

books.title: Being and Nothingness
books.author: Jean-Paul Sartre
books.why: Sartre's existential phenomenology of freedom, the Other, and concrete situated consciousness.

books.title: The Phenomenology Reader
books.author: Dermot Moran and Tim Mooney (eds.)
books.why: Comprehensive anthology covering Husserl, Heidegger, Sartre, and Merleau-Ponty in the development of phenomenology.

completion.name: Existentialist Feminism
completion.thinkerBio: Simone de Beauvoir (1908-1986) applied Sartrean existentialism systematically to women's condition; contemporary scholars including Sonia Kruks and Sara Heinamaa have developed and extended de Beauvoir's existentialist feminist framework.
completion.quote: Woman is made, not born -- the existentialist insight that existence precedes essence reveals that femininity is a socially constructed project, not a biological destiny.

related.name: The Second Sex
related.note: This entry develops the broader existentialist feminist framework that The Second Sex's specific arguments are grounded in.

related.name: Existence Precedes Essence
related.note: De Beauvoir's feminism is the most consequential application of Sartre's existentialist slogan to a specific social and political domain.

related.name: Situation
related.note: De Beauvoir's concept of situation, central to existentialist feminism, refines Sartrean freedom by attending to material and social constraint.

related.name: Embodied Freedom
related.note: Existentialist feminism's central contribution is showing how freedom is always embodied and situated rather than abstract and unconditioned.

books.title: The Second Sex
books.author: Simone de Beauvoir
books.why: The foundational text of existentialist feminism.

books.title: Simone de Beauvoir and the Politics of Ambiguity
books.author: Sonia Kruks
books.why: Kruks's contemporary development of de Beauvoir's existentialist feminist political philosophy.

books.title: Toward a Phenomenology of Sexual Difference
books.author: Sara Heinamaa
books.why: Heinamaa's rigorous phenomenological extension of existentialist feminist themes through Husserl and Merleau-Ponty.

completion.name: Expressivism
completion.thinkerBio: A.J. Ayer (1910-1989) introduced emotivism; Simon Blackburn (b. 1944) and Allan Gibbard (b. 1942) developed sophisticated quasi-realist and norm-expressivist successors.
completion.quote: To call something good is to express a pro-attitude toward it and invite others to share it.

related.name: Non-Cognitivism
related.note: Expressivism is the sophisticated contemporary development of non-cognitivism.

related.name: Error Theory
related.note: Both anti-realist, differing on whether moral claims are truth-apt.

related.name: Moral Realism
related.note: Expressivism denies moral claims state genuinely true or false propositions.

books.title: Spreading the Word
books.author: Simon Blackburn
books.why: Blackburn's quasi-realist development of expressivism.

books.title: Wise Choices, Apt Feelings
books.author: Allan Gibbard
books.why: Gibbard's technically rigorous norm-expressivism.

books.title: Language, Truth and Logic
books.author: A.J. Ayer
books.why: The original emotivist statement.

completion.name: Extended Mind
completion.thinkerBio: Andy Clark (b. 1957) and David Chalmers (b. 1966) developed the extended mind thesis in their 1998 paper of the same name.
completion.quote: When a non-biological resource plays the functional role we'd attribute to a cognitive process inside the skull, it counts as part of the cognitive system.

related.name: Embodied Cognition
related.note: Embodied cognition and the extended mind are parts of the broader 4E approach to cognition.

related.name: Functionalism
related.note: The extended mind relies on the functionalist parity principle -- function determines cognitive status regardless of substrate location.

related.name: Artificial Intelligence and Mind
related.note: The extended mind thesis has direct implications for how we understand human-AI cognitive coupling.

books.title: Supersizing the Mind
books.author: Andy Clark
books.why: Clark's comprehensive development of the extended mind thesis.

books.title: The Extended Mind
books.author: Richard Menary (ed.)
books.why: The essential anthology including the original Clark and Chalmers paper.

books.title: Being There
books.author: Andy Clark
books.why: Clark's earlier accessible groundwork for the extended mind thesis.

completion.name: Facticity
completion.thinkerBio: Jean-Paul Sartre (1905-1980) developed facticity as the given dimension of existence standing in tension with transcendence, in Being and Nothingness.
completion.quote: Facticity is the given, fixed dimension of existence -- our body, our past, our situation -- that we did not choose.

related.name: Existence Precedes Essence
related.note: Facticity is the given pole standing in permanent tension with the transcendence that grounds radical freedom.

related.name: Bad Faith
related.note: Bad faith often consists in treating facticity as if it were the whole of one's being.

related.name: Thrownness
related.note: Sartre's facticity and Heidegger's thrownness both name the given, unchosen dimension of existence.

books.title: Being and Nothingness
books.author: Jean-Paul Sartre
books.why: The primary source developing facticity alongside transcendence.

books.title: Existentialism Is a Humanism
books.author: Jean-Paul Sartre
books.why: Sartre's more accessible statement of the facticity-transcendence structure.

books.title: Sartre: A Philosophical Biography
books.author: Thomas Flynn
books.why: Traces the development of facticity through Sartre's career.

completion.name: Falsifiability
completion.thinkerBio: Karl Popper (1902–1994) was an Austrian-British philosopher of science who developed falsifiability in The Logic of Scientific Discovery (1934/1959); he also made major contributions to political philosophy in The Open Society and Its Enemies (1945).
completion.quote: A theory that is not refutable by any conceivable event is non-scientific.

related.name: Paradigm Shift
related.note: Kuhn showed that actual science protects paradigms from falsification — normal science doesn't abandon theories on single refutations as Popper's model requires.

related.name: Demarcation Problem
related.note: Falsifiability is Popper's proposed solution to demarcation — the criterion separating science from pseudoscience and metaphysics.

related.name: Scientific Method
related.note: Popper's hypothetico-deductivism replaces Baconian inductivism with bold conjecture and rigorous attempted refutation.

related.name: Underdetermination
related.note: The Duhem-Quine thesis shows no single hypothesis is strictly falsifiable in isolation — tests always target whole systems of assumptions.

books.title: The Logic of Scientific Discovery
books.author: Karl Popper
books.why: The foundational text — Popper's full argument for falsifiability as the criterion of scientific demarcation.

books.title: Conjectures and Refutations
books.author: Karl Popper
books.why: Popper's most accessible essays on scientific method and the growth of knowledge.

books.title: Criticism and the Growth of Knowledge
books.author: Lakatos and Musgrave (eds.)
books.why: The 1965 colloquium where Popper, Kuhn, Lakatos, and Feyerabend debated the structure of science — essential context.

completion.name: Feminist Ethics of Care
completion.thinkerBio: Virginia Held (b. 1929) developed care ethics into a comprehensive political philosophy in The Ethics of Care (2006); Joan Tronto made explicit the political dimensions of who performs devalued care work in Moral Boundaries (1993).
completion.quote: The assignment of care work to women, minorities, and the poor maintains power differentials by freeing the powerful from care responsibilities.

related.name: Care Ethics
related.note: This entry develops Gilligan and Noddings's original care ethics into a full political philosophy addressing the structural organization of care work.

related.name: Justice as Fairness
related.note: Held argues care provides a better foundation for political philosophy than Rawlsian justice, which starts from abstract principles rather than concrete relationships of dependency.

related.name: Feminist Political Philosophy
related.note: Political care ethics extends feminist political philosophy's critique of liberalism to specifically address the political organization of dependency and care labor.

related.name: Ubuntu
related.note: Both political care ethics and Ubuntu ground political philosophy in relationships of mutual care and interdependence rather than in atomistic individual rights.

books.title: The Ethics of Care
books.author: Virginia Held
books.why: Held's mature systematic development of care ethics into a comprehensive political philosophy.

books.title: Moral Boundaries
books.author: Joan Tronto
books.why: Tronto's foundational text making explicit the political dimensions of care work's gendered and racialized distribution.

books.title: Love's Labor
books.author: Eva Feder Kittay
books.why: Kittay's philosophical analysis of dependency work and its implications for theories of justice and equality.

completion.name: Feminist Metaphysics
completion.thinkerBio: Sally Haslanger (b. 1955) developed the ameliorative approach to gender concepts at MIT; Judith Butler's later work in Bodies That Matter (1993) clarified the material and discursive status of sex.
completion.quote: Someone is a woman if they are systematically subordinated along social dimensions marked by features taken to justify that subordination -- gender is real but social.

related.name: The Second Sex
related.note: The Second Sex is the founding text for feminist metaphysics -- the philosophical analysis of the ontological status of gender and sexual difference.

related.name: Gender Performativity
related.note: Both Haslanger's ameliorative social-position account and Butler's performativity treat gender as real but socially constituted rather than naturally given.

related.name: Social Ontology
related.note: Feminist metaphysics is a specific application of social ontology -- the study of how social kinds like gender are constituted and maintained.

related.name: Standpoint Epistemology
related.note: Haslanger's social position account of gender connects directly to standpoint epistemology's claim that social position shapes both identity and epistemic access.

books.title: Resisting Reality
books.author: Sally Haslanger
books.why: Haslanger's foundational collection developing the ameliorative approach to gender and race concepts.

books.title: Bodies That Matter
books.author: Judith Butler
books.why: Butler's clarification of the materiality of sex as itself discursively mediated rather than denied.

books.title: Feminism and Philosophy
books.author: Ann Garry and Marilyn Pearsall (eds.)
books.why: A comprehensive anthology covering feminist metaphysics alongside other core areas of feminist philosophy.

completion.name: Feminist Philosophy of Language
completion.thinkerBio: Rae Langton (b. 1961) developed the silencing thesis applying speech act theory to pornography in 'Speech Acts and Unspeakable Acts' (1993); Jennifer Hornsby and Susan Brison further developed the analysis of how oppressive speech can silence its targets.
completion.quote: Pornography may silence women by constituting their refusals as something other than what the speaker intends, depriving them of illocutionary authority.

related.name: Speech Act Theory
related.note: Langton applies Austin's speech act framework to argue that pornography performs harmful illocutionary acts, not merely descriptive ones.

related.name: Speech and Power
related.note: The silencing thesis is a central case study for how speech can constitute and enact power relations rather than merely describing pre-existing social facts.

related.name: Epistemic Injustice
related.note: Both the silencing thesis and epistemic injustice examine how social structures can deprive speakers of the credibility or authority needed for their speech to function normally.

related.name: Gender Performativity
related.note: Both the silencing thesis and gender performativity draw on speech act theory to show how language constitutes rather than merely reflects social and gendered reality.

books.title: Sexual Solipsism
books.author: Rae Langton
books.why: Langton's collected essays developing the silencing thesis and her broader philosophy of objectification and speech.

books.title: Speech Acts and Unspeakable Acts
books.author: Rae Langton, in Philosophy and Public Affairs
books.why: Langton's original foundational paper -- included in the Sexual Solipsism collection -- developing the silencing thesis.

books.title: Only Words
books.author: Catharine MacKinnon
books.why: MacKinnon's influential and provocative legal-philosophical argument connecting pornography to speech and harm.

completion.name: Feminist Philosophy of Science
completion.thinkerBio: Helen Longino (b. 1944) developed contextual empiricism examining the role of background values in scientific reasoning; Evelyn Fox Keller (b. 1936) showed how gendered scientific styles affected Barbara McClintock's groundbreaking genetics research.
completion.quote: A feeling for the organism -- empathetic, intimate engagement with research subjects -- enabled discoveries that a more distanced methodology had missed.

related.name: Standpoint Epistemology
related.note: Feminist philosophy of science is the direct application of standpoint epistemology's insights to scientific practice and objectivity.

related.name: Scientific Realism
related.note: Longino's contextual empiricism complicates simple scientific realism by showing how value-laden background assumptions partially constitute scientific knowledge.

related.name: Theory-Ladenness of Observation
related.note: Feminist philosophy of science extends theory-ladenness to show how gendered values, not just theoretical commitments, shape what scientists observe and investigate.

related.name: Social Epistemology
related.note: Longino's account of objectivity as a property of critical communities rather than individuals is a central contribution to social epistemology.

books.title: The Fate of Knowledge
books.author: Helen Longino
books.why: Longino's foundational development of contextual empiricism and social objectivity in science.

books.title: A Feeling for the Organism
books.author: Evelyn Fox Keller
books.why: Keller's influential biography of Barbara McClintock showing how gendered scientific styles affected major discoveries.

books.title: Reflections on Gender and Science
books.author: Evelyn Fox Keller
books.why: Keller's broader philosophical essays on gender, objectivity, and the practice of science.

completion.name: Feminist Political Philosophy
completion.thinkerBio: Mary Wollstonecraft (1759-1797) founded the tradition with A Vindication of the Rights of Woman (1792); Susan Moller Okin (1946-2004) at Stanford produced the most influential late twentieth century critique of how mainstream political philosophy ignored gender in Justice, Gender, and the Family (1989).
completion.quote: The family is the first school of justice -- or injustice.

related.name: Liberalism
related.note: Liberal feminism extends the liberal framework to women, but feminist critics show the public/private distinction systematically excludes domestic injustice.

related.name: Justice as Fairness
related.note: Okin's critique applies the Rawlsian veil of ignorance to the family, revealing that gendered domestic arrangements would not survive impartial scrutiny.

related.name: The Second Sex
related.note: De Beauvoir's existentialist analysis of women's condition provides the philosophical foundation for later feminist political critique.

related.name: Reproductive Rights
related.note: Reproductive autonomy is among the central concerns of contemporary feminist political philosophy, grounding bodily integrity as a matter of political equality.

books.title: A Vindication of the Rights of Woman
books.author: Mary Wollstonecraft
books.why: The founding text of feminist political philosophy -- the case that women's apparent inferiority results from inadequate education, not nature.

books.title: Justice, Gender, and the Family
books.author: Susan Moller Okin
books.why: Okin's foundational critique applying Rawlsian justice theory to expose the injustice of gendered family structures.

books.title: Women and Human Development
books.author: Martha Nussbaum
books.why: Nussbaum's capabilities approach applied specifically to women's global flourishing and political equality.

completion.name: Fictional Entities
completion.thinkerBio: Peter van Inwagen (b. 1942) defended Meinongianism about fictional characters; Amie Thomasson (b. 1968) developed artifactualism, treating fictional characters as abstract artifacts created through authorship.
completion.quote: Fictional characters genuinely exist as abstract artifacts -- human-created objects that come into being through authorship and persist through cultural practice.

related.name: The Problem of Universals
related.note: Whether fictional characters are abstract objects connects to the broader debate about abstract objects generally.

related.name: Narrative and Fiction
related.note: The metaphysics of fictional entities is the ontological foundation for broader questions about narrative and reference in fiction.

related.name: Possible Worlds
related.note: Some theories treat fictional worlds analogously to possible worlds, though incomplete in ways possible worlds are not.

related.name: Social Ontology
related.note: Thomasson's artifactualism treats fictional characters as a social-institutional object sustained by collective practice.

books.title: Fiction and Metaphysics
books.author: Amie Thomasson
books.why: Thomasson's foundational development of artifactualism about fictional entities.

books.title: Existence: Essays in Ontology
books.author: Peter van Inwagen
books.why: Van Inwagen's essays including his approach to fictional objects.

books.title: Fictional Objects
books.author: Stuart Brock and Anthony Everett (eds.)
books.why: A comprehensive anthology covering positions on the metaphysics of fiction.

completion.name: Forgiveness
completion.thinkerBio: Jeffrie Murphy (b. 1940) developed the philosophical analysis of forgiveness and resentment; Charles Griswold produced the most comprehensive contemporary account.
completion.quote: The forgiveness of the person who has wronged you is among the most powerful moral goods -- and the most philosophically misunderstood.

related.name: Punishment
related.note: Forgiveness releases the demand for retribution central to punitive justice.

related.name: Transitional Justice
related.note: South Africa's TRC was built explicitly on the possibility of political forgiveness.

related.name: Ubuntu as Political Philosophy
related.note: Ubuntu's restorative approach provides philosophical grounding for TRC-style forgiveness.

books.title: Forgiveness and Mercy
books.author: Murphy and Hampton
books.why: The foundational philosophical exchange on forgiveness.

books.title: Forgiveness: A Philosophical Exploration
books.author: Charles Griswold
books.why: The most comprehensive philosophical analysis of forgiveness.

books.title: No Future Without Forgiveness
books.author: Desmond Tutu
books.why: Tutu's first-hand account of political forgiveness in practice.

completion.name: Formal Logic
completion.thinkerBio: Gottlob Frege (1848-1925) invented modern quantifier-variable logic; Kurt Godel (1906-1978) proved the incompleteness theorems that reshaped understanding of formal systems.
completion.quote: Any sufficiently powerful consistent formal system contains true statements that cannot be proved within that system.

related.name: Frege's Philosophy of Language
related.note: Frege's invention of quantifier-variable notation grounds his entire philosophy of language and logic.

related.name: Philosophy of Mathematics
related.note: Formal logic is foundational for the logicist program and subsequent philosophy of mathematics.

related.name: Logical Consequence
related.note: Tarski's model-theoretic account of logical consequence builds directly on formal logic's development.

books.title: Begriffsschrift
books.author: Gottlob Frege, in From Frege to Godel
books.why: Frege's foundational invention of modern symbolic logic, in the essential source anthology.

books.title: Godel's Proof
books.author: Nagel and Newman
books.why: The clearest accessible explanation of the incompleteness theorems.

books.title: Principia Mathematica
books.author: Whitehead and Russell
books.why: The ambitious attempt to derive mathematics from formal logic.

completion.name: Formalism in Art
completion.thinkerBio: Clive Bell (1881-1964) introduced 'significant form' as the criterion of aesthetic value in Art (1914); Roger Fry (1866-1934) developed a parallel formalist account of post-impressionist painting, together founding the Bloomsbury aesthetic tradition.
completion.quote: Significant form is that arrangement and combination of lines and colours that moves us aesthetically, apart from any representational content.

related.name: Aesthetic Disinterestedness
related.note: Aesthetic formalism inherits Kantian disinterestedness -- pure formal appreciation requires suspension of all practical and representational interests.

related.name: Mimesis
related.note: Formalism explicitly rejects the idea that representational accuracy or content has aesthetic value independent of formal arrangement.

related.name: Art as Expression
related.note: Formalism locates aesthetic value purely in formal properties; expression theory locates it in the externalization of the artist's emotional experience.

related.name: The Ontology of Art
related.note: Formalism's emphasis on structure connects to questions about what precisely constitutes the identity of an artwork across different media and instances.

books.title: Art
books.author: Clive Bell
books.why: Bell's foundational statement of significant form as the criterion of aesthetic value.

books.title: Vision and Design
books.author: Roger Fry
books.why: Fry's parallel formalist essays applying the theory to post-impressionist painting.

books.title: Languages of Art
books.author: Nelson Goodman
books.why: Goodman's analytic philosophy of art including a sophisticated critique of formalist assumptions about representation.

completion.name: Foundationalism
completion.thinkerBio: Rene Descartes (1596-1650) is the paradigmatic classical foundationalist; Roderick Chisholm (1916-1999) at Brown developed the most systematic contemporary foundationalist epistemology across multiple editions of Theory of Knowledge.
completion.quote: Knowledge must rest on a foundation of basic beliefs that are justified without depending on any other beliefs.

related.name: Cogito Ergo Sum
related.note: The cogito is the paradigmatic foundationalist basic belief -- certain, self-evident, and the starting point for all further knowledge.

related.name: Coherentism
related.note: Foundationalism and coherentism are the two great rival structures of justification -- hierarchical foundations versus a holistic web of mutual support.

related.name: Reliabilism
related.note: Foundationalism is typically internalist, requiring the believer's access to their justifying basic beliefs; reliabilism is externalist about justification.

related.name: Skepticism
related.note: Foundationalism is motivated by the demand to escape the skeptical regress of justification by finding a non-inferentially justified stopping point.

books.title: Theory of Knowledge
books.author: Roderick Chisholm
books.why: The most systematic contemporary defence of foundationalist epistemology across three editions.

books.title: Meditations on First Philosophy
books.author: Rene Descartes
books.why: The classical foundationalist text -- finding the cogito as a certain foundation for rebuilding knowledge.

books.title: The Structure of Empirical Knowledge
books.author: Laurence BonJour
books.why: BonJour's critique of foundationalism and defence of coherentism -- essential for understanding the debate's stakes.

completion.name: Four-Dimensionalism
completion.thinkerBio: David Lewis (1941-2001) developed modal realism and four-dimensionalism as part of a comprehensive metaphysical system; Theodore Sider (b. 1967) defended four-dimensionalism in Writing the Book of the World (2011).
completion.quote: Objects persist through time by having distinct temporal parts at different times, just as objects have spatial parts at different spatial locations.

related.name: The Ship of Theseus
related.note: Four-dimensionalism resolves the ship puzzle by treating the ship as a four-dimensional worm with different temporal parts having different plank-compositions.

related.name: Material Constitution
related.note: Four-dimensionalism resolves material constitution puzzles by treating the statue and the clay as the same four-dimensional object viewed differently.

related.name: Possible Worlds
related.note: Lewis's four-dimensionalism about persistence and his modal realism about possible worlds are parts of the same comprehensive system.

related.name: Identity Over Time
related.note: Four-dimensionalism dissolves rather than answers traditional questions about identity over time by denying objects wholly exist at any single moment.

books.title: On the Plurality of Worlds
books.author: David Lewis
books.why: Lewis's comprehensive metaphysical system including four-dimensionalism about persistence.

books.title: Writing the Book of the World
books.author: Theodore Sider
books.why: Sider's contemporary defence of four-dimensionalism.

books.title: Four-Dimensionalism
books.author: Theodore Sider
books.why: Sider's focused monograph on temporal parts.

completion.name: Free Will and Moral Responsibility
completion.thinkerBio: Peter Strawson (1919-2006) transformed the free will debate with 'Freedom and Resentment' (1962), grounding responsibility in reactive attitudes rather than metaphysics.
completion.quote: Our practices of holding each other responsible are grounded in participation in reactive attitudes, not in metaphysical facts about causation.

related.name: Free Will vs Determinism
related.note: This entry develops the specific connection between the metaphysical free will debate and moral responsibility practices.

related.name: Moral Luck
related.note: Moral luck and free will together undermine the metaphysical basis for reactive attitudes of blame and praise.

related.name: Punishment
related.note: Theories of punishment presuppose an account of when moral responsibility is genuinely applicable.

books.title: Freedom and Resentment
books.author: P.F. Strawson, in Studies in the Philosophy of Thought and Action
books.why: Strawson's transformative essay grounding responsibility in reactive attitudes.

books.title: Freedom Evolves
books.author: Daniel Dennett
books.why: Dennett's compatibilist development connecting free will to moral responsibility.

books.title: Free Will
books.author: Gary Watson (ed.)
books.why: The definitive anthology covering the responsibility debate.

completion.name: Free Will vs Determinism
completion.thinkerBio: The modern debate was shaped by David Hume (1711–1776) and Immanuel Kant (1724–1804); Peter Strawson (1919–2006) transformed it with 'Freedom and Resentment' (1962); Peter van Inwagen (b. 1942) and Harry Frankfurt (b. 1929) define the contemporary incompatibilist and compatibilist positions.
completion.quote: The question of free will is whether our choices can be genuinely ours in a causally determined world.

related.name: Free Will and Moral Responsibility
related.note: The free will debate directly concerns moral responsibility — whether persons can be held accountable for actions in a determined world.

related.name: Moral Luck
related.note: Moral luck presses the same tension — if outcomes are not fully under our control, the basis for differential moral assessment is threatened.

related.name: Karma
related.note: Both karma and compatibilist free will attempt to preserve moral accountability within a causally structured universe.

related.name: Causation
related.note: The debate turns on the nature of causation — whether deterministic causation is compatible with the self-determination freedom requires.

books.title: An Essay on Free Will
books.author: Peter van Inwagen
books.why: The most rigorous contemporary defence of incompatibilism — the consequence argument stated with full precision.

books.title: Freedom Evolves
books.author: Daniel Dennett
books.why: Dennett's compatibilist argument — why free will is worth wanting and how evolution explains how we got it.

books.title: Free Will
books.author: Gary Watson (ed.)
books.why: The definitive anthology covering the full debate including Frankfurt's hierarchical compatibilism.

completion.name: Freedom and Responsibility
completion.thinkerBio: Jean-Paul Sartre (1905-1980) developed the existentialist account of radical freedom and total responsibility in Being and Nothingness (1943); Simone de Beauvoir (1908-1986) refined it with the concept of situated freedom in The Ethics of Ambiguity (1947).
completion.quote: We are condemned to be free, and with this freedom comes total responsibility for what we make of ourselves.

related.name: Existence Precedes Essence
related.note: Radical freedom and total responsibility follow directly from the claim that existence precedes essence -- there is no fixed nature to excuse our choices.

related.name: Bad Faith
related.note: Bad faith is the flight from the responsibility that radical freedom entails -- the comforting fiction that one's choices were determined by external factors.

related.name: Situation
related.note: De Beauvoir's concept of situation refines Sartrean freedom -- freedom is always exercised within and against material and social constraints, not in an abstract vacuum.

related.name: Free Will vs Determinism
related.note: Existentialist freedom and the analytic free will debate both ask whether and how genuine agency is possible, though existentialism emphasizes lived experience over causal metaphysics.

books.title: Being and Nothingness
books.author: Jean-Paul Sartre
books.why: Sartre's full ontological account of radical freedom and the responsibility it entails.

books.title: The Ethics of Ambiguity
books.author: Simone de Beauvoir
books.why: De Beauvoir's refinement of Sartrean freedom through the concept of situation and material constraint.

books.title: Existentialism Is a Humanism
books.author: Jean-Paul Sartre
books.why: Sartre's most accessible defence of existentialist freedom against charges of moral relativism and despair.

completion.name: Frege's Philosophy of Language
completion.thinkerBio: Gottlob Frege (1848-1925) developed the sense-reference distinction and invented quantifier-variable notation, founding both modern formal logic and analytic philosophy of language as we know them today.
completion.quote: The reference of a sign is the object it designates; the sense is the manner in which the object is presented to the mind.

related.name: Frege's Sense and Reference
related.note: This entry covers the broader philosophical project within which the sense-reference distinction is the central technical innovation.

related.name: Formal Logic
related.note: Frege's invention of quantifier-variable notation made it possible for the first time to formally represent the logical structure of mathematical reasoning.

related.name: Philosophy of Mathematics
related.note: Frege's logicist program, attempting to reduce arithmetic to pure logic, is foundational for the entire subsequent field of philosophy of mathematics.

related.name: Reference and Rigid Designation
related.note: Kripke's rigid designation theory directly challenges Frege's descriptivist account of how proper names secure their reference.

books.title: The Frege Reader
books.author: Michael Beaney (ed.)
books.why: The essential anthology of Frege's foundational writings across logic, mathematics, and philosophy of language.

books.title: Frege: Philosophy of Language
books.author: Michael Dummett
books.why: Dummett's monumental scholarly study of Frege's complete philosophy of language.

books.title: Frege: Philosophy of Mathematics
books.author: Michael Dummett
books.why: Dummett's companion volume focused specifically on Frege's logicist philosophy of mathematics.

completion.name: Frege's Sense and Reference
completion.thinkerBio: Gottlob Frege (1848-1925) introduced the sense-reference distinction in 'On Sense and Reference' (1892), one of the most influential papers in the history of philosophy of language and the founding text of analytic philosophy of language.
completion.quote: The reference of a sign is the object it designates; the sense is the manner in which the object is presented.

related.name: Reference and Rigid Designation
related.note: Kripke's rigid designation theory directly challenges Frege's descriptivism -- names refer without the mediation of sense or description.

related.name: Propositional Attitudes
related.note: Frege used the sense-reference distinction to explain the opacity of propositional attitude contexts -- substituting co-referring terms can change truth value.

related.name: The Liar's Paradox
related.note: Both Frege's logical innovations and the analysis of the Liar's Paradox were part of the same late nineteenth and early twentieth century project of formalizing logic and language.

related.name: Names and Descriptions
related.note: The Frege-Russell descriptivist tradition holds that names express senses equivalent to definite descriptions, a view Kripke later challenged.

books.title: The Frege Reader
books.author: Michael Beaney (ed.)
books.why: The essential anthology of Frege's writings including the foundational sense-reference paper.

books.title: Frege: Philosophy of Language
books.author: Michael Dummett
books.why: Dummett's monumental scholarly study of Frege's philosophy of language.

books.title: Naming and Necessity
books.author: Saul Kripke
books.why: Kripke's direct challenge to the Frege-Russell descriptivist tradition -- essential for understanding the contemporary stakes.

completion.name: Functionalism
completion.thinkerBio: Hilary Putnam (1926–2016) at Harvard introduced multiple realizability and functionalism in the 1960s–70s; Jerry Fodor (1935–2017) developed the language of thought as the functionalist cognitive architecture — both defined the computational theory of mind for a generation.
completion.quote: Mental states are defined by their causal roles, not by their physical composition.

related.name: Multiple Realizability
related.note: Multiple realizability is Putnam's argument for functionalism — if mental states can be realized in different substrates, they must be defined by function not composition.

related.name: Hard Problem of Consciousness
related.note: Chalmers's hard problem challenges functionalism — even perfect functional organization might leave phenomenal experience unexplained.

related.name: Chinese Room
related.note: Searle's Chinese Room attacks the sufficiency of functional organization for genuine understanding.

related.name: Artificial Intelligence and Mind
related.note: Functionalism is the philosophical foundation for strong AI — if minds are functional organizations, they can in principle be implemented in silicon.

books.title: Representation and Reality
books.author: Hilary Putnam
books.why: Putnam's later re-evaluation of functionalism — he ultimately rejected it, and the reasons illuminate its limits.

books.title: The Language of Thought
books.author: Jerry Fodor
books.why: The most developed functionalist cognitive architecture — mentalese and computational mental representations.

books.title: Philosophy of Mind
books.author: Jaegwon Kim
books.why: The clearest textbook treatment of functionalism, its motivations, and its challenges.

completion.name: Fundamentality and Infinite Regress
completion.thinkerBio: Jonathan Schaffer (b. 1970) developed priority monism, arguing the cosmos as a whole is fundamental; Elizabeth Barnes questioned whether fundamentalism itself is required, defending coherent infinite descent.
completion.quote: Perhaps reality has no bottom level -- perhaps the regress of dependence is infinite and well-founded, like the infinite regress of fractions between zero and one.

related.name: Grounding and Metaphysical Dependence
related.note: Fundamentality debates are conducted using the vocabulary of grounding -- whether there must be an ultimate, non-derivative level of grounded facts.

related.name: The Problem of Universals
related.note: Priority monism's claim that the whole is prior to its parts inverts the standard mereological picture relevant to debates about universals.

related.name: Process Philosophy
related.note: Process philosophy's rejection of substance as fundamental connects to debates about whether reality has any privileged fundamental level.

related.name: Naturalism
related.note: Naturalism typically assumes physical facts are fundamental, a substantive commitment fundamentality debates make explicit.

books.title: Monism: The Priority of the Whole
books.author: Jonathan Schaffer
books.why: Schaffer's foundational paper developing priority monism.

books.title: Composition, Coincidence, and Metaontology
books.author: Elizabeth Barnes (ed.)
books.why: Covers debates about fundamentality, composition, and infinite descent.

books.title: Writing the Book of the World
books.author: Theodore Sider
books.why: Sider's structuralist account connects fundamentality to metaphysical structure.

completion.name: Gender Performativity
completion.thinkerBio: Judith Butler (b. 1956) is an American philosopher at Berkeley whose Gender Trouble (1990) transformed feminist theory, queer theory, and philosophy of gender; drawing on Foucault, Derrida, and Lacan, Butler argued that gender is constituted through repeated performance within regulatory frameworks.
completion.quote: There is no gender identity behind the expressions of gender — that identity is performatively constituted by the very expressions that are said to be its results.

related.name: Speech Act Theory
related.note: Butler applies Austin's performative speech acts to gender — gendering acts constitute rather than express a pre-given gender identity.

related.name: The Second Sex
related.note: De Beauvoir's 'becoming a woman' is Butler's starting point — Butler radicalizes it by denying even 'sex' as a natural pre-discursive category.

related.name: Feminist Metaphysics
related.note: Gender performativity is a feminist metaphysics — gender has no natural or essential foundation but is constituted through discursive-performative practices.

related.name: Deconstruction
related.note: Butler applies Derrida's deconstructive analysis to gender — the binary masculine/feminine is shown to be unstable and self-undermining.

books.title: Gender Trouble
books.author: Judith Butler
books.why: The primary source — Butler's argument that gender is performatively constituted rather than expressive of a natural inner truth.

books.title: Bodies That Matter
books.author: Judith Butler
books.why: Butler's response to critics — the clarification that materiality is itself discursively constituted.

books.title: Undoing Gender
books.author: Judith Butler
books.why: Butler's more accessible essays applying gender performativity to trans rights and recognition.

completion.name: General Will
completion.thinkerBio: Jean-Jacques Rousseau (1712-1778) developed the general will in The Social Contract (1762), holding that legitimate political authority expresses not the sum of private interests but the authentic collective good.
completion.quote: The general will is always rightful and always tends to the public advantage -- it is not the sum of private wills but their genuine collective expression.

related.name: Social Contract
related.note: The general will is Rousseau's distinctive contribution to social contract theory.

related.name: Democracy
related.note: Rousseau's general will is the democratic transformation of the social contract idea.

related.name: Communitarianism
related.note: Both hold that genuine political community involves more than the aggregation of individual preferences.

related.name: Utilitarianism
related.note: The general will is explicitly distinguished from the mere sum of private wills, contrasting with utilitarian aggregation.

books.title: The Social Contract
books.author: Jean-Jacques Rousseau
books.why: The primary source -- Rousseau's foundational development of the general will.

books.title: Rousseau: A Very Short Introduction
books.author: Robert Wokler
books.why: A clear, accessible introduction to Rousseau's political philosophy.

books.title: Rousseau, Dewey, and Democracy
books.author: Melvin Rogers
books.why: Connects Rousseau's general will to the broader democratic tradition.

completion.name: Global Justice
completion.thinkerBio: Thomas Pogge (b. 1953) argues wealthy nations harm the global poor through unjust institutions; Peter Singer (b. 1946) grounds obligations in utilitarian impartiality.
completion.quote: If we can prevent something bad without sacrificing anything of comparable moral importance, we ought to do it.

related.name: Utilitarianism
related.note: Singer's argument is the most influential application of impartiality across borders.

related.name: Cosmopolitanism and Obligations
related.note: Cosmopolitanism is the political-philosophical grounding of global justice claims.

related.name: Social Contract
related.note: Contract theory restricts justice to domestic societies; global justice challenges this.

books.title: Famine, Affluence, and Morality
books.author: Peter Singer
books.why: Singer's foundational argument for global aid obligations.

books.title: World Poverty and Human Rights
books.author: Thomas Pogge
books.why: Pogge's institutional harm argument.

books.title: The Life You Can Save
books.author: Peter Singer
books.why: Singer's accessible practical case.

completion.name: Grounding
completion.thinkerBio: Kit Fine (b. 1946) and Jonathan Schaffer (b. 1970) developed contemporary grounding theory as the relation of metaphysical dependence.
completion.quote: Grounding is the relation that holds between a fact and the more fundamental facts that explain it.

related.name: Grounding and Metaphysical Dependence
related.note: This entry is the introductory concept for the fuller technical development.

related.name: Substance and Accident
related.note: Grounding gives contemporary vocabulary for the relation between substances and their accidents.

related.name: Naturalism
related.note: Naturalism is often expressed as the claim all facts are grounded in physical facts.

books.title: On What Grounds What
books.author: Jonathan Schaffer
books.why: Schaffer's foundational paper on grounding as metaphysical structure.

books.title: Metaphysical Grounding
books.author: Correia and Schnieder (eds.)
books.why: The essential anthology of foundational papers.

books.title: Writing the Book of the World
books.author: Theodore Sider
books.why: Connects grounding to fundamentality and structure.

completion.name: Grounding and Metaphysical Dependence
completion.thinkerBio: Kit Fine (b. 1946) at NYU and Jonathan Schaffer (b. 1970) at Rutgers are the leading developers of contemporary grounding theory, which seeks to formalize the relation of metaphysical dependence underlying much philosophical explanation.
completion.quote: Grounding is the relation that holds between a fact and the more fundamental facts that explain it.

related.name: Substance and Accident
related.note: Grounding theory provides a contemporary vocabulary for the relation between substances and their accidents -- accidents are grounded in the substances that bear them.

related.name: Emergence and Supervenience
