related.note: Grounding is often invoked to explain supervenience relations -- mental facts may supervene on and be grounded in physical facts.

related.name: Naturalism
related.note: Naturalism is often expressed as the claim that all facts are ultimately grounded in physical facts.

related.name: Process Philosophy
related.note: Grounding theory typically assumes a layered, hierarchical structure of reality; process philosophy denies any such fixed hierarchical structure of fundamentality.

books.title: Writing the Book of the World
books.author: Theodore Sider
books.why: Sider's structuralist metaphysics develops fundamentality and grounding into a comprehensive metaphysical picture.

books.title: Metaphysical Grounding
books.author: Fabrice Correia and Benjamin Schnieder (eds.)
books.why: The essential anthology collecting the foundational papers in contemporary grounding theory.

books.title: On What Grounds What
books.author: Jonathan Schaffer
books.why: Schaffer's foundational paper developing grounding as the central relation of metaphysical structure.

completion.name: Hard Problem of Consciousness
completion.thinkerBio: David Chalmers (b. 1966) at NYU articulated the hard problem in 'Facing Up to the Problem of Consciousness' (1995) and The Conscious Mind (1996); Daniel Dennett (b. 1942) at Tufts has been his principal opponent in the most productive debate in contemporary philosophy of mind.
completion.quote: Why is there something it is like to undergo conscious experience? Why doesn't all this information-processing go on in the dark?

related.name: Qualia
related.note: Qualia are the specific phenomenal properties whose existence generates the hard problem — the redness of red, the painfulness of pain.

related.name: Functionalism
related.note: Functionalism holds mental states are defined by causal roles — but even a perfect functional duplicate might lack phenomenal experience.

related.name: Panpsychism
related.note: Panpsychism avoids the emergence of consciousness from non-conscious matter by making consciousness fundamental rather than emergent.

related.name: The Hard Problem Revisited
related.note: Contemporary responses — type-B physicalism, illusionism, Russellian monism — are all attempts to resolve or dissolve the hard problem.

books.title: The Conscious Mind
books.author: David Chalmers
books.why: The foundational text — Chalmers's full argument for the hard problem and its implications for physicalism.

books.title: Consciousness Explained
books.author: Daniel Dennett
books.why: The major opposition — Dennett's eliminativist account that the hard problem is an illusion produced by confused thinking.

books.title: The Character of Consciousness
books.author: David Chalmers
books.why: Chalmers's comprehensive later treatment addressing all major responses to the hard problem.

completion.name: Hedonism
completion.thinkerBio: Epicurus (341-270 BCE) developed philosophical hedonism distinguishing tranquil pleasures from base ones; contemporary hedonism about wellbeing is defended by Fred Feldman (b. 1941) and challenged by experience machine thought experiments.
completion.quote: Pleasure is the beginning and end of the blessed life -- but the highest pleasures are tranquility and freedom from disturbance, not mere bodily indulgence.

related.name: Utilitarianism
related.note: Bentham's hedonic calculus is a direct descendant of Epicurean hedonism, treating pleasure and pain as the fundamental currency of moral value.

related.name: Eudaimonia
related.note: Aristotle's objective eudaimonia requires activity and external goods; hedonism reduces wellbeing entirely to the subjective experience of pleasure.

related.name: Death and Dying
related.note: Epicurus's hedonism directly grounds his argument that death should not be feared -- it eliminates the subject of experience rather than causing painful experience.

related.name: Stoic Ethics
related.note: Epicurean hedonism and Stoic virtue ethics represent the two great competing schools of Hellenistic philosophy on the nature of the good life.

books.title: The Essential Epicurus
books.author: Epicurus (trans. Eugene O'Connor)
books.why: The primary surviving texts of Epicurus including his letters and the foundational hedonist arguments.

books.title: Pleasure and the Good Life
books.author: Fred Feldman
books.why: The most rigorous contemporary philosophical defence of hedonism about wellbeing.

books.title: The Nozick Reader
books.author: Robert Nozick (David Schmidtz, ed.)
books.why: Contains Nozick's famous experience machine thought experiment, the central challenge to hedonism.

completion.name: Hegemony
completion.thinkerBio: Antonio Gramsci (1891–1937) was an Italian Marxist theorist who developed hegemony while imprisoned by Mussolini; his Prison Notebooks (written 1929–1935, published 1947–51) constitute one of the most original contributions to twentieth-century political philosophy.
completion.quote: The dominant class does not merely rule — it leads. Hegemony is leadership by consent rather than domination by force.

related.name: Power-Knowledge
related.note: Both Gramsci's hegemony and Foucault's power-knowledge show how power operates through the production and naturalization of knowledge rather than direct coercion.

related.name: Ideology and False Consciousness
related.note: Gramsci's hegemony and Althusser's ideological state apparatuses both explain how the dominant class maintains power through civil society and culture.

related.name: Critical Theory
related.note: Frankfurt School critical theory addresses the same puzzle as Gramsci — why the working class does not revolt against capitalism despite its interests.

related.name: Deliberative Democracy
related.note: Gramsci's analysis of manufactured consent challenges deliberative democracy's assumption that genuine rational consensus is achievable in unequal societies.

books.title: Selections from the Prison Notebooks
books.author: Antonio Gramsci
books.why: The source — Gramsci's development of hegemony, the organic intellectual, and the war of position.

books.title: Hegemony and Socialist Strategy
books.author: Laclau and Mouffe
books.why: The post-Marxist extension of Gramsci's hegemony concept to contemporary radical politics.

books.title: Prison Notebooks
books.author: Antonio Gramsci (complete)
books.why: The complete notebooks — essential for understanding Gramsci's full political philosophy.

completion.name: Hermeneutics
completion.thinkerBio: Wilhelm Dilthey (1833–1911) established hermeneutics as a philosophical discipline; Hans-Georg Gadamer (1900–2002) transformed it in Truth and Method (1960); Paul Ricoeur (1913–2005) extended it to narrative, symbol, and action theory.
completion.quote: To understand is always to understand otherwise.

related.name: Language Games
related.note: Both Wittgenstein and Gadamer hold that meaning is embedded in practices and forms of life that cannot be fully made explicit.

related.name: Hermeneutics of Suspicion
related.note: The hermeneutics of trust (Gadamer, Ricoeur) and the hermeneutics of suspicion (Marx, Nietzsche, Freud) are contrasting orientations toward interpretation.

related.name: Narrative Identity
related.note: Ricoeur's narrative self-constitution is the application of hermeneutics to personal identity — understanding the self through the stories one tells.

related.name: Structuralism
related.note: Structuralism seeks universal laws beneath surface diversity; hermeneutics insists on the irreducibility of historical particularity and tradition.

books.title: Truth and Method
books.author: Hans-Georg Gadamer
books.why: The foundational text of philosophical hermeneutics — the fusion of horizons and the universality of language.

books.title: Interpretation Theory
books.author: Paul Ricoeur
books.why: Ricoeur's synthesis of structural analysis and hermeneutic understanding — the bridge between the two traditions.

books.title: Hermeneutics: An Introduction
books.author: Jens Zimmermann
books.why: The clearest contemporary introduction to philosophical hermeneutics from Schleiermacher through Gadamer and Ricoeur.

completion.name: Hermeneutics of Suspicion
completion.thinkerBio: Paul Ricoeur (1913-2005) coined 'hermeneutics of suspicion' to name the interpretive approach shared by Karl Marx, Friedrich Nietzsche, and Sigmund Freud, each of whom taught readers to look beneath the manifest surface of texts and consciousness.
completion.quote: The three masters of suspicion teach us to read beneath the surface of texts, practices, and consciousness for hidden meanings that the manifest content conceals.

related.name: Ideology and False Consciousness
related.note: Marxist ideology critique is one of the three foundational sources of the hermeneutics of suspicion, alongside Nietzsche's genealogy and Freud's psychoanalysis.

related.name: Will to Power
related.note: Nietzsche's genealogical method, reading moral claims as disguised expressions of will to power, is one of the three masters of suspicion's foundational approaches.

related.name: The Unconscious
related.note: Freudian psychoanalytic interpretation, reading manifest content as disguised unconscious wish, is the third foundational source of the hermeneutics of suspicion.

related.name: Hermeneutics
related.note: Ricoeur explicitly contrasts the hermeneutics of suspicion with a hermeneutics of trust that opens itself to the meaning a text offers rather than reducing it to hidden causes.

books.title: Freud and Philosophy
books.author: Paul Ricoeur
books.why: Ricoeur's foundational text introducing the concept of the hermeneutics of suspicion through a sustained engagement with Freud.

books.title: The Conflict of Interpretations
books.author: Paul Ricoeur
books.why: Ricoeur's broader collection of essays on hermeneutic method, including the tension between suspicion and trust.

books.title: On the Genealogy of Morality
books.author: Friedrich Nietzsche
books.why: Nietzsche's genealogical method is the paradigmatic exercise of suspicious interpretation that Ricoeur's concept names.

completion.name: Higher-Order Evidence
completion.thinkerBio: David Christensen (b. 1954) and Adam Elga (b. 1975) developed conciliationism about peer disagreement as higher-order evidence.
completion.quote: Discovering an epistemic peer reaches a different conclusion from the same evidence is itself evidence one of you has erred.

related.name: Calibration and Epistemic Rationality
related.note: Higher-order evidence bears directly on how well-calibrated agents should respond to disagreement.

related.name: Epistemic Humility
related.note: Conciliationism about peer disagreement is a formal expression of epistemic humility in the face of disagreement.

related.name: Epistemic Autonomy
related.note: Conciliationism in the face of peer disagreement is in tension with maintaining full epistemic autonomy and self-trust.

books.title: Disagreement
books.author: Richard Feldman and Ted Warfield (eds.)
books.why: The essential anthology on peer disagreement and higher-order evidence.

books.title: The Epistemology of Disagreement
books.author: David Christensen and Jennifer Lackey (eds.)
books.why: A focused contemporary anthology on conciliationism and its critics.

books.title: Reasoning About Uncertainty
books.author: Joseph Halpern
books.why: Formal context for reasoning under evidential uncertainty.

completion.name: Humor and Comedy
completion.thinkerBio: Henri Bergson (1859-1941) developed a social-mechanical theory of laughter in Laughter (1900); John Morreall (b. 1947) connected humor to cognitive flexibility, social bonding, and psychological resilience.
completion.quote: We laugh at the encrustation of the mechanical upon the living -- behavior that is rigid and automatic where it should be flexible and responsive.

related.name: Katharsis
related.note: Both katharsis and theories of humor investigate how aesthetic and emotional experiences serve psychological functions of release, education, or cognitive reorientation.

related.name: Art and Morality
related.note: The question of whether humor can be harmful, as in 'punching down' jokes, connects humor theory directly to broader debates about art and morality.

related.name: Emotions and Cognition
related.note: Incongruity theories of humor share with cognitive appraisal theory the view that emotional response, including amusement, involves evaluative cognitive processing.

related.name: Embodied Cognition
related.note: Morreall's connection of humor to cognitive flexibility and resilience parallels embodied cognition's broader interest in adaptive, situated cognitive function.

books.title: Laughter: An Essay on the Meaning of the Comic
books.author: Henri Bergson
books.why: Bergson's foundational social-mechanical theory of laughter and the comic.

books.title: Comic Relief
books.author: John Morreall
books.why: Morreall's comprehensive contemporary philosophy of humor connecting it to cognitive flexibility and wellbeing.

books.title: Humour: A Very Short Introduction
books.author: Noel Carroll
books.why: A clear, comprehensive introduction to the major philosophical theories of humor including incongruity theory.

completion.name: Idealism
completion.thinkerBio: George Berkeley (1685-1753) developed subjective idealism, holding that to be is to be perceived; G.W.F. Hegel (1770-1831) developed absolute idealism, identifying ultimate reality with the self-unfolding of Absolute Spirit through history.
completion.quote: Esse est percipi -- to be is to be perceived. There is no material substance existing independently of perceiving minds.

related.name: Advaita Vedanta
related.note: Both Advaita Vedanta and Western idealism hold that mind or consciousness is fundamental to reality, though through very different metaphysical frameworks.

related.name: Yogacara and Mind-Only
related.note: Yogacara's denial of mind-independent external objects has deep structural affinities with Berkeleyan subjective idealism.

related.name: Substance Dualism
related.note: Berkeley developed idealism partly to dissolve Cartesian dualism's interaction problem by denying material substance entirely rather than positing two substances.

related.name: Dialectics
related.note: Hegel's absolute idealism is the metaphysical framework within which his dialectical method of Spirit's self-development operates.

books.title: A Treatise Concerning the Principles of Human Knowledge
books.author: George Berkeley
books.why: Berkeley's foundational and surprisingly rigorous defence of subjective idealism.

books.title: The Phenomenology of Spirit
books.author: G.W.F. Hegel
books.why: Hegel's systematic development of absolute idealism through the dialectical history of consciousness.

books.title: Idealism: The History of a Philosophy
books.author: Jeremy Dunham, Iain Hamilton Grant, and Sean Watson
books.why: A comprehensive historical survey of idealism from Berkeley through Hegel to its contemporary descendants.

completion.name: Identity Over Time
completion.thinkerBio: John Locke (1632–1704) formulated the memory criterion in An Essay Concerning Human Understanding (1689); Derek Parfit (1942–2017) transformed the debate in Reasons and Persons (1984) by arguing that personal identity is indeterminate and not what matters in survival.
completion.quote: Personal identity consists not in the identity of substance, but in the identity of consciousness.

related.name: The Ship of Theseus
related.note: Personal identity over time is the most practically urgent particular case of the general Ship of Theseus puzzle.

related.name: Personal Identity
related.note: Identity over time and personal identity are the same problem — what makes you the same person as the infant in that photograph.

related.name: Anatta
related.note: Parfit's no-self conclusion arrives at structurally similar results to Buddhist anatta through analytic philosophical argument.

related.name: Memory and Personal Identity
related.note: Locke's memory criterion grounds identity over time in epistemological continuity — I am the same person as that child because I remember being them.

books.title: Reasons and Persons
books.author: Derek Parfit
books.why: Part III contains Parfit's argument that personal identity is indeterminate and not what matters in survival.

books.title: Personal Identity
books.author: Sydney Shoemaker and Richard Swinburne
books.why: Two contrasting views — psychological continuity vs substance — in one concise volume.

books.title: Self and World
books.author: Quassim Cassam
books.why: Contemporary account connecting metaphysical questions about personal identity to phenomenology and epistemology.

completion.name: Ideology and False Consciousness
completion.thinkerBio: Louis Althusser (1918-1990) developed the theory of ideological state apparatuses in 'Ideology and Ideological State Apparatuses' (1970); Slavoj Zizek (b. 1949) extended ideology critique through Lacanian psychoanalysis.
completion.quote: They know very well what they are doing, but still they are doing it -- cynical distance is not liberation from ideology but its most effective contemporary form.

related.name: Hegemony
related.note: Althusser's ideological state apparatuses and Gramsci's hegemony both explain how dominant classes maintain power through civil society and culture.

related.name: The Unconscious
related.note: Zizek's extension of ideology critique through Lacan locates ideological mystification in unconscious social practice rather than merely conscious false belief.

related.name: Power-Knowledge
related.note: Both Althusserian ideology and Foucauldian power-knowledge examine how subjects are produced by structures of power that operate prior to conscious choice.

related.name: The Spectacle
related.note: Both ideology theory and Debord's spectacle analyze how capitalist society produces forms of consciousness that obscure or naturalize its own contradictions.

books.title: On Ideology
books.author: Louis Althusser
books.why: Contains Althusser's foundational essay on ideological state apparatuses and interpellation.

books.title: The Sublime Object of Ideology
books.author: Slavoj Zizek
books.why: Zizek's foundational Lacanian extension of ideology critique into cynical distance and enjoyment.

books.title: Mapping Ideology
books.author: Slavoj Zizek (ed.)
books.why: An essential anthology collecting the major texts in the development of ideology theory from Marx through Zizek.

completion.name: Imagination
completion.thinkerBio: Immanuel Kant (1724-1804) developed a sophisticated theory of productive imagination in the Critique of Pure Reason and Critique of Judgment; Jean-Paul Sartre (1905-1980) gave imagination a phenomenological treatment in The Imaginary (1940), distinguishing it sharply from perception.
completion.quote: Imagination is not a weaker form of perception but a wholly different mode of consciousness -- the positing of an absent or unreal object.

related.name: Art as Expression
related.note: Expression requires imagination -- both the artist's imaginative clarification of experience and the audience's imaginative reception of the resulting work.

related.name: Aesthetic Disinterestedness
related.note: Kant's account of beauty depends on the free harmonious play of imagination and understanding, unconstrained by either concepts or practical interest.

related.name: Existence Precedes Essence
related.note: For Sartre, imagination is closely tied to freedom -- the capacity to posit what is absent or unreal is itself an exercise of the negating freedom of consciousness.

related.name: Imagination and Mental Imagery
related.note: Both philosophical accounts of imagination and the cognitive science of mental imagery investigate the structure and function of representing what is not present.

books.title: Critique of Judgment
books.author: Immanuel Kant
books.why: Kant's account of the free play of imagination and understanding underlying aesthetic judgment.

books.title: The Imaginary
books.author: Jean-Paul Sartre
books.why: Sartre's phenomenology of imagination as a distinct mode of consciousness from perception.

books.title: Imagination: A Very Short Introduction
books.author: Jennifer Gosetti-Ferencei
books.why: A clear, comprehensive introduction connecting philosophical theories of imagination across traditions.

completion.name: Imagination and Mental Imagery
completion.thinkerBio: Jean-Paul Sartre (1905-1980) developed the phenomenology of imagination in The Imaginary (1940); Stephen Kosslyn (b. 1948) conducted the foundational experimental work supporting depictive theories of mental imagery in cognitive science.
completion.quote: Mental images are depictive, quasi-pictorial representations with genuine spatial properties, not merely propositional descriptions.

related.name: Imagination
related.note: This entry connects philosophical accounts of imagination to the cognitive science debate over the depictive versus propositional nature of mental imagery.

related.name: Existence Precedes Essence
related.note: For Sartre, imagination is closely tied to freedom -- the capacity to posit absent or unreal objects is an exercise of the negating freedom of consciousness.

related.name: Qualia
related.note: Whether mental images have genuine qualitative, picture-like character connects directly to broader debates about the nature of phenomenal qualia.

related.name: Language of Thought
related.note: If mental imagery is genuinely depictive rather than propositional, this challenges Fodor's claim that all cognition operates through a language-like symbolic medium.

books.title: The Imaginary
books.author: Jean-Paul Sartre
books.why: Sartre's foundational phenomenological account distinguishing imagination from perception.

books.title: Image and Brain
books.author: Stephen Kosslyn
books.why: Kosslyn's comprehensive presentation of the experimental case for depictive mental imagery.

books.title: The Imagery Debate
books.author: Michael Tye
books.why: A balanced philosophical analysis of the depictive versus propositional debate over mental imagery.

completion.name: Implicature and Pragmatic Inference
completion.thinkerBio: H.P. Grice (1913-1988) developed conversational implicature in his 1967 Logic Lectures, later published in Studies in the Way of Words (1989), establishing the conversational maxims that ground pragmatic inference.
completion.quote: Speakers communicate more than they literally say, and hearers infer these meanings by assuming the speaker is observing maxims of cooperative conversation.

related.name: Pragmatics
related.note: This entry develops Grice's foundational maxims that ground the broader study of pragmatic inference and conversational meaning.

related.name: Relevance and Communication
related.note: Sperber and Wilson's relevance theory replaces Grice's four maxims with a single unified cognitive principle of optimal relevance.

related.name: Speech Act Theory
related.note: Implicature theory complements speech act theory -- both examine how utterances communicate beyond their literal semantic content.

related.name: Metaphor
related.note: Grice's framework was extended to explain metaphor as a deliberate violation of the maxim of quality that hearers resolve through pragmatic inference.

books.title: Studies in the Way of Words
books.author: H.P. Grice
books.why: The foundational collection containing Grice's original development of conversational implicature and the cooperative principle.

books.title: Relevance: Communication and Cognition
books.author: Dan Sperber and Deirdre Wilson
books.why: The most influential subsequent development, replacing Grice's maxims with a unified relevance principle.

books.title: Pragmatics
books.author: Stephen Levinson
books.why: The standard comprehensive textbook treatment of implicature and pragmatic theory.

completion.name: Inauthenticity and the They-Self
completion.thinkerBio: Martin Heidegger (1889-1976) introduced das Man (the they-self) and inauthenticity in Being and Time (1927) as the default, anonymous mode of everyday existence from which authentic existence must be actively won.
completion.quote: Everyone is the other, and no one is himself -- the they-self, which everyday Dasein is, is the nobody.

related.name: Authenticity
related.note: The they-self and inauthenticity are the default conditions from which authentic existence must be won through confrontation with one's finitude and freedom.

related.name: Bad Faith
related.note: Sartre's bad faith and Heidegger's inauthenticity are parallel analyses of flight from genuine selfhood, grounded in different ontologies.

related.name: Thrownness
related.note: The they-self is one way of being thrown into a pre-given social world of inherited interpretations, practices, and norms.

related.name: Hegemony
related.note: Both the they-self and Gramscian hegemony describe how conformity to dominant social patterns operates without explicit coercion.

books.title: Being and Time
books.author: Martin Heidegger
books.why: The source -- Division One develops das Man and inauthenticity as fundamental existential structures.

books.title: Heidegger: A Very Short Introduction
books.author: Michael Inwood
books.why: The most accessible introduction to Heidegger's account of the they-self and inauthenticity.

books.title: The Present Age
books.author: Soren Kierkegaard
books.why: Kierkegaard's earlier critique of conformity and the public anticipates Heidegger's they-self by nearly a century.

completion.name: Indexicals and Context
completion.thinkerBio: David Kaplan (b. 1933) developed two-dimensional semantics for indexicals in 'Demonstratives' (1989); John Perry (b. 1943) developed the essential indexical argument showing indexical thought cannot be replaced by objective descriptions.
completion.quote: A shopper who knows 'the person making a mess is I' is moved to act in ways that knowing the same fact in third-person terms would not produce.

related.name: Frege's Sense and Reference
related.note: Kaplan's two-dimensional semantics extends and complicates Frege's original sense-reference framework for context-sensitive expressions.

related.name: Cogito Ergo Sum
related.note: Both indexical thought and the Cartesian cogito privilege the irreducible first-person perspective as philosophically fundamental.

related.name: The Self as Construction
related.note: Perry's essential indexical argument bears on debates about the metaphysical status of the self.

related.name: Semantic Externalism
related.note: Both indexicals and semantic externalism complicate simple internalist pictures of meaning.

books.title: Themes from Kaplan
books.author: Joseph Almog, John Perry, Howard Wettstein (eds.)
books.why: Contains Kaplan's foundational paper developing two-dimensional semantics for indexical expressions.

books.title: The Problem of the Essential Indexical
books.author: John Perry
books.why: Perry's foundational argument that indexical thought is irreducible to objective description.

books.title: Demonstratives, Indexicals and Anaphora
books.author: Francois Recanati
books.why: A comprehensive contemporary treatment of indexical reference and context-sensitivity.

completion.name: Inference to Best Explanation
completion.thinkerBio: Charles Sanders Peirce (1839–1914) introduced abduction as a third form of inference; Peter Lipton (1954–2007) at Cambridge gave IBE its most sophisticated philosophical treatment in Inference to the Best Explanation (1991/2004).
completion.quote: We infer the truth of a hypothesis because it provides the best explanation of the evidence.

related.name: Scientific Realism
related.note: The no-miracles argument for scientific realism is an IBE — the best explanation of science's predictive success is that theories are approximately true.

related.name: Pragmatism
related.note: IBE as abduction (Peirce) and pragmatism share the same philosophical ancestry — both evaluate hypotheses by their explanatory consequences.

related.name: Bayesian Epistemology
related.note: Bayesian confirmation theory provides a formal framework for IBE — the probability of a hypothesis increases with how well it explains the evidence.

related.name: Causation in Science
related.note: IBE in causal reasoning identifies the best causal explanation of observed effects — the mechanism most plausibly producing the data.

books.title: Inference to the Best Explanation
books.author: Peter Lipton
books.why: The definitive philosophical treatment — Lipton's account of IBE and the 'loveliest' explanation.

books.title: Collected Papers Vol. 2
books.author: Charles Sanders Peirce
books.why: Contains Peirce's original account of abduction — the inference to a hypothesis that explains surprising facts.

books.title: The View from Nowhere
books.author: Thomas Nagel
books.why: Nagel's account of how explanation and understanding connect to objectivity — relevant to IBE's epistemic claims.

completion.name: Intellectual Virtues
completion.thinkerBio: Linda Zagzebski (b. 1946) and Jason Baehr (b. 1975) developed the systematic account of intellectual virtues as the basis for virtue epistemology.
completion.quote: Intellectual humility, courage, and open-mindedness are stable character traits, not merely reliable processes.

related.name: Virtue Epistemology
related.note: Intellectual virtues are the specific character traits that virtue epistemology's account of knowledge is grounded in.

related.name: Epistemic Humility
related.note: Intellectual humility is one of the central virtues identified by virtue epistemologists.

related.name: Epistemic Akrasia
related.note: Epistemic akrasia represents a failure of the intellectual virtues that good epistemic agency requires.

books.title: Virtues of the Mind
books.author: Linda Zagzebski
books.why: Zagzebski's foundational Aristotelian development of intellectual virtue.

books.title: The Inquiring Mind
books.author: Jason Baehr
books.why: Baehr's accessible development connecting intellectual virtues to education.

books.title: A Virtue Epistemology
books.author: Ernest Sosa
books.why: Sosa's systematic apt belief framework grounding virtue-theoretic knowledge.

completion.name: Interpretation
completion.thinkerBio: E.D. Hirsch (1928-2021) defended intentionalism about textual meaning in Validity in Interpretation (1967); Roland Barthes (1915-1980) famously declared 'The Death of the Author' (1967) in the same year, arguing the opposite extreme.
completion.quote: Once a text is published, the author dies as the source of its meaning -- meaning is produced in the reading.

related.name: Hermeneutics
related.note: Gadamer's hermeneutics offers a middle position between Hirsch's intentionalism and Barthes's reader-centered view -- interpretation as dialogue between text and interpreter.

related.name: Deconstruction
related.note: Derrida's deconstruction radicalizes Barthes's challenge to authorial authority, showing that texts undermine even their apparent intended meanings.

related.name: Narrative and Fiction
related.note: Debates about interpretation directly bear on what fictional texts can be correctly said to mean and whether authorial intention settles such questions.

related.name: Hermeneutics of Suspicion
related.note: Both the death of the author and the hermeneutics of suspicion challenge the idea that a text's surface or its author's conscious intentions exhaust its meaning.

books.title: Validity in Interpretation
books.author: E.D. Hirsch
books.why: Hirsch's foundational defence of authorial intention as the criterion of correct textual meaning.

books.title: Image, Music, Text
books.author: Roland Barthes
books.why: Contains 'The Death of the Author' -- Barthes's influential argument against authorial authority over meaning.

books.title: Truth and Method
books.author: Hans-Georg Gadamer
books.why: Gadamer's hermeneutic middle path between pure intentionalism and pure reader-response theory.

completion.name: Intersectionality
completion.thinkerBio: Kimberlé Crenshaw (b. 1959) is an American legal scholar who introduced intersectionality in 'Demarginalizing the Intersection of Race and Sex' (1989) and 'Mapping the Margins' (1991); she developed it specifically to address the failures of anti-discrimination law to protect Black women.
completion.quote: If I were to identify a single metaphor for intersectionality, it would be a traffic intersection — multiple roads of oppression crossing simultaneously.

related.name: Feminist Political Philosophy
related.note: Intersectionality transforms feminist political philosophy by showing that gender cannot be analyzed independently of race, class, and other axes of oppression.

related.name: Standpoint Epistemology
related.note: Intersectionality specifies that standpoints are intersectional, compounding epistemic advantages and disadvantages in complex ways.

related.name: Epistemic Injustice
related.note: Intersectional subjects experience compound epistemic injustice — their testimony is deflated along multiple dimensions of marginalized identity.

related.name: Race and Political Philosophy
related.note: Intersectionality provides the analytical framework for race and political philosophy — race cannot be addressed without simultaneously addressing gender and class.

books.title: On Intersectionality: Essential Writings
books.author: Kimberlé Crenshaw
books.why: Crenshaw's own collected essays on intersectionality — the primary source from its originator.

books.title: Black Feminist Thought
books.author: Patricia Hill Collins
books.why: Collins's matrix of domination — the most systematic development of intersectional social theory.

books.title: Sister Outsider
books.author: Audre Lorde
books.why: Lorde's essays on race, gender, and sexuality — essential reading for understanding the experiential basis of intersectionality.

completion.name: Intersectionality and Political Theory
completion.thinkerBio: Patricia Hill Collins (b. 1948) developed the matrix of domination, extending Crenshaw's legal intersectionality into a comprehensive social theory of interlocking systems of oppression and their controlling images.
completion.quote: Controlling images -- stereotyped representations that justify oppression -- function as a dimension of domination operating through cultural representation itself.

related.name: Intersectionality
related.note: This entry develops Crenshaw's original legal concept into Collins's comprehensive social theory of the matrix of domination.

related.name: Race and Political Philosophy
related.note: Collins's controlling images concept directly extends Mills's racial contract theory by analyzing how cultural representation justifies racial and gendered subordination.

related.name: Hegemony
related.note: Controlling images and Gramscian hegemony both examine how cultural representation naturalizes and justifies systems of social domination.

related.name: Standpoint Epistemology
related.note: Collins's earlier standpoint epistemology work directly informs the matrix of domination's account of how multiple oppressions shape knowledge and experience.

books.title: Black Feminist Thought
books.author: Patricia Hill Collins
books.why: Collins's foundational and most comprehensive development of the matrix of domination and controlling images.

books.title: On Intersectionality: Essential Writings
books.author: Kimberle Crenshaw
books.why: Crenshaw's original collected legal essays that Collins's social theory builds upon and extends.

books.title: Intersectionality
books.author: Patricia Hill Collins and Sirma Bilge
books.why: A comprehensive contemporary overview of intersectionality theory's development and applications.

completion.name: Intersubjectivity
completion.thinkerBio: Edmund Husserl (1859-1938) treated intersubjectivity as phenomenology's hardest problem; Maurice Merleau-Ponty (1908-1961) rooted it in embodiment rather than inference.
completion.quote: We recognize other persons not through theoretical inference but through a primordial bodily resonance.

related.name: Phenomenology of Perception
related.note: Merleau-Ponty grounds intersubjectivity in bodily resonance and intercorporeality.

related.name: Existential Phenomenology
related.note: Intersubjectivity is grounded in embodied encounter within existential phenomenology's broader framework.

related.name: Theory of Mind
related.note: Both intersubjectivity and theory of mind investigate how we understand other minded beings.

books.title: Cartesian Meditations
books.author: Edmund Husserl
books.why: Husserl's foundational and demanding treatment of intersubjectivity.

books.title: Phenomenology of Perception
books.author: Maurice Merleau-Ponty
books.why: Merleau-Ponty's embodied alternative grounding intersubjectivity in bodily resonance.

books.title: The Phenomenology Reader
books.author: Moran and Mooney (eds.)
books.why: Comprehensive coverage of intersubjectivity across the phenomenological tradition.

completion.name: Jain Epistemology
completion.thinkerBio: Mahavira (traditional dates: 599-527 BCE) is the founding Tirthankara whose teachings underlie Jain philosophy; Umasvati (c. second century CE) systematized Jain metaphysics and epistemology in the Tattvarthasutra.
completion.quote: Reality is complex and multi-faceted -- no single perspective can capture it completely, and every claim is true from some standpoint and incomplete from others.

related.name: Moral Relativism
related.note: Jain anekantavada is not relativism -- it holds that reality genuinely exceeds any single description, not that all claims are equally valid regardless of perspective.

related.name: The Liar's Paradox
related.note: Both syadvada's seven-fold predication and Western paradoxes of self-reference investigate how apparently contradictory predications can be coherently structured.

related.name: Jain Philosophy of Ahimsa
related.note: Both Jain epistemology and Jain ahimsa reflect the tradition's characteristic combination of metaphysical rigor and ethical comprehensiveness.

related.name: Epistemic Humility
related.note: Anekantavada is among the most philosophically sophisticated grounds for epistemic humility in any tradition -- it builds partiality of perspective into the structure of reality itself.

books.title: The Jaina Path of Purification
books.author: Padmanabh Jaini
books.why: The definitive scholarly study of Jain philosophy including its sophisticated epistemological doctrines.

books.title: Anekantavada: The Central Philosophy of Jainism
books.author: Bharat Mahodaya
books.why: A focused scholarly treatment of anekantavada and syadvada, Jainism's central epistemological doctrines.

books.title: Tattvartha Sutra
books.author: Umasvati (trans. Nathmal Tatia)
books.why: The foundational systematic Jain philosophical text covering metaphysics and epistemology.

completion.name: Jain Philosophy of Ahimsa
completion.thinkerBio: Mahavira (traditional dates: 599-527 BCE) is the twenty-fourth Tirthankara of Jainism, whose teachings on non-violence extended to all living beings; the Jain tradition has developed the most rigorous and comprehensive philosophy of non-harm of any world religious tradition.
completion.quote: All souls are fundamentally equal -- the distinction between a human soul and an earthworm's soul is a difference of degree, not of kind.

related.name: Environmental Ethics
related.note: Jain ahimsa extended to all living beings -- even micro-organisms -- is the most radical non-Western parallel to contemporary environmental ethics.

related.name: Buddhist Ethics
related.note: Both Jain ahimsa and Buddhist ethics extend moral concern to all sentient beings, though Jainism's prohibitions are considerably more absolute.

related.name: Civil Disobedience
related.note: Gandhi's satyagraha and his philosophy of nonviolent resistance drew explicitly on Jain ahimsa as a primary intellectual source.

related.name: Care Ethics
related.note: Both Jain ahimsa and care ethics ground ethical obligation in attentive responsiveness to the vulnerability of others, extended in Jainism to the broadest possible scope.

books.title: The Jaina Path of Purification
books.author: Padmanabh Jaini
books.why: The definitive scholarly study of Jain philosophy and practice including the doctrine of ahimsa.

books.title: Ahimsa: Non-Violence in Indian Tradition
books.author: Unto Tahtinen
books.why: A comparative philosophical study of non-violence doctrines across Jain, Hindu, and Buddhist traditions.

books.title: Hind Swaraj
books.author: Mahatma Gandhi
books.why: Gandhi's own development of nonviolent philosophy, explicitly indebted to Jain ahimsa.

completion.name: Justice and Equality
completion.thinkerBio: John Rawls (1921-2002) developed the most systematic liberal egalitarianism; Ronald Dworkin (1931-2013) defended equality of resources; G.A. Cohen (1941-2009) pressed both from a socialist direction.
completion.quote: A just society is one arranged so that its least advantaged members do as well as possible.

related.name: Original Position
related.note: The difference principle derived from the original position is Rawls's egalitarian principle -- inequalities are just only if they benefit the least advantaged.

related.name: Luck Egalitarianism
related.note: Luck egalitarianism is the development of the egalitarian intuition that inequalities from brute luck rather than choice are unjust.

related.name: Socialism
related.note: Socialist justice theories extend egalitarianism to argue that structural features of capitalist property relations are themselves sources of injustice.

related.name: Liberalism
related.note: Liberal egalitarianism combines liberty and equality -- Rawls, Dworkin, and Sen all accept a liberal framework while building strong egalitarian principles within it.

books.title: A Theory of Justice
books.author: John Rawls
books.why: The foundational liberal egalitarian text -- the two principles of justice including the difference principle.

books.title: Sovereign Virtue
books.author: Ronald Dworkin
books.why: Dworkin's equality of resources -- the most systematic attempt to cash out what genuine equality requires in practice.

books.title: Self-Ownership, Freedom, and Equality
books.author: G.A. Cohen
books.why: Cohen's critique of Rawls and Nozick from a socialist egalitarian perspective.

completion.name: Justice as Fairness
completion.thinkerBio: John Rawls (1921-2002) developed justice as fairness across A Theory of Justice (1971), Political Liberalism (1993), and Justice as Fairness: A Restatement (2001), refining the theory in response to decades of philosophical critique.
completion.quote: Each person is to have an equal right to the most extensive basic liberty compatible with a like liberty for others.

related.name: Original Position
related.note: Justice as fairness is the theory that the original position thought experiment is designed to derive -- the two principles of justice.

related.name: Utilitarianism
related.note: Justice as fairness is explicitly designed as an alternative to utilitarianism, taking the separateness of persons seriously rather than aggregating welfare.

related.name: Luck Egalitarianism
related.note: Luck egalitarianism refines and radicalizes the difference principle's treatment of natural talent and social background as morally arbitrary.

related.name: Communitarianism
related.note: Communitarian critics like Sandel argue justice as fairness presupposes an impoverished, unencumbered conception of the self.

books.title: A Theory of Justice
books.author: John Rawls
books.why: The original and most comprehensive statement of justice as fairness.

books.title: Justice as Fairness: A Restatement
books.author: John Rawls
books.why: Rawls's own simplified and refined restatement, responding to decades of critique.

books.title: Political Liberalism
books.author: John Rawls
books.why: Rawls's reformulation of justice as fairness as a political rather than comprehensive moral doctrine.

completion.name: Karma
completion.thinkerBio: The concept of karma is found across Hindu, Buddhist, and Jain traditions; its first systematic discussion appears in the Upanishads (c. 800–400 BCE), with the Bhagavad Gita providing the most philosophically sophisticated treatment through the doctrine of nishkama karma.
completion.quote: As a man acts, as he behaves, so does he become — the doer of good becomes good, the doer of evil becomes evil.

related.name: Dharma
related.note: Dharma and karma are interdependent — acting according to one's dharma generates good karma; violation generates negative consequences.

related.name: Free Will vs Determinism
related.note: Karma raises the same question as the free will debate — how is moral accountability possible within a causally structured universe?

related.name: Moral Luck
related.note: Karma denies moral luck at the cosmic level — apparent luck is the result of prior karma, making no suffering ultimately arbitrary.

related.name: Buddhist Ethics
related.note: Karma is the causal mechanism through which ethical action bears fruit — understanding it is fundamental to Buddhist ethical motivation.

books.title: The Bhagavad Gita
books.author: trans. Barbara Stoler Miller
books.why: The most philosophically sophisticated treatment of karma and the doctrine of nishkama karma.

books.title: What the Buddha Taught
books.author: Walpola Rahula
books.why: The clearest short explanation of the Buddhist doctrine of karma and its relation to dependent origination.

books.title: Karma and Rebirth in Classical Indian Traditions
books.author: Wendy Doniger O'Flaherty (ed.)
books.why: Comprehensive scholarly anthology of karma doctrines across Hindu, Buddhist, and Jain traditions.

completion.name: Karma and Free Will
completion.thinkerBio: Buddhist and Hindu philosophical traditions address the tension between karmic causation and genuine moral agency through distinct metaphysical frameworks.
completion.quote: If all present experience results from past karma, and karma generates future experience necessarily, is genuine choice possible?

related.name: Karma
related.note: This entry develops the specific tension between karma's causal determinism and the freedom karma's ethical framework presupposes.

related.name: Free Will vs Determinism
related.note: Karma raises the same fundamental question as the Western free will debate through a different metaphysical framework.

related.name: The Bhagavad Gita and Action
related.note: The Gita's nishkama karma is one proposed resolution -- desireless action generates no new karmic binding.

books.title: The Bhagavad Gita
books.author: trans. Barbara Stoler Miller
books.why: The Gita's resolution through desireless action performed without ego-attachment.

books.title: Karma and Rebirth in Classical Indian Traditions
books.author: Wendy Doniger O'Flaherty (ed.)
books.why: Comprehensive scholarly treatment of karma across traditions including the free will tension.

books.title: Samkhya: A Dualist Tradition in Indian Philosophy
books.author: Gerald Larson
books.why: Samkhya's account of Purusha's freedom from Prakriti's causal determinism.

completion.name: Katharsis
completion.thinkerBio: Aristotle (384–322 BCE) introduced katharsis in the Poetics (c. 335 BCE) in a single famously obscure sentence; the debate about its meaning — purgation, purification, or clarification — has continued for two thousand years.
completion.quote: Tragedy effects through pity and fear the proper purgation of these emotions.

related.name: Mimesis
related.note: Katharsis is the effect that mimesis in tragedy achieves — it justifies artistic representation against Plato's attack.

related.name: Art and Morality
related.note: Katharsis is Aristotle's response to Plato's fear that tragedy inflames emotion — it purifies, educates, or clarifies rather than corrupting.

related.name: Tragedy
related.note: Katharsis is the defining effect of tragedy as a genre — what distinguishes it from mere depiction of suffering.

related.name: Emotions and Cognition
related.note: Nussbaum's cognitive theory of emotion supports the clarification interpretation — tragedy educates emotional intelligence.

books.title: Poetics
books.author: Aristotle (trans. Malcolm Heath)
books.why: The primary source — the famous definition and its context in Aristotle's analysis of tragedy.

books.title: The Fragility of Goodness
books.author: Martha Nussbaum
books.why: Nussbaum's influential cognitive interpretation of katharsis as emotional education through tragedy.

books.title: Aristotle on the Art of Poetry
books.author: Ingram Bywater
books.why: A scholarly commentary on the Poetics including the full history of katharsis interpretation.

completion.name: Kitsch
completion.thinkerBio: Clement Greenberg (1909-1994) distinguished avant-garde from kitsch in his influential 1939 essay; Milan Kundera (1929-2023) gave kitsch a political dimension in The Unbearable Lightness of Being.
completion.quote: Kitsch is the absolute denial of shit -- the aesthetic of a world in which everything unpleasant is excluded in favor of the sentimental.

related.name: The Spectacle
related.note: Both kitsch and Debord's spectacle theory examine how mass culture commodifies and standardizes aesthetic experience.

related.name: Art and Morality
related.note: Whether the distinction between kitsch and genuine art is defensible or merely cultural snobbery connects to broader art-morality debates.

related.name: Ideology and False Consciousness
related.note: Kundera's political kitsch and ideology theory both examine how comforting, simplified representations serve to mask uncomfortable realities.

books.title: Avant-Garde and Kitsch
books.author: Clement Greenberg, in Art and Culture
books.why: Greenberg's foundational essay distinguishing avant-garde art from kitsch.

books.title: The Unbearable Lightness of Being
books.author: Milan Kundera
books.why: Kundera's philosophical novel developing kitsch's political and existential dimensions.

books.title: Kitsch and Art
books.author: Tomas Kulka
books.why: A systematic philosophical analysis of kitsch as an aesthetic category.

completion.name: Knowing How vs Knowing That
completion.thinkerBio: Gilbert Ryle (1900-1976) distinguished knowing how from knowing that in The Concept of Mind (1949); Jason Stanley (b. 1969) and Timothy Williamson (b. 1955) challenged Ryle's distinction, arguing knowing how reduces to propositional knowledge.
completion.quote: Knowing how to F is knowing that a certain way W is a way for one to F, under an appropriate practical mode of presentation.

related.name: Wu Wei
related.note: Ryle's irreducible practical knowing how resonates with wu wei's emphasis on skilled action, which intellectualism challenges.

related.name: The Mind-Body Problem
related.note: Ryle's category mistake critique of dualism connects to his dispositional account of mental concepts.

related.name: Functionalism
related.note: Ryle's dispositional analysis anticipates aspects of functionalism's treatment of mental states.

related.name: Wang Yangming's Mind-Only Philosophy
related.note: Both investigate the connection between practical knowledge and skilled performance.

books.title: The Concept of Mind
books.author: Gilbert Ryle
books.why: Ryle's foundational text on knowing how/knowing that.

books.title: Know How
books.author: Jason Stanley
books.why: Stanley's full development of the intellectualist account.

books.title: Knowledge and Practice
books.author: Adrian Moore (ed.)
books.why: Covers debates on the nature of practical knowledge.

completion.name: Knowledge and Context
completion.thinkerBio: John Hawthorne (b. 1964) developed the knowledge-action principle and lottery puzzles challenging fixed epistemic standards; Jeremy Fantl and Matthew McGrath developed interest-relative invariantism connecting knowledge to practical stakes.
completion.quote: It is appropriate to treat p as a reason for action only if one knows p -- and raising the practical stakes raises the epistemic threshold required for knowledge.

related.name: Contextualism
related.note: Both hold that knowledge attributions are sensitive to practical context, though differently located.

related.name: The Gettier Problem
related.note: Lottery cases extend the project of refining when justified true belief constitutes knowledge.

related.name: Practical Reason
related.note: The knowledge-action principle connects epistemology directly to practical rationality.

related.name: Skepticism
related.note: High-stakes scenarios function similarly to skeptical scenarios in raising the bar for knowledge.

books.title: Knowledge and Lotteries
books.author: John Hawthorne
books.why: Hawthorne's foundational development of lottery puzzles.

books.title: Knowledge in an Uncertain World
books.author: Jeremy Fantl and Matthew McGrath
books.why: Their development of interest-relative invariantism.

books.title: Contextualism in Philosophy
books.author: Gerhard Preyer and Georg Peter (eds.)
books.why: Covers contextualism and its rivals.

completion.name: Language Games
completion.thinkerBio: Ludwig Wittgenstein (1889–1951) was an Austrian-British philosopher whose Tractatus Logico-Philosophicus (1921) and Philosophical Investigations (1953) represent two radically different philosophies; the Investigations, published posthumously, introduced language games as a therapeutic tool.
completion.quote: The meaning of a word is its use in the language.

related.name: Meaning as Use
related.note: Language games are the context within which meaning-as-use is cashed out — different games have different rules and different uses for the same words.

related.name: Hermeneutics
related.note: Both Wittgenstein and Gadamer hold that meaning is embedded in practices and forms of life rather than decontextualized mental content.

related.name: Ordinary Language Philosophy
related.note: Ordinary language philosophy develops Wittgenstein's therapeutic approach — dissolving philosophical problems by returning words to their ordinary contexts.

related.name: Deconstruction
related.note: Both Wittgenstein and Derrida challenge the assumption of unified, stable meaning — though by very different philosophical methods.

books.title: Philosophical Investigations
books.author: Ludwig Wittgenstein
books.why: The source — Wittgenstein's development of language games, meaning as use, and the private language argument.

books.title: The Blue and Brown Books
books.author: Ludwig Wittgenstein
books.why: Wittgenstein's preliminary notes toward the Investigations — more accessible and equally illuminating.

books.title: Wittgenstein
books.author: Ray Monk
books.why: The definitive biography — essential for understanding the development from the Tractatus to the Investigations.

completion.name: Laws of Nature
completion.thinkerBio: David Hume grounded laws in regularity; D.M. Armstrong (1926-2014) developed the necessitarian view treating laws as relations between universals.
completion.quote: Laws support counterfactuals and accidents don't -- but what grounds this necessity?

related.name: Causation
related.note: The debate about whether laws are Humean regularities or necessitating relations is a direct consequence of Hume's causal analysis.

related.name: The Problem of Universals
related.note: Armstrong's necessitarian laws treat them as relations between universals.

related.name: Dispositions and Powers
related.note: Dispositionalism holds laws express essential natures of properties rather than external necessitating relations.

books.title: What Is a Law of Nature?
books.author: D.M. Armstrong
books.why: Armstrong's foundational necessitarian account of laws.

books.title: An Enquiry Concerning Human Understanding
books.author: David Hume
books.why: The Humean regularity view's foundational source.

books.title: Nature's Metaphysics
books.author: Alexander Bird
books.why: The dispositionalist alternative to both regularity and necessitarian views.

completion.name: Legalism and Political Realism
completion.thinkerBio: Han Fei Zi (c. 280-233 BCE) was the most sophisticated philosophical theorist of Legalism, synthesizing earlier legalist thinkers including Shang Yang (390-338 BCE) into a systematic political philosophy of law, technique, and power.
completion.quote: The ruler who relies on the virtue of his ministers will be deceived; the ruler who relies on clear law and strict technique will be secure.

related.name: Confucian Self and Society
related.note: Legalism explicitly rejects the Confucian view that good governance depends on the moral virtue of rulers and ministers, insisting on institutional design instead.

related.name: Sovereignty
related.note: Legalist statecraft and Western theories of sovereignty both emphasize the centrality of effective, often unconstrained, state power for political stability.

related.name: Social Contract
related.note: Hobbes's social contract and Legalism converge on the need for strong centralized authority but differ on the philosophical justification -- consent versus pure efficacy.

related.name: Wu Wei
related.note: Legalism's emphasis on active, systematic institutional control directly opposes the Taoist ideal of governing through minimal interference and natural spontaneity.

books.title: Han Feizi: Basic Writings
books.author: Han Fei Zi (trans. Burton Watson)
books.why: The primary source -- Han Fei's systematic development of law, technique, and power as the foundations of statecraft.

books.title: The Book of Lord Shang
books.author: Shang Yang (trans. Yuri Pines)
books.why: The foundational earlier Legalist text developing the practical institutional reforms Han Fei later systematized philosophically.

books.title: The Art of War and Other Classics of Eastern Philosophy
books.author: various
books.why: Places Legalist political realism in the broader context of classical Chinese strategic and political thought.

completion.name: Liberalism
completion.thinkerBio: John Locke (1632–1704) grounded natural rights liberalism in self-ownership; John Stuart Mill (1806–1873) grounded it in the harm principle; John Rawls (1921–2002) constructed a systematic liberal theory from a contractualist foundation — together they define the liberal tradition.
completion.quote: The only purpose for which power can be rightfully exercised over any member of a civilised community, against his will, is to prevent harm to others.

related.name: Social Contract
related.note: Classical liberalism is built on the Lockean social contract — governments formed to protect pre-political rights can be resisted when they violate them.

related.name: Communitarianism
related.note: Communitarianism challenges liberal individualism — the self is constituted by community, not prior to it and independent of it.

related.name: Feminist Political Philosophy
related.note: Feminist critics show the public/private distinction systematically excludes domestic injustice from liberal political analysis.

related.name: Rights
related.note: Rights are the primary currency of liberal political theory — the pre-political entitlements that the state exists to protect.

books.title: Two Treatises of Government
books.author: John Locke
books.why: The foundational natural rights liberalism — the text that grounds the entire liberal tradition.

books.title: On Liberty
books.author: John Stuart Mill
books.why: Mill's harm principle and the case for individual freedom of thought and action — the clearest liberal argument.

books.title: Political Liberalism
books.author: John Rawls
books.why: Rawls's development of liberalism as a political doctrine appropriate for pluralist democratic societies.

completion.name: Logical Consequence
completion.thinkerBio: Alfred Tarski (1901-1983) developed the model-theoretic account of logical consequence; J.C. Beall and Greg Restall developed logical pluralism, arguing multiple correct accounts of logical consequence correspond to different legitimate logics.
completion.quote: An argument is valid if and only if there is no model in which the premises are true and the conclusion false.

related.name: Formal Logic
related.note: Tarski's model-theoretic account of logical consequence is foundational for the modern formal logical tradition.

related.name: The Liar's Paradox
related.note: Paraconsistent logics, which tolerate the Liar without explosion, represent alternative accounts logical pluralism takes seriously.

related.name: Vagueness and Language
related.note: Both logical pluralism and vagueness challenge the assumption that classical bivalent logic is uniquely correct.

related.name: Scientific Realism
related.note: Logical pluralism and scientific realism debates both ask whether there is a single privileged correct framework.

books.title: Logical Pluralism
books.author: J.C. Beall and Greg Restall
books.why: Beall and Restall's foundational development of logical pluralism.

books.title: Philosophy of Logic
books.author: W.V.O. Quine
books.why: Quine's classic treatment of logical truth, consequence, and the status of logic.

books.title: Truth and Other Enigmas
books.author: Michael Dummett
books.why: Dummett's essays bearing on the foundations of logical consequence and classical versus intuitionistic logic.

completion.name: Logical Positivism
completion.thinkerBio: Moritz Schlick (1882-1936) founded the Vienna Circle; Rudolf Carnap (1891-1970) developed its most systematic philosophical program, holding that only analytically true or empirically verifiable statements are cognitively meaningful.
completion.quote: Metaphysical statements are not false but meaningless -- they fail the verification criterion that separates genuine cognitive content from mere pseudo-statements.

related.name: Verificationism
related.note: Verificationism is the central doctrine of logical positivism, distinguishing meaningful scientific statements from meaningless metaphysical ones.

related.name: Analytic / Synthetic Distinction
related.note: Logical positivism relies heavily on a sharp analytic-synthetic distinction, later challenged decisively by Quine's holism.

related.name: Non-Cognitivism
related.note: Ayer used logical positivist verificationism to argue moral statements are not empirically verifiable, grounding his emotivism.

related.name: Naturalism
related.note: Logical positivism and naturalism share a commitment to empirical science as the touchstone of meaningful and justified claims.

books.title: The Logical Structure of the World
books.author: Rudolf Carnap
books.why: Carnap's systematic attempt to construct all meaningful knowledge from verifiable experiential statements.

books.title: Language, Truth and Logic
books.author: A.J. Ayer
books.why: The most accessible English-language statement of logical positivism.

books.title: The Vienna Circle
books.author: Friedrich Stadler
books.why: The definitive scholarly history of the Vienna Circle.

completion.name: Luck Egalitarianism
completion.thinkerBio: G.A. Cohen (1941-2009) and Richard Arneson (b. 1945) developed luck egalitarianism in the late 1980s; Elizabeth Anderson (b. 1959) produced the most influential critique in 'What Is the Point of Equality?' (1999).
completion.quote: Inequalities are just only if they result from genuine choices rather than from unchosen circumstances outside a person's control.

related.name: Justice and Equality
related.note: Luck egalitarianism refines Rawlsian egalitarianism -- it identifies exactly what makes inequalities unjust: they track unchosen circumstances rather than genuine choices.

related.name: Moral Luck
related.note: Moral luck and luck egalitarianism both ask how much of what happens to us is within our control and what follows for moral assessment.

related.name: Original Position
related.note: Luck egalitarianism gives a more precise reading of the veil of ignorance -- it specifies that natural talent and social background are the morally arbitrary factors to be corrected.

related.name: Cosmopolitanism and Obligations
related.note: Global luck egalitarianism extends the framework globally -- the accident of birth in a wealthy nation is brute luck that generates global redistributive obligations.

books.title: On the Currency of Egalitarian Justice
books.author: G.A. Cohen
books.why: Cohen's collected essays on luck egalitarianism.

books.title: Equality and Responsibility
books.author: Marc Fleurbaey
books.why: The most rigorous technical development of luck egalitarianism.

books.title: Equality and Partiality
books.author: Thomas Nagel
books.why: Nagel's attempt to reconcile equality with personal partiality.

completion.name: Madhyamaka Philosophy
completion.thinkerBio: Nagarjuna (c. 150-250 CE) founded the Madhyamaka school of Buddhist philosophy with his Mulamadhyamakakarika, the most rigorous philosophical development of the doctrine of emptiness in the entire Buddhist tradition.
completion.quote: All phenomena are empty of intrinsic existence -- including emptiness itself, which is not a substitute absolute but merely another conventional designation.

related.name: Dependent Origination
related.note: Nagarjuna radicalized dependent origination into a complete metaphysics of emptiness -- nothing has intrinsic existence apart from its conditions.

related.name: Anatta
related.note: Madhyamaka extends the doctrine of no-self universally -- not only persons but all phenomena lack intrinsic, independent existence.

related.name: Advaita Vedanta
related.note: Both Madhyamaka and Advaita are non-dualist traditions that challenge conventional distinctions as ultimately unreal, though they differ on whether any ultimate reality remains.

related.name: Buddhism in China
related.note: The Tiantai school's three truths doctrine represents a sophisticated Chinese philosophical development of Nagarjuna's original two-truths framework.

books.title: Nagarjuna's Middle Way
books.author: Nagarjuna (trans. Mark Siderits and Shoryu Katsura)
books.why: The definitive scholarly translation and commentary on Nagarjuna's foundational philosophical text.

books.title: Buddhism as Philosophy
books.author: Mark Siderits
books.why: A clear analytic philosophical introduction to Madhyamaka emptiness and its place in Buddhist metaphysics.

books.title: Emptiness Yoga
books.author: Jeffrey Hopkins
books.why: A detailed scholarly study of how Madhyamaka emptiness functions in Tibetan philosophical and meditative practice.

completion.name: Material Constitution
completion.thinkerBio: Judith Jarvis Thomson (1929-2020) and David Wiggins developed constitution as a relation distinct from identity, addressing the statue-clay puzzle.
completion.quote: The lump constitutes the statue without being identical to it -- they have different persistence conditions despite sharing all matter.

related.name: The Ship of Theseus
related.note: Material constitution is a version of the same puzzle about identity through change.

related.name: Mereology
related.note: Constitution puzzles directly engage mereological questions about parts and wholes.

related.name: Four-Dimensionalism
related.note: Four-dimensionalism resolves constitution puzzles by treating the statue and clay as the same four-dimensional object differently described.

books.title: Material Beings
books.author: Peter van Inwagen
books.why: Van Inwagen's restricted composition approach to constitution puzzles.

books.title: Coincident Objects
books.author: Katherine Hawley
books.why: A rigorous contemporary treatment of the statue-clay problem.

books.title: Ordinary Objects
books.author: Amie Thomasson
books.why: Thomasson's defence of ordinary constituted objects.

completion.name: Meaning and Truth Conditions
completion.thinkerBio: Donald Davidson (1917-2003) proposed truth-conditional semantics using Tarski's truth theory; Michael Dummett (1925-2011) challenged it with verification-transcendent worries.
completion.quote: To know the meaning of a sentence is to know its truth conditions -- the conditions under which it would be true.

related.name: The Nature of Truth
related.note: Truth-conditional semantics presupposes a substantive account of what truth is, connecting directly to broader truth debates.

related.name: Verificationism
related.note: Dummett's anti-realism replaces truth conditions with verification-transcendent-free assertibility conditions.

related.name: Logical Consequence
related.note: Tarski's truth theory, which grounds Davidson's semantics, is the same framework underlying model-theoretic logical consequence.

books.title: Inquiries into Truth and Interpretation
books.author: Donald Davidson
books.why: Davidson's foundational essays developing truth-conditional semantics.

books.title: Truth and Other Enigmas
books.author: Michael Dummett
books.why: Dummett's foundational challenge grounding anti-realist semantics.

books.title: The Logical Basis of Metaphysics
books.author: Michael Dummett
books.why: Dummett's fuller development of the realism-antirealism divide in semantics.

completion.name: Meaning as Use
completion.thinkerBio: Ludwig Wittgenstein (1889-1951) developed meaning as use in the Philosophical Investigations, rejecting reference-based theories of meaning.
completion.quote: The meaning of a word is its use in the language.

related.name: Language Games
related.note: Language games are the context within which meaning-as-use is cashed out.

related.name: Ordinary Language Philosophy
related.note: Ordinary language philosophy develops Wittgenstein's use-based approach to meaning.

related.name: Pragmatism
related.note: Both meaning-as-use and pragmatism reject reference-based theories in favor of practice-based ones.

books.title: Philosophical Investigations
books.author: Ludwig Wittgenstein
books.why: The foundational source for meaning as use.

books.title: Wittgenstein: A Very Short Introduction
books.author: A.C. Grayling
books.why: A clear introduction to Wittgenstein's later philosophy of language.

books.title: Wittgenstein on Rules and Private Language
books.author: Saul Kripke
books.why: Kripke's influential interpretation of Wittgenstein's meaning-as-use.

completion.name: Memory and Personal Identity
completion.thinkerBio: John Locke (1632-1704) grounded personal identity in memory; Sydney Shoemaker (b. 1931) clarified the logical structure through quasi-memory.
completion.quote: I am the same person as the child in that memory because I can remember, from the inside, being that child.

related.name: Personal Identity
related.note: Locke's memory criterion is the classical starting point for personal identity theory.

related.name: Identity Over Time
related.note: Memory is Locke's proposed criterion for identity over time, grounding it in psychological continuity.

related.name: Narrative Identity
related.note: Both memory-based and narrative accounts of identity ground personhood in psychological rather than purely biological continuity.

books.title: An Essay Concerning Human Understanding
books.author: John Locke
books.why: Locke's foundational chapter on personal identity and memory.

books.title: Personal Identity
books.author: John Perry (ed.)
books.why: The essential anthology including Shoemaker's quasi-memory analysis.

books.title: Reasons and Persons
books.author: Derek Parfit
books.why: Parfit's development and critique of memory-based personal identity.

completion.name: Mencius on Human Nature
completion.thinkerBio: Mencius (371-289 BCE) is the most influential developer of Confucian moral philosophy after Confucius himself; his doctrine that human nature is fundamentally good shaped the dominant strand of subsequent Confucian and Neo-Confucian thought.
completion.quote: Humans universally feel alarm and compassion when they see a child about to fall into a well -- this reveals the innate goodness of human nature.

related.name: Confucian Self-Cultivation
related.note: Mencius's account of innate moral goodness grounds self-cultivation as nurturing what is naturally present rather than imposing external constraint.

related.name: Confucian Li
related.note: Mencius holds that human nature is fundamentally good, requiring li only to nurture inherent virtue; Xunzi holds li is needed to constrain inherently selfish human nature.

related.name: Care Ethics
related.note: Mencius's argument from spontaneous compassion for a child in danger parallels care ethics' grounding of morality in natural responsiveness to vulnerability.

related.name: Virtue Ethics
related.note: Both Mencian moral psychology and Aristotelian virtue ethics ground moral excellence in the cultivation of natural human capacities rather than their suppression.

books.title: Mencius
books.author: trans. Bryan Van Norden
books.why: The primary source -- Mencius's foundational argument for the innate goodness of human nature.

books.title: Mencius and Early Chinese Thought
books.author: Bryan Van Norden
books.why: A rigorous scholarly study situating Mencius within the broader debate about human nature in early Chinese philosophy.

books.title: Xunzi: The Complete Text
books.author: Xunzi (trans. Eric Hutton)
books.why: The crucial opposing view -- essential for understanding the stakes of the Mencius-Xunzi debate on human nature.

completion.name: Mental Content
completion.thinkerBio: Jerry Fodor (1935-2017) and Fred Dretske (1932-2013) developed rival naturalistic theories of how mental states acquire representational content.
completion.quote: A mental symbol means F if it was selected to be activated by F -- but what explains this specifically, rather than any correlated property?

related.name: The Intentionality of Consciousness
related.note: Mental content theories attempt to naturalize the intentionality that Brentano identified as the mark of the mental.

related.name: Language of Thought
related.note: Fodor's language of thought hypothesis requires a theory of mental content to explain how mentalese symbols acquire meaning.

related.name: Semantic Externalism
related.note: Both mental content theories and semantic externalism ground meaning partly in causal-historical facts external to the individual mind.

books.title: Psychosemantics
books.author: Jerry Fodor
books.why: Fodor's foundational naturalistic theory of mental content.

books.title: Knowledge and the Flow of Information
books.author: Fred Dretske
books.why: Dretske's rival information-theoretic account of mental content.

books.title: A Theory of Content and Other Essays
books.author: Jerry Fodor
books.why: Fodor's further development addressing the disjunction problem.

completion.name: Mereological Nihilism
completion.thinkerBio: Peter van Inwagen (b. 1942) developed restricted composition; Trenton Merricks (b. 1966) and other eliminativists developed mereological nihilism, holding that composite objects do not exist -- only simples arranged in certain ways.
completion.quote: There are no tables, only simples arranged table-wise -- composite objects are a useful fiction layered over a more austere reality of fundamental particles.

related.name: Mereology
related.note: Mereological nihilism is the most radical answer to the composition question, denying that any composition relation produces genuine wholes except possibly living organisms.

related.name: The Ship of Theseus
related.note: Nihilism dissolves rather than answers the ship puzzle by denying that ships are genuine composite objects in the first place.

related.name: Naturalism
related.note: Mereological nihilism is motivated by a parsimonious, scientifically-minded naturalism that resists positing composite objects beyond fundamental physical simples.

related.name: Occam's Razor
related.note: Nihilism is the most thoroughgoing application of Occam's Razor to ordinary object ontology, eliminating composite objects as unnecessary posits.

books.title: Material Beings
books.author: Peter van Inwagen
books.why: Van Inwagen's foundational development of restricted composition, the most influential moderate alternative to full nihilism.

books.title: Objects and Persons
books.author: Trenton Merricks
books.why: Merricks's rigorous defence of eliminativism about most composite objects.

books.title: Ordinary Objects
books.author: Amie Thomasson
books.why: Thomasson's defence of ordinary objects against nihilist elimination, providing essential context for the debate.

completion.name: Mereology
completion.thinkerBio: Stanislaw Lesniewski (1886-1939) founded formal mereology in the 1910s; David Lewis (1941-2001) developed mereological universalism -- the view that any collection of objects composes a sum -- as part of his comprehensive metaphysical system.
completion.quote: Composition as identity: the parts, taken together, just are the whole.

related.name: The Ship of Theseus
related.note: The Ship of Theseus raises mereological questions about whether the identity of a whole is determined by its parts or by formal and functional structure.

related.name: Material Constitution
related.note: Material constitution puzzles directly engage mereology -- what makes the statue and the lump of clay distinct objects despite sharing all the same parts.

related.name: Identity Over Time
related.note: Mereological questions about composition bear directly on whether an object with replaced parts remains numerically identical.

related.name: Process Philosophy
related.note: Process philosophy denies that wholes are simply composed of static parts -- events and processes do not decompose mereologically in the same way as substances.

books.title: Parts of Classes
books.author: David Lewis
books.why: Lewis's systematic development of mereology and composition as identity.

books.title: Material Beings
books.author: Peter van Inwagen
books.why: Van Inwagen's restricted composition -- only living organisms compose genuine wholes -- the most influential alternative to mereological universalism.

books.title: Composition as Identity
books.author: Donald Baxter and Aaron Cotnoir (eds.)
books.why: An anthology of contemporary essays on the relationship between composition and identity.

completion.name: Metaethics: The Terrain
completion.thinkerBio: G.E. Moore (1873-1958), J.L. Mackie (1917-1981), and Simon Blackburn (b. 1944) represent the three major positions in contemporary metaethics -- realism, error theory, and expressivism respectively -- that organize the entire field's landscape.
completion.quote: Metaethics asks not what is good or right but what kind of thing 'good' and 'right' are, and how moral knowledge, if it exists, is possible.

related.name: Moral Realism
related.note: Moral realism is one of the three main positions this entry maps -- the view that moral claims express genuine beliefs that can be true and known.

related.name: Error Theory
related.note: Error theory is the second major position -- moral claims aspire to objective truth but systematically and uniformly fail to achieve it.

related.name: Expressivism
related.note: Expressivism is the third major position -- moral claims express non-cognitive attitudes rather than stating truth-apt propositions about facts.

related.name: Moral Realism vs Anti-Realism
related.note: Both entries map the same fundamental metaethical landscape from complementary organizing angles.

books.title: Principia Ethica
books.author: G.E. Moore
books.why: Moore's foundational text establishing non-naturalist realism as a distinct metaethical position.

books.title: Ethics: Inventing Right and Wrong
books.author: J.L. Mackie
