books.why: Mackie's foundational error theory, the second pole organizing the contemporary metaethical landscape.

books.title: Ethical Theory: An Anthology
books.author: Russ Shafer-Landau (ed.)
books.why: The comprehensive anthology giving the clearest overview of the full metaethical terrain across all major positions.

completion.name: Metaphor
completion.thinkerBio: Aristotle (384-322 BCE) treated metaphor as ornamental; George Lakoff (b. 1941) and Mark Johnson (b. 1949) argued metaphor is conceptually fundamental in Metaphors We Live By (1980).
completion.quote: Our ordinary conceptual system is fundamentally metaphorical -- we understand abstract domains through concrete bodily experience.

related.name: Embodied Cognition
related.note: Conceptual metaphor theory grounds abstract thought in sensorimotor bodily experience, connecting to embodied cognition.

related.name: Relevance and Communication
related.note: Relevance theory explains metaphor as loose language achieving optimal relevance rather than a special rhetorical device.

related.name: Pragmatics
related.note: Grice's framework was extended to explain metaphor as a deliberate maxim violation resolved through pragmatic inference.

books.title: Metaphors We Live By
books.author: George Lakoff and Mark Johnson
books.why: The foundational text establishing conceptual metaphor theory.

books.title: Poetics
books.author: Aristotle
books.why: Aristotle's classical treatment of metaphor as rhetorical ornament.

books.title: Philosophy in the Flesh
books.author: Lakoff and Johnson
books.why: The fuller extension of embodied metaphor to all abstract philosophical thought.

completion.name: Mimamsa and Vedic Authority
completion.thinkerBio: Jaimini (dates uncertain, possibly second century BCE) is the traditional founder of Mimamsa; Prabhakara Mishra (active c. seventh century CE) developed an influential theory of testimony and linguistic meaning within the school.
completion.quote: The Vedas are eternal, self-subsisting sounds whose authority does not depend on any author and cannot be undermined by questioning an author's reliability.

related.name: Testimony
related.note: Mimamsa's theory of self-validating testimony anticipates and parallels contemporary anti-reductionist accounts of testimonial knowledge as a basic epistemic source.

related.name: Nyaya Logic
related.note: Both Mimamsa and Nyaya developed sophisticated theories of valid knowledge sources (pramanas) within the broader classical Indian philosophical tradition.

related.name: Hermeneutics
related.note: Mimamsa's sophisticated hermeneutical principles for interpreting the Vedas anticipate concerns later developed independently in Western hermeneutic philosophy.

related.name: Frege's Sense and Reference
related.note: Mimamsa's theories of linguistic meaning and the connection between words and referents independently anticipate concerns central to Fregean philosophy of language.

books.title: The Mimamsa Nyaya Prakasa
books.author: Apadeva (trans. Franklin Edgerton)
books.why: A classical introductory text to Mimamsa hermeneutical and epistemological principles.

books.title: A History of Indian Philosophy Vol. 1
books.author: Surendranath Dasgupta
books.why: The definitive scholarly history covering Mimamsa philosophy in relation to other Indian philosophical schools.

books.title: Word and the World
books.author: Bimal Krishna Matilal
books.why: Matilal's rigorous philosophical analysis of Indian theories of language and meaning, including Mimamsa contributions.

completion.name: Mimesis
completion.thinkerBio: Plato (428–348 BCE) attacked mimesis in the Republic as epistemically and morally dangerous; Aristotle (384–322 BCE) defended it in the Poetics as philosophically valuable — the imitation of universal human patterns rather than mere copying of particulars.
completion.quote: The object of mimesis is not what has happened, but what might happen according to necessity or probability.

related.name: Katharsis
related.note: Katharsis is the effect that mimesis in tragedy achieves — it justifies artistic representation against Plato's attack.

related.name: Art and Morality
related.note: The debate about art and morality is partly a debate about mimesis — whether representing evil inflames or educates.

related.name: Narrative and Fiction
related.note: Fictional narrative is the most developed form of mimesis — the representation of possible human actions in temporal sequence.

related.name: Art as Expression
related.note: Expression theory locates art's value in externalizing inner experience; mimesis locates it in representing external reality.

books.title: Poetics
books.author: Aristotle (trans. Malcolm Heath)
books.why: The primary source — Aristotle's account of mimesis in tragedy as philosophically more valuable than history.

books.title: Mimesis
books.author: Erich Auerbach
books.why: The classic study of mimesis across Western literature from Homer to Woolf.

books.title: Plato's Republic
books.author: Plato (trans. C.D.C. Reeve)
books.why: Contains Plato's attack on mimesis — the foundational critique that Aristotle's Poetics answered.

completion.name: Modality and Necessity
completion.thinkerBio: Saul Kripke (1940-2022) transformed modal metaphysics through possible worlds semantics; David Lewis (1941-2001) developed the most systematic account of necessity and possibility through modal realism.
completion.quote: Necessity is truth in all possible worlds; possibility is truth in at least one possible world.

related.name: Possible Worlds
related.note: Possible worlds provide the formal semantic framework within which necessity and possibility are analyzed.

related.name: Essentialism
related.note: Kripke used modal analysis to rehabilitate essentialism -- essential properties hold in all possible worlds.

related.name: A Priori / A Posteriori
related.note: Kripke's necessary a posteriori truths dissolve the traditional equation of necessity with a priority.

books.title: Naming and Necessity
books.author: Saul Kripke
books.why: Kripke's transformative lectures on modality, necessity, and reference.

books.title: On the Plurality of Worlds
books.author: David Lewis
books.why: Lewis's systematic modal realist account of necessity and possibility.

books.title: Modality
books.author: Joseph Melia
books.why: A clear introductory survey of modal metaphysics and its debates.

completion.name: Models and Simulations
completion.thinkerBio: Mary Morgan (b. 1952) and Margaret Morrison developed the view of models as autonomous mediators between theory and phenomena; Eric Winsberg (b. 1968) examined the distinctive epistemic status of knowledge generated by computer simulations.
completion.quote: Models are autonomous agents -- they function as mediators between theory and phenomena rather than simply applying theory to observation.

related.name: Scientific Explanation
related.note: Models often serve explanatory functions distinct from direct deductive-nomological subsumption, mediating between abstract theory and concrete phenomena.

related.name: Theory-Ladenness of Observation
related.note: Both model-based reasoning and theory-ladenness examine how prior theoretical commitments shape what scientists can observe and represent.

related.name: Inference to Best Explanation
related.note: Evaluating which model best explains a phenomenon is itself an exercise in inference to the best explanation, applied to the choice between competing models.

related.name: Underdetermination
related.note: Multiple models can often fit the same data equally well, making model choice another domain in which theory choice is underdetermined by evidence alone.

books.title: Models as Mediators
books.author: Mary Morgan and Margaret Morrison (eds.)
books.why: The foundational anthology developing the view of models as autonomous mediators between theory and phenomena.

books.title: Science in the Age of Computer Simulation
books.author: Eric Winsberg
books.why: Winsberg's foundational examination of the distinctive epistemology of computer simulation in contemporary science.

books.title: Models and Mathematics in Science
books.author: Sorin Bangu (ed.)
books.why: A contemporary anthology examining the philosophical role of mathematical and computational modeling in science.

completion.name: Mohism and Universal Love
completion.thinkerBio: Mozi (Mo Di, c. 470-391 BCE) founded Mohism as the most systematic philosophical alternative to Confucianism in classical China, developing both ethical doctrine and a sophisticated logical-epistemological school known as Later Mohism.
completion.quote: If people loved one another universally, states would not attack one another and individuals would not injure each other.

related.name: Confucian Ren
related.note: Mozi argued against the Confucian emphasis on differential care grounded in role relationships in favor of strict impartial universal love.

related.name: Utilitarianism
related.note: Mozi's consequentialist argument for universal love -- that the world would be better off if everyone cared for all people equally -- has deep structural affinities with utilitarian reasoning.

related.name: Care Ethics
related.note: Care ethics insists on the moral legitimacy and necessity of partiality toward particular relationships, directly opposing Mozi's demand for strict impartiality.

related.name: Cosmopolitanism and Obligations
related.note: Mozi's universal love and contemporary cosmopolitanism both reject the moral relevance of particular relationships and national boundaries in grounding obligation.

books.title: Mozi: A Study and Translation of the Ethical and Political Writings
books.author: trans. Ian Johnston
books.why: The most comprehensive scholarly translation of Mozi's foundational philosophical writings.

books.title: The Essential Mozi
books.author: Chris Fraser
books.why: An accessible introduction to Mohist philosophy, logic, and political theory in its classical context.

books.title: The Ethical and Political Works of Motse
books.author: Yi-Pao Mei (trans.)
books.why: An earlier classic translation that remains valuable for the breadth of Mohist texts it covers.

completion.name: Moksha
completion.thinkerBio: Moksha is discussed across all major schools of Hindu philosophy; the Bhagavad Gita synthesizes the major paths (jnana, bhakti, karma yoga) toward it, while Patanjali's Yoga Sutras provide the most systematic practical methodology.
completion.quote: Liberation is not the acquisition of something new but the removal of the ignorance that obscures what was already true.

related.name: Brahman and Atman
related.note: Moksha consists in the direct realization of the identity of Atman and Brahman, dissolving the ignorance that generates the illusion of separate selfhood.

related.name: Karma
related.note: Moksha is the cessation of the conditions -- accumulated karma driven by ignorance and desire -- that perpetuate the cycle of rebirth.

related.name: Yoga Philosophy
related.note: Patanjali's eightfold yogic path is the systematic practical methodology for achieving moksha through the stilling of mental fluctuations.

related.name: Nirvana
related.note: Both moksha and Buddhist nirvana name a state of liberation from suffering and the cycle of rebirth, though they differ in their underlying metaphysics of self.

books.title: The Bhagavad Gita
books.author: trans. Barbara Stoler Miller
books.why: The Gita's synthesis of the major paths to moksha -- knowledge, devotion, and selfless action.

books.title: The Yoga Sutras of Patanjali
books.author: trans. Edwin Bryant
books.why: The systematic practical methodology for achieving moksha through yogic discipline.

books.title: Classical Indian Philosophy
books.author: Bina Gupta
books.why: A comprehensive scholarly survey of moksha across the major schools of Indian philosophy.

completion.name: Moral Epistemology
completion.thinkerBio: Robert Audi (b. 1941) defends a pluralistic intuitionism grounding moral knowledge in rational intuition; David Enoch (b. 1973) developed robust realism, using a 'companions in guilt' strategy comparing moral knowledge to mathematical knowledge.
completion.quote: Moral knowledge is no worse off epistemically than mathematical knowledge -- both involve grasping necessary truths that resist straightforward empirical verification.

related.name: Moral Intuitionism
related.note: Audi's pluralistic intuitionism is the contemporary development of the moral epistemology that Moore and Ross's intuitionism originally established.

related.name: Moral Realism
related.note: Enoch's robust realism requires a corresponding moral epistemology explaining how we can have reliable knowledge of the objective moral facts realism posits.

related.name: A Priori / A Posteriori
related.note: The companions in guilt strategy draws a direct parallel between moral knowledge and mathematical a priori knowledge, both resistant to straightforward empirical verification.

related.name: Moral Uncertainty
related.note: Moral epistemology asks how we can know moral truths at all; moral uncertainty assumes we often cannot achieve confident knowledge and asks how we should act regardless.

books.title: The Good in the Right
books.author: Robert Audi
books.why: Audi's systematic development of pluralistic intuitionism as a contemporary moral epistemology.

books.title: Taking Morality Seriously
books.author: David Enoch
books.why: Enoch's foundational development of robust moral realism and the companions in guilt epistemological strategy.

books.title: Moral Knowledge: New Readings in Moral Epistemology
books.author: Sarah McGrath and Aaron Zimmerman (eds.)
books.why: A comprehensive contemporary anthology covering the major positions in moral epistemology.

completion.name: Moral Intuitionism
completion.thinkerBio: G.E. Moore (1873-1958) defended non-naturalist intuitionism; W.D. Ross (1877-1971) developed prima facie duties in The Right and the Good (1930).
completion.quote: The rightness of an action is self-evident to careful reflection.

related.name: Moral Realism
related.note: Intuitionism is one species of moral realism grounded in rational intuition.

related.name: Moral Epistemology
related.note: Intuitionism is the epistemological claim that some moral truths are known non-inferentially.

related.name: Moral Particularism
related.note: Intuitionism grounds ethics in self-evident principles; particularism denies principles capture moral relevance.

books.title: Principia Ethica
books.author: G.E. Moore
books.why: Moore's foundational non-naturalist intuitionism.

books.title: The Right and the Good
books.author: W.D. Ross
books.why: Ross's prima facie duties, the most systematic intuitionism.

books.title: Ethics: Fundamentals
books.author: Russ Shafer-Landau
books.why: Clear situating of intuitionism among its competitors.

completion.name: Moral Luck
completion.thinkerBio: Thomas Nagel (b. 1937) at NYU and Bernard Williams (1929–2003) jointly introduced the concept in a 1976 symposium; their contrasting approaches — Nagel more systematic, Williams more skeptical of moral theory — define the two main positions in the debate.
completion.quote: The truck driver who accidentally kills a child is not merely unlucky — his life is changed, and we judge him differently.

related.name: Free Will vs Determinism
related.note: Moral luck presses the same basic tension — if outcomes are not fully under our control, the basis for differential moral assessment is threatened.

related.name: Consequentialism and Its Limits
related.note: Williams's integrity argument and his work on moral luck are both critiques of how consequentialism handles the agent's relationship to their own actions.

related.name: Punishment
related.note: Moral luck directly challenges retributive punishment: if desert depends on outcomes partly outside one's control, what grounds differential sentencing?

related.name: Free Will and Moral Responsibility
related.note: Moral luck and the problem of free will both undermine the metaphysical basis for the reactive attitudes of blame and praise.

books.title: Mortal Questions
books.author: Thomas Nagel
books.why: Contains Nagel's foundational essay on moral luck alongside classic papers on consciousness and the meaning of life.

books.title: Moral Luck
books.author: Daniel Statman (ed.)
books.why: The essential anthology collecting key papers including Williams and Nagel's original contributions.

books.title: Ethics and the Limits of Philosophy
books.author: Bernard Williams
books.why: Williams's full critique of moral theory — moral luck is central to his case against systematic ethics.

completion.name: Moral Motivation
completion.thinkerBio: David Hume (1711–1776) argued that reason alone cannot motivate and all motivation comes from passion, in A Treatise of Human Nature (1739); Immanuel Kant (1724–1804) argued that genuine moral worth requires acting from duty through reason alone — their disagreement defines the modern debate in moral psychology.
completion.quote: Reason is, and ought only to be, the slave of the passions.

related.name: Categorical Imperative
related.note: Kant holds that genuine moral worth requires acting from duty through reason alone, not from inclination or sympathy.

related.name: Care Ethics
related.note: Care ethics aligns with Hume in treating emotional responsiveness to others as central to moral motivation rather than an obstacle.

related.name: Moral Psychology
related.note: Contemporary moral psychology engages directly with the Hume-Kant debate through dual-process accounts of how intuition and reasoning interact.

related.name: Virtue Ethics
related.note: Virtue ethics resolves the Hume-Kant tension by making good character the integration of reason and appropriate emotional response.

books.title: A Treatise of Human Nature
books.author: David Hume
books.why: Book III contains Hume's argument that reason alone cannot motivate and that morality is grounded in sentiment.

books.title: Groundwork of the Metaphysics of Morals
books.author: Immanuel Kant
books.why: Kant's counter-argument that genuine moral worth requires acting from duty through reason, not from inclination.

books.title: Morality: An Introduction to Ethics
books.author: Bernard Williams
books.why: Williams's account of moral motivation and the limits of both Humean and Kantian frameworks.

completion.name: Moral Particularism
completion.thinkerBio: Jonathan Dancy (b. 1946) developed moral particularism in Moral Reasons (1993) and Ethics Without Principles (2004).
completion.quote: The very same feature that counts in favour of an action in one case can count against it in another.

related.name: Virtue Ethics
related.note: Practical wisdom is the virtue basis for particularist moral perception.

related.name: Categorical Imperative
related.note: Kant's universalizability is the paradigmatic generalist principle particularism denies.

related.name: Moral Psychology
related.note: Haidt's intuitionism supports particularist holistic moral judgment.

books.title: Ethics Without Principles
books.author: Jonathan Dancy
books.why: Dancy's systematic case against moral principles.

books.title: Moral Reasons
books.author: Jonathan Dancy
books.why: The earlier, clearer statement of the particularist challenge.

books.title: Moral Particularism
books.author: Brad Hooker and Margaret Little (eds.)
books.why: Essential responses from both sides of the debate.

completion.name: Moral Progress
completion.thinkerBio: Richard Rorty (1931-2007) offered a neo-pragmatist account of moral progress as expanding sympathy; Peter Singer developed the expanding circle argument.
completion.quote: The arc of the moral universe is long, but it bends toward justice.

related.name: Moral Realism
related.note: Realism accounts for progress as getting closer to moral truth.

related.name: Nihilism
related.note: Moral progress requires some standard against which change counts as improvement.

related.name: Ubuntu as Political Philosophy
related.note: South Africa's TRC embodies moral progress through restorative justice.

books.title: The Better Angels of Our Nature
books.author: Steven Pinker
books.why: Pinker's empirical argument for long-term moral progress.

books.title: The Expanding Circle
books.author: Peter Singer
books.why: Singer's philosophical argument for the progressive expansion of moral concern.

books.title: Contingency, Irony, and Solidarity
books.author: Richard Rorty
books.why: Rorty's neo-pragmatist account of progress as expanding sympathy.

completion.name: Moral Psychology
completion.thinkerBio: Jonathan Haidt (b. 1963) developed the social intuitionist model; Joshua Greene (b. 1974) developed the dual-process account of moral judgment.
completion.quote: Moral intuitions arise quickly and automatically, and moral reasoning usually begins after the verdict is already in.

related.name: Moral Particularism
related.note: Haidt's intuitionism supports particularism -- moral judgment is holistic, not principle-driven.

related.name: The Trolley Problem
related.note: The trolley problem is the primary instrument in moral psychology experiments.

related.name: Moral Motivation
related.note: Haidt's intuitionism supports Hume over Kant on the sources of moral motivation.

books.title: The Righteous Mind
books.author: Jonathan Haidt
books.why: Haidt's influential social intuitionist model.

books.title: Moral Tribes
books.author: Joshua Greene
books.why: Greene's dual-process account of moral cognition.

books.title: Gut Feelings
books.author: Gerd Gigerenzer
books.why: A defence of intuition as ecological rationality, relevant context.

completion.name: Moral Realism
completion.thinkerBio: Derek Parfit (1942-2017) defended moral realism in On What Matters; Russ Shafer-Landau (b. 1963) wrote the most systematic contemporary non-naturalist defence.
completion.quote: Some things really are better or worse for people, regardless of what anyone believes or feels.

related.name: Moral Relativism
related.note: Realism holds moral facts are objective and universal; relativism denies mind-independent moral truth.

related.name: Error Theory
related.note: Error theory accepts realist semantics but denies any moral claims are true.

related.name: Moral Intuitionism
related.note: Intuitionism is a species of realism holding moral truths are known via rational intuition.

books.title: On What Matters (Vols 1 & 2)
books.author: Derek Parfit
books.why: Parfit's late defence of convergent moral realism across ethical traditions.

books.title: Moral Realism: A Defence
books.author: Russ Shafer-Landau
books.why: The most rigorous contemporary non-naturalist realist argument.

books.title: Principia Ethica
books.author: G.E. Moore
books.why: The foundational non-naturalist realist text.

completion.name: Moral Realism vs Anti-Realism
completion.thinkerBio: Russ Shafer-Landau (b. 1963) at Wisconsin-Madison wrote the most systematic contemporary defence of moral realism; Simon Blackburn (b. 1944) represents the most sophisticated anti-realist alternative through quasi-realism.
completion.quote: The debate between moral realism and anti-realism is the central organizing question of contemporary metaethics.

related.name: Moral Realism
related.note: This entry surveys the full landscape of which moral realism is one pole.

related.name: Expressivism
related.note: Expressivism is the leading anti-realist alternative, holding moral claims express attitudes rather than state facts.

related.name: Error Theory
related.note: Error theory is the other major anti-realist position, holding moral claims aspire to truth but uniformly fail.

related.name: Metaethics: The Terrain
related.note: Both entries map the same metaethical landscape from complementary angles.

books.title: Moral Realism: A Defence
books.author: Russ Shafer-Landau
books.why: The most comprehensive contemporary defence of non-naturalist moral realism.

books.title: Spreading the Word
books.author: Simon Blackburn
books.why: The leading anti-realist alternative -- quasi-realism's attempt to earn moral objectivity without realist metaphysics.

books.title: Ethical Theory: An Anthology
books.author: Russ Shafer-Landau (ed.)
books.why: Comprehensive anthology covering both sides of the realism debate alongside normative ethics.

completion.name: Moral Relativism
completion.thinkerBio: Moral relativism draws on anthropologists like Ruth Benedict (1887–1948) and philosophers including Gilbert Harman (1938–2021); its modern philosophical articulation owes most to Harman's work, though the empirical motivation comes from cross-cultural anthropology.
completion.quote: What is right in one culture may be wrong in another, and neither verdict is objectively correct.

related.name: Moral Realism
related.note: Moral realism holds moral facts are objective and universal; relativism denies any mind-independent moral truth.

related.name: Error Theory
related.note: Both deny objective moral truth; error theory says moral claims are false while relativism says they are true relative to a framework.

related.name: Standpoint Epistemology
related.note: Standpoint theory accepts that social position shapes moral knowledge, though it stops short of full relativism.

related.name: Multiculturalism
related.note: Multicultural politics draws on relativist intuitions about cultural legitimacy while typically resisting the full relativist conclusion.

books.title: Ethics: Inventing Right and Wrong
books.author: J.L. Mackie
books.why: Mackie's error theory is the most philosophically disciplined anti-realist position, engaging seriously with both relativism and objectivism.

books.title: Morality: An Introduction to Ethics
books.author: Bernard Williams
books.why: Williams challenges both naive relativism and naive objectivism with characteristic philosophical precision.

books.title: Patterns of Culture
books.author: Ruth Benedict
books.why: The classic anthropological case for cultural relativism — still the most readable empirical motivation for the view.

completion.name: Moral Uncertainty
completion.thinkerBio: William MacAskill (b. 1987) and Toby Ord developed systematic approaches to acting under moral uncertainty through the effective altruism movement.
completion.quote: Moral uncertainty -- not knowing which moral theory is correct -- is the normal condition of careful moral reasoners.

related.name: Bayesian Epistemology
related.note: Acting under moral uncertainty parallels Bayesian reasoning under empirical uncertainty.

related.name: Moral Realism
related.note: Moral uncertainty presupposes there is a truth to be uncertain about.

related.name: Population Ethics
related.note: Population ethics is a domain of deep, largely irresolvable moral uncertainty.

books.title: Moral Uncertainty
books.author: MacAskill, Bykvist, Ord
books.why: The first systematic treatment of decision-making under moral uncertainty.

books.title: The Precipice
books.author: Toby Ord
books.why: Applies moral uncertainty to existential risk.

books.title: Doing Good Better
books.author: William MacAskill
books.why: Accessible introduction to reasoning under moral and empirical uncertainty.

completion.name: Multiculturalism
completion.thinkerBio: Charles Taylor (b. 1931) at McGill developed the philosophy of recognition underlying multiculturalism in 'The Politics of Recognition' (1992); Will Kymlicka (b. 1962) developed the most systematic liberal theory of minority rights.
completion.quote: The withholding of recognition can be a form of oppression -- our identity is partly shaped by recognition or its absence, from others.

related.name: Recognition and Misrecognition
related.note: Taylor's politics of recognition is the philosophical foundation for multiculturalism -- misrecognition by majority institutions damages minority self-understanding.

related.name: Liberalism
related.note: Kymlicka's liberal multiculturalism argues that minority rights are compatible with, and required by, core liberal commitments to individual autonomy.

related.name: Negritude
related.note: Both multiculturalism and Negritude assert the value of marginalized cultural identities against assimilationist pressure from dominant frameworks.

related.name: Decolonizing Knowledge
related.note: Both multiculturalism and decolonizing knowledge projects challenge the universalization of majority cultural and epistemic frameworks as neutral defaults.

books.title: Multiculturalism: Examining the Politics of Recognition
books.author: Charles Taylor et al.
books.why: Taylor's foundational essay on recognition alongside critical responses from leading political philosophers.

books.title: Multicultural Citizenship
books.author: Will Kymlicka
books.why: Kymlicka's systematic liberal theory of minority rights and group-differentiated citizenship.

books.title: Strange Multiplicity
books.author: James Tully
books.why: Tully's account of constitutionalism that accommodates rather than suppresses cultural diversity.

completion.name: Multiple Realizability
completion.thinkerBio: Hilary Putnam (1926-2016) introduced multiple realizability in the late 1960s as the central argument for functionalism, showing that mental states can be realized by radically different physical substrates across different organisms.
completion.quote: The same mental state can be realized in different physical substrates -- human, octopus, or in principle silicon.

related.name: Functionalism
related.note: Multiple realizability is the central argument for functionalism -- if mental states are realized differently across substrates, they must be defined by function, not physical composition.

related.name: Substance Dualism
related.note: Multiple realizability was developed partly against type-identity theory, a physicalist alternative to dualism that multiple realizability also challenges.

related.name: Reductionism
related.note: Multiple realizability is the central argument against strict psychophysical reduction -- special sciences cannot be reduced if their kinds are multiply realized.

related.name: Philosophy of Biology
related.note: Multiple realizability in philosophy of mind has structural parallels in philosophy of biology's account of convergent evolution producing similar functions through different mechanisms.

books.title: Mind, Language and Reality
books.author: Hilary Putnam
books.why: Putnam's original papers developing multiple realizability and functionalism.

books.title: Philosophy of Mind
books.author: Jaegwon Kim
books.why: The clearest textbook treatment of multiple realizability and its implications for reductionism.

books.title: Representation and Reality
books.author: Hilary Putnam
books.why: Putnam's later reconsideration of the functionalist conclusions he drew from multiple realizability.

completion.name: Names and Descriptions
completion.thinkerBio: Bertrand Russell (1872-1970) developed the description theory of names in 'On Denoting' (1905); Saul Kripke (b. 1940) challenged it decisively with the direct reference theory in Naming and Necessity (1972).
completion.quote: If 'Aristotle' means 'the teacher of Alexander,' then 'Aristotle taught Alexander' is necessarily true -- which seems clearly wrong.

related.name: Frege's Sense and Reference
related.note: Russell's description theory and Frege's sense-reference distinction both attempt to explain how names can be informative.

related.name: Reference and Rigid Designation
related.note: Kripke's rigid designation theory is the direct challenge to Russell's descriptivism.

related.name: Essentialism
related.note: Kripke's direct reference theory is the foundation for his broader revival of essentialism.

related.name: Semantic Externalism
related.note: Kripke's causal-historical account and Putnam's externalism both ground reference in facts external to descriptive knowledge.

books.title: Logic and Knowledge
books.author: Bertrand Russell
books.why: Contains Russell's foundational paper 'On Denoting' developing the description theory of names.

books.title: Naming and Necessity
books.author: Saul Kripke
books.why: Kripke's decisive challenge to descriptivism through direct reference and rigid designation.

books.title: Reference and Existence
books.author: Saul Kripke
books.why: Kripke's later treatment extending his theory to fictional and non-existent entities.

completion.name: Narrative Identity
completion.thinkerBio: Paul Ricoeur (1913-2005) developed narrative identity through his hermeneutic philosophy in Oneself as Another (1990); Alasdair MacIntyre (b. 1929) connected narrative identity to virtue ethics and the intelligibility of moral action.
completion.quote: A self-constituting narrative organizes memories, projects, values, and relationships into a coherent whole -- the self is both author and character of its own story.

related.name: Hermeneutics
related.note: Ricoeur's narrative hermeneutics provides the framework for narrative identity -- understanding the self through interpretation of one's own life story.

related.name: Personal Identity
related.note: Narrative identity theory responds to Parfit by arguing that even if metaphysical identity is indeterminate, narrative self-constitution provides what matters for personhood.

related.name: Virtue Ethics
related.note: MacIntyre connects narrative identity to virtue ethics -- moral action is intelligible only as part of an ongoing narrative embedded in a tradition.

related.name: Narrative and Self
related.note: Both entries address narrative identity from complementary angles -- one through hermeneutics and virtue ethics, the other through analytic philosophy of language.

books.title: Oneself as Another
books.author: Paul Ricoeur
books.why: Ricoeur's foundational hermeneutic development of narrative identity.

books.title: After Virtue
books.author: Alasdair MacIntyre
books.why: MacIntyre's connection of narrative identity to the intelligibility of virtuous action within tradition.

books.title: The Constitution of Selves
books.author: Marya Schechtman
books.why: Schechtman's analytic philosophical development of narrative self-constitution responding to Parfit.

completion.name: Narrative and Self
completion.thinkerBio: Marya Schechtman (b. 1960) developed the narrative self-constitution view in The Constitution of Selves (1996); Galen Strawson (b. 1952) challenged universal narrativity by defending the legitimacy of an episodic, non-narrative mode of selfhood.
completion.quote: A self-constituting narrative is a lived, first-personal interpretation organizing memories, projects, values, and relationships into a coherent whole.

related.name: Narrative Identity
related.note: This entry connects narrative self-constitution specifically to the philosophy of language's concern with how narrative as a linguistic form constitutes meaning.

related.name: Personal Identity
related.note: Schechtman's narrative view responds directly to Parfit by arguing that even if metaphysical identity is indeterminate, narrative self-constitution provides what matters for personhood.

related.name: Hermeneutics
related.note: Ricoeur's hermeneutic account of narrative identity is the philosophical predecessor that Schechtman's analytic development of narrative self-constitution builds upon.

related.name: Anatta
related.note: Strawson's defence of episodic, non-narrative selfhood challenges the assumption, shared by some narrative theorists, that a unified self-narrative is necessary for genuine personhood.

books.title: The Constitution of Selves
books.author: Marya Schechtman
books.why: Schechtman's foundational development of the narrative self-constitution view as a response to Parfit's challenge to personal identity.

books.title: Against Narrativity
books.author: Galen Strawson, in Ratio
books.why: Strawson's influential challenge to universal narrativity, defending episodic selfhood as a legitimate alternative.

books.title: Oneself as Another
books.author: Paul Ricoeur
books.why: Ricoeur's foundational hermeneutic treatment of narrative identity that informs subsequent analytic developments.

completion.name: Nationalism
completion.thinkerBio: Benedict Anderson (1936-2015) analyzed nations as imagined communities in his influential 1983 study; David Miller (b. 1946) at Oxford developed the most rigorous contemporary philosophical defence of liberal nationalism.
completion.quote: The nation is an imagined political community -- imagined as both inherently limited and sovereign.

related.name: Cosmopolitanism and Obligations
related.note: Nationalism holds that special obligations exist between co-nationals; cosmopolitanism denies that national membership is morally relevant to obligations.

related.name: Communitarianism
related.note: Liberal nationalism shares with communitarianism the view that membership in particular communities is constitutive of identity and generates special obligations.

related.name: Pan-Africanism
related.note: Pan-Africanism's transnational solidarity across the African diaspora challenges the bounded territorial conception of nationhood central to standard nationalism.

related.name: Self-Determination
related.note: Nationalism grounds claims to national self-determination, a principle that has shaped decolonization and international law throughout the twentieth century.

books.title: Imagined Communities
books.author: Benedict Anderson
books.why: The foundational analysis of nations as socially constructed imagined communities.

books.title: On Nationality
books.author: David Miller
books.why: The most rigorous contemporary liberal philosophical defence of nationalism and national obligations.

books.title: Nations and Nationalism
books.author: Ernest Gellner
books.why: Gellner's influential sociological and historical account of nationalism's modern origins.

completion.name: Naturalism
completion.thinkerBio: W.V.O. Quine (1908-2000) was the most influential proponent of philosophical naturalism, arguing that philosophy should be continuous with natural science rather than serving as its a priori foundation.
completion.quote: Philosophy of science is philosophy enough -- epistemology should be pursued as a chapter of empirical psychology, not as first philosophy prior to science.

related.name: Naturalized Epistemology
related.note: Quine's naturalized epistemology is the most influential application of naturalism specifically to questions about knowledge and justification.

related.name: Underdetermination
related.note: Quine's holism about belief, central to his attack on the analytic-synthetic distinction, is the philosophical foundation of his broader naturalism.

related.name: Grounding and Metaphysical Dependence
related.note: Naturalism is often expressed as the metaphysical claim that all facts are ultimately grounded in physical facts studied by natural science.

related.name: Functionalism
related.note: Functionalism in philosophy of mind is typically motivated by naturalistic commitments -- mental states must be explicable without positing non-physical substances.

books.title: Word and Object
books.author: W.V.O. Quine
books.why: Quine's systematic development of naturalism and its implications for language, reference, and ontology.

books.title: Naturalism in Question
books.author: Mario De Caro and David Macarthur (eds.)
books.why: An anthology examining the varieties and limits of contemporary philosophical naturalism.

books.title: From Stimulus to Science
books.author: W.V.O. Quine
books.why: Quine's later accessible essays summarizing his naturalistic philosophy across logic, language, and epistemology.

completion.name: Naturalized Epistemology
completion.thinkerBio: W.V.O. Quine (1908-2000) proposed replacing a priori epistemology with empirical cognitive psychology in 'Epistemology Naturalized' (1969).
completion.quote: Epistemology should be pursued as a chapter of empirical psychology, not as first philosophy prior to science.

related.name: Naturalism
related.note: Naturalized epistemology is the most influential application of naturalism to questions about knowledge.

related.name: Underdetermination
related.note: Quine's holism about belief is the philosophical foundation of his naturalized epistemology.

related.name: Reliabilism
related.note: Both ground epistemic norms in facts about cognitive processes studied empirically.

books.title: Ontological Relativity and Other Essays
books.author: W.V.O. Quine
books.why: Contains 'Epistemology Naturalized,' the foundational essay.

books.title: Naturalizing Epistemology
books.author: Hilary Kornblith (ed.)
books.why: The essential anthology on naturalized epistemology.

books.title: Word and Object
books.author: W.V.O. Quine
books.why: Quine's broader naturalistic philosophical project.

completion.name: Nausea and Contingency
completion.thinkerBio: Jean-Paul Sartre (1905-1980) developed nausea as a fundamental philosophical mood in his novel Nausea (1938) and in Being and Nothingness.
completion.quote: The world of explanations and reasons is not the world of existence -- things simply are, without justification.

related.name: Existence Precedes Essence
related.note: Nausea reveals the radical contingency that grounds Sartre's claim that existence precedes essence.

related.name: The Absurd
related.note: Nausea and the absurd both name confrontations with a universe that offers no inherent justification.

related.name: Facticity
related.note: Nausea is the mood in which brute facticity is felt rather than merely intellectually acknowledged.

books.title: Nausea
books.author: Jean-Paul Sartre
books.why: Sartre's novel developing nausea as the experience of radical contingency.

books.title: Being and Nothingness
books.author: Jean-Paul Sartre
books.why: The philosophical framework within which nausea is theoretically grounded.

books.title: Sartre: A Philosophical Biography
books.author: Thomas Flynn
books.why: Context connecting the novel to Sartre's philosophical development.

completion.name: Negative Dialectics
completion.thinkerBio: Theodor Adorno (1903-1969) developed negative dialectics in his 1966 book of the same name, attempting a form of dialectical thinking that resists the drive toward Hegelian synthesis in favor of preserving irreducible non-identity.
completion.quote: After Auschwitz, philosophy can no longer proceed as if its task were to reconcile subject and object -- it must bear witness to what cannot be reconciled.

related.name: Dialectics
related.note: Negative dialectics retains the dialectical method while explicitly refusing Hegel's drive toward positive synthesis, insisting contradiction be preserved rather than resolved.

related.name: Dialectics of Enlightenment
related.note: Negative Dialectics develops the methodological implications of the diagnosis Adorno offered jointly with Horkheimer in their earlier collaborative work.

related.name: Critical Theory
related.note: Negative dialectics represents the most philosophically demanding and pessimistic development within the broader critical theory tradition.

related.name: The Problem of Universals
related.note: Adorno's commitment to the non-identical -- what exceeds conceptual capture -- challenges any philosophy that assumes concepts can fully and adequately grasp particular things.

books.title: Negative Dialectics
books.author: Theodor Adorno
books.why: Adorno's most demanding and systematic philosophical work, developing the method of negative dialectics in full.

books.title: Aesthetic Theory
books.author: Theodor Adorno
books.why: Adorno's late aesthetic theory, applying negative dialectical method to art's relation to non-identity and social critique.

books.title: Adorno: A Critical Introduction
books.author: Simon Jarvis
books.why: A clear and rigorous philosophical introduction to Adorno's notoriously difficult negative dialectical method.

completion.name: Negritude
completion.thinkerBio: Aimé Césaire (1913–2008) was a Martinican poet and politician; Léopold Sédar Senghor (1906–2001) was a Senegalese poet and the first president of Senegal — both co-founded Negritude in 1930s Paris as a response to French assimilationist colonial policy.
completion.quote: My Negritude is not a stone, its deafness hurled against the clamor of the day — it dips into the red flesh of the soil.

related.name: Pan-Africanism
related.note: Negritude is the cultural and philosophical dimension of Pan-Africanism — the assertion of African cultural value as the basis for political solidarity.

related.name: Colonialism and Postcolonialism
related.note: Negritude is a founding moment of postcolonial thought — the counter-assertion of colonized cultural value against assimilationist colonial policy.

related.name: The Second Sex
related.note: Both de Beauvoir's feminism and Negritude assert the value of a marginalized identity against the dominant framework that defines it as 'Other.'

related.name: Decolonizing Knowledge
related.note: Negritude's assertion of African ways of knowing against Eurocentric universalism is a founding moment of the decolonizing knowledge project.

books.title: Notebook of a Return to the Native Land
books.author: Aimé Césaire
books.why: Césaire's founding poetic text of Negritude — the assertion of Black beauty and African heritage against colonial shame.

books.title: On African Socialism
books.author: Léopold Sédar Senghor
books.why: Senghor's philosophical development of Negritude into a theory of African socialism and cultural values.

books.title: Black Skin, White Masks
books.author: Frantz Fanon
books.why: Fanon's psychoanalytic critique of Negritude — the most important challenge to its essentialist assumptions.

completion.name: Neo-Confucian Cosmology
completion.thinkerBio: Zhang Zai (1020-1077 CE) developed a qi-based cosmology in the Western Inscription; Cheng Yi (1033-1107 CE) and Zhu Xi (1130-1200 CE) developed the concept of li (principle) as the normative structure governing all things.
completion.quote: Heaven is my father and earth is my mother -- all people are my brothers and sisters, and all things are my companions.

related.name: The Concept of De
related.note: Both de and the qi-li framework attempt to explain how cosmic structure relates to individual moral cultivation in Chinese philosophy.

related.name: Wang Yangming's Mind-Only Philosophy
related.note: Wang Yangming rejected Zhu Xi's externalist investigation of li in things in favor of locating moral principle entirely within the mind.

related.name: Care Ethics
related.note: Zhang Zai's cosmic kinship -- all people as brothers and sisters, all things as companions -- grounds universal care in cosmological rather than purely interpersonal terms.

related.name: Process Philosophy
related.note: The qi-based cosmology of constant condensation and dispersal has structural affinities with Whitehead's process metaphysics of ongoing creative transformation.

books.title: Sources of Chinese Tradition Vol. 1
books.author: William Theodore de Bary and Irene Bloom (eds.)
books.why: Contains key Neo-Confucian texts including Zhang Zai's Western Inscription and excerpts from Zhu Xi.

books.title: Zhu Xi's Reading of the Analects
books.author: Daniel K. Gardner
books.why: Gardner's scholarly study of Zhu Xi's systematic li-based interpretive framework.

books.title: Neo-Confucianism: A Philosophical Introduction
books.author: JeeLoo Liu
books.why: A clear contemporary introduction to the full Neo-Confucian cosmological tradition including Zhang Zai and Zhu Xi.

completion.name: Neo-Confucianism
completion.thinkerBio: Zhu Xi (1130-1200 CE) synthesized Neo-Confucian metaphysics through the interplay of li (principle) and qi (vital energy); Wang Yangming later challenged his externalism.
completion.quote: Li is the eternal rational principle that gives everything its nature; qi is the energetic stuff through which li is actualized.

related.name: Neo-Confucian Cosmology
related.note: This entry introduces the general Neo-Confucian synthesis that the cosmology entry develops in fuller cosmological detail.

related.name: Wang Yangming's Mind-Only Philosophy
related.note: Wang Yangming directly challenged Zhu Xi's externalist investigation of li in things.

related.name: Confucian Self-Cultivation
related.note: Zhu Xi's investigation of things (gewu) is his proposed method for the classical Confucian project of self-cultivation.

books.title: Zhu Xi's Reading of the Analects
books.author: Daniel K. Gardner
books.why: Gardner's scholarly study of Zhu Xi's systematic li-based framework.

books.title: Neo-Confucianism: A Philosophical Introduction
books.author: JeeLoo Liu
books.why: A clear contemporary introduction to the full Neo-Confucian tradition.

books.title: Sources of Chinese Tradition Vol. 1
books.author: de Bary and Bloom (eds.)
books.why: Contains key primary texts from the Neo-Confucian synthesis.

completion.name: Nihilism
completion.thinkerBio: Friedrich Nietzsche (1844–1900) provided nihilism's definitive philosophical diagnosis; the term was popularized in Ivan Turgenev's Fathers and Sons (1862) but its philosophical depth comes from Nietzsche's analysis in The Gay Science, The Will to Power, and Thus Spoke Zarathustra.
completion.quote: God is dead. God remains dead. And we have killed him.

related.name: Eternal Recurrence
related.note: Eternal recurrence is Nietzsche's proposed test for whether one can create meaning without cosmic backing.

related.name: The Absurd
related.note: Both Camus's absurdism and Nietzsche's nihilism begin from the recognition that the universe provides no inherent meaning, but they respond differently.

related.name: Will to Power
related.note: Active nihilism expressed through will to power — creating new values — is Nietzsche's answer to the nihilistic collapse.

related.name: Existence Precedes Essence
related.note: Both Sartrean existentialism and Nietzschean nihilism begin from the absence of pre-given human essence or cosmic meaning.

books.title: The Will to Power
books.author: Friedrich Nietzsche
books.why: Nietzsche's most sustained engagement with nihilism and its overcoming through value-creation.

books.title: Nausea
books.author: Jean-Paul Sartre
books.why: The literary experience of confronting radical contingency — the experiential complement to Nietzsche's philosophical analysis.

books.title: The Myth of Sisyphus
books.author: Albert Camus
books.why: Camus's alternative response to nihilism — revolt and affirmation rather than Nietzsche's value-creation.

completion.name: Non-Cognitivism
completion.thinkerBio: A.J. Ayer (1910-1989) and Charles Stevenson (1908-1979) established non-cognitivism as the dominant mid-century metaethical position.
completion.quote: To say that lying is wrong is to express one's disapproval, not to state a fact.

related.name: Expressivism
related.note: Expressivism is the sophisticated contemporary development of non-cognitivism.

related.name: Moral Realism
related.note: Non-cognitivism denies moral claims state truths that can be true or false.

related.name: Error Theory
related.note: Both anti-realist, differing on whether moral claims aspire to truth at all.

books.title: Language, Truth and Logic
books.author: A.J. Ayer
books.why: The original emotivist argument.

books.title: Ethics and Language
books.author: Charles Stevenson
books.why: Stevenson's more developed emotivism.

books.title: Moral Discourse and Practice
books.author: Stephen Darwall (ed.)
books.why: The essential anthology of cognitivist and non-cognitivist papers.

completion.name: Nonviolence as Philosophy
completion.thinkerBio: Mohandas Gandhi (1869-1948) developed satyagraha as a comprehensive philosophy of truth-force and nonviolent resistance; Martin Luther King Jr. (1929-1968) synthesized Gandhian nonviolence with Christian theology in the American civil rights movement.
completion.quote: Satyagraha holds that means and ends cannot be separated -- violence, even in service of just ends, corrupts those ends by reproducing the conditions being resisted.

related.name: Civil Disobedience
related.note: Civil disobedience is the political practice grounded in Gandhi and King's broader philosophy of nonviolence.

related.name: Jain Philosophy of Ahimsa
related.note: Gandhi's satyagraha and his philosophy of nonviolent resistance drew explicitly on Jain ahimsa as a primary intellectual source.

related.name: Power and Domination
related.note: Nonviolent resistance is a specific strategy for contesting structural power and domination without reproducing the violence of the system being resisted.

related.name: Forgiveness
related.note: Both nonviolence and forgiveness aim at transformation of relationships rather than mere defeat of an opponent, requiring a particular moral orientation toward adversaries.

books.title: Hind Swaraj
books.author: Mahatma Gandhi
books.why: Gandhi's foundational philosophical text developing satyagraha and the inseparability of means and ends.

books.title: Letter from Birmingham Jail
books.author: Martin Luther King Jr.
books.why: King's most powerful philosophical and theological defence of nonviolent direct action.

books.title: Stride Toward Freedom
books.author: Martin Luther King Jr.
books.why: King's account of the Montgomery bus boycott and his developing philosophy of nonviolent resistance.

completion.name: Norm and Value
completion.thinkerBio: Christine Korsgaard (b. 1952) developed Kantian constructivism grounding normativity in reflective endorsement; T.M. Scanlon (b. 1940) developed the buck-passing account of value, analyzing goodness in terms of reasons rather than as a primitive property.
completion.quote: To say something is good is to say it has properties that give reasons for certain responses -- the buck is passed to the reason-giving properties themselves.

related.name: Categorical Imperative
related.note: Korsgaard's Kantian constructivism grounds normative force in the reflective endorsement structure that Kant's categorical imperative formalizes.

related.name: Moral Realism
related.note: Constructivism holds that normative truths are constituted by rational activity rather than discovered as independent facts, challenging robust moral realism.

related.name: Contractualism
related.note: Both Scanlon's buck-passing account of value and his contractualism analyze normative concepts in terms of reasons rather than treating them as metaphysically primitive.

related.name: Practical Reason
related.note: Both Korsgaard's constructivism and Scanlon's buck-passing are attempts to demystify metaethics by grounding normativity in a more tractable theory of practical reason.

books.title: The Sources of Normativity
books.author: Christine Korsgaard
books.why: Korsgaard's foundational development of Kantian constructivism and reflective endorsement as the source of normative authority.

books.title: What We Owe to Each Other
books.author: T.M. Scanlon
books.why: Scanlon's foundational development of the buck-passing account of value within his broader contractualist framework.

books.title: Being Realistic about Reasons
books.author: T.M. Scanlon
books.why: Scanlon's later defence of reasons-based realism, clarifying his commitments against both robust realism and pure constructivism.

completion.name: Nyaya Logic
completion.thinkerBio: Aksapada Gautama (c. second century CE) founded the Nyaya school of Indian philosophy; Dignaga (fifth century CE) and Dharmakirti (seventh century CE) developed the rigorous Buddhist epistemological tradition that engaged in extended debate with Nyaya logicians.
completion.quote: The five-membered argument requires that every step of inference be made fully explicit for use in public philosophical debate.

related.name: Inference to Best Explanation
related.note: Nyaya's systematic treatment of inference (anumana) and contemporary IBE both investigate the structure and validity of non-deductive reasoning.

related.name: Formal Logic
related.note: The Nyaya five-membered argument is more elaborate than Aristotle's syllogism, representing an independently developed tradition of formal logical analysis.

related.name: Buddhist Meditation and Epistemology
related.note: Nyaya philosophers engaged in extended technical debate with Buddhist epistemologists like Dignaga and Dharmakirti over the nature of perception and inference.

related.name: Testimony
related.note: Nyaya's identification of testimony (shabda) as one of four valid pramanas anticipates contemporary anti-reductionist accounts of testimonial knowledge.

books.title: Nyaya-Vaisesika
books.author: Karl Potter (ed.)
books.why: The definitive scholarly volume on Nyaya philosophy within the Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies series.

books.title: Indian Philosophy: A Very Short Introduction
books.author: Sue Hamilton
books.why: A clear introduction situating Nyaya logic within the broader landscape of classical Indian philosophy.

books.title: Classical Indian Philosophy
books.author: Bina Gupta
books.why: A comprehensive scholarly survey covering Nyaya epistemology and logic in detail.

completion.name: Occam's Razor
completion.thinkerBio: William of Ockham (c. 1287–1347) was an English Franciscan friar and philosopher who wielded parsimony extensively against Scholastic metaphysics; the 'razor' bears his name though it was formulated verbatim by later commentators from his principle 'Plurality must never be posited without necessity.'
completion.quote: Do not multiply entities beyond necessity.

related.name: Scientific Method
related.note: Occam's Razor is a fundamental methodological principle in science — among equally predictive theories, prefer the simpler.

related.name: Formal Logic
related.note: The razor grounds the preference for minimal logical vocabulary — every primitive entity or relation must earn its place.

related.name: Substance Dualism
related.note: The razor is deployed against substance dualism — the immaterial mind is an entity multiplied beyond what the phenomena require.

related.name: Abstract Objects
related.note: Nominalism about abstract objects is motivated by Occam's Razor — universals and sets should be eliminated if possible.

books.title: The Philosophy of Science: An Introduction
books.author: Samir Okasha
books.why: Excellent coverage of the role of parsimony and simplicity in scientific theory choice.

books.title: Simplicity, Inference and Modelling
books.author: Arnold Zellner et al.
books.why: Technical and philosophical analysis of simplicity as a criterion of theory selection.

books.title: Ockham's Philosophical Writings
books.author: William of Ockham
books.why: Selections from Ockham's works — the philosophical context in which the razor is deployed against Scholastic excess.

completion.name: Ontology
completion.thinkerBio: W.V.O. Quine (1908-2000) developed the criterion of ontological commitment; Alexius Meinong (1853-1920) defended a maximally permissive ontology including non-existent objects.
completion.quote: To be is to be the value of a bound variable.

related.name: Abstract Objects
related.note: Ontology's central question of what exists is applied specifically to mathematical and abstract entities.

related.name: Fictional Entities
related.note: Meinong's permissive ontology directly bears on how to treat fictional and non-existent objects.

related.name: The Problem of Universals
related.note: Ontological commitment to universals versus particulars is a central ontological dispute.

books.title: From a Logical Point of View
books.author: W.V.O. Quine
books.why: Contains Quine's foundational criterion of ontological commitment.

books.title: On Object Theory
books.author: Alexius Meinong
books.why: Meinong's permissive ontology of non-existent objects.

books.title: Ontology
books.author: Richard Cameron and Ross Cameron (eds.)
books.why: A contemporary anthology surveying major ontological positions.

completion.name: Ordinary Language Philosophy
completion.thinkerBio: J.L. Austin (1911-1960) led the Oxford ordinary language movement, dissolving philosophical puzzles through careful attention to actual linguistic usage; Gilbert Ryle (1900-1976) applied the method to expose Cartesian dualism as a category mistake.
completion.quote: Many philosophical problems arise from words being lifted out of their ordinary contexts and asked to perform impossible tasks they were never designed for.

related.name: Language Games
related.note: Ordinary language philosophy develops Wittgenstein's therapeutic approach -- dissolving philosophical problems by returning words to their ordinary contexts of use.

related.name: Speech Act Theory
related.note: Speech act theory is the most systematic technical product of the ordinary language philosophy movement that Austin led at Oxford.

related.name: The Mind-Body Problem
related.note: Ryle's category mistake analysis dissolves rather than answers the traditional mind-body problem by exposing it as a misuse of ordinary mental vocabulary.

related.name: Verificationism
related.note: Ordinary language philosophy rejected logical positivism's formal verificationist criterion in favor of careful attention to the actual diverse uses of natural language.

books.title: Sense and Sensibilia
books.author: J.L. Austin
books.why: Austin's foundational application of ordinary language method to dissolve puzzles about perception and sense-data theory.

books.title: The Concept of Mind
books.author: Gilbert Ryle
books.why: Ryle's foundational application of ordinary language method to dissolve the Cartesian mind-body problem.

books.title: Philosophy and Ordinary Language
books.author: Charles Travis
books.why: A contemporary scholarly defence and development of the ordinary language philosophical method.

completion.name: Original Position
completion.thinkerBio: John Rawls (1921–2002) was a Harvard political philosopher whose A Theory of Justice (1971) revived systematic political philosophy after decades of metaethical preoccupation; his work dominated political philosophy for the final decades of the twentieth century.
completion.quote: Justice is the first virtue of social institutions, as truth is of systems of thought.

related.name: Social Contract
related.note: The original position is Rawls's transformation of the social contract tradition into a rigorous thought experiment about the standpoint of fairness.

related.name: Utilitarianism
related.note: The difference principle blocks utilitarian reasoning — behind the veil, no rational agent would accept utilitarian risk of being the worst-off.

related.name: Justice as Fairness
related.note: The original position is the device through which the two principles of justice as fairness are derived.

related.name: Communitarianism
related.note: Sandel's critique targets the unencumbered self behind the veil as a psychologically implausible abstraction stripped of constitutive attachments.

books.title: A Theory of Justice
books.author: John Rawls
books.why: The source — Rawls's full argument for justice as fairness including the original position and difference principle.

books.title: Liberalism and the Limits of Justice
books.author: Michael Sandel
books.why: The most influential communitarian critique of Rawls — essential for understanding what the original position assumes.

books.title: Justice: What's the Right Thing to Do?
books.author: Michael Sandel
books.why: The most accessible entry to debates about justice, using vivid cases to test Rawlsian and utilitarian intuitions.

completion.name: Pain and Suffering
completion.thinkerBio: Ludwig Wittgenstein (1889-1951) used pain as the central case in his private language argument; Elaine Scarry (b. 1946) at Harvard developed the most influential philosophical analysis of pain's world-destroying and politically significant character in The Body in Pain (1985).
completion.quote: Intense pain is world-destroying -- it obliterates the capacity for speech, thought, and the sense of shared humanity.

related.name: Language Games
related.note: Wittgenstein's private language argument uses pain to show that meaning cannot be grounded in private, inner ostensive definition but requires public criteria.

related.name: Qualia
related.note: Pain is the paradigm case of a qualitative mental state -- its irreducible felt quality is central to debates about the nature of phenomenal qualia.

related.name: Buddhist Ethics
related.note: Both Western philosophical analysis of pain and Buddhist diagnosis of dukkha treat suffering as a central category requiring careful philosophical attention.

related.name: Punishment
related.note: Scarry's analysis of how torture exploits pain's capacity to destroy language and agency connects directly to the ethics of state violence and punishment.

books.title: Philosophical Investigations
books.author: Ludwig Wittgenstein
books.why: Contains the foundational private language argument with pain as the central illustrative case.

books.title: The Body in Pain
books.author: Elaine Scarry
books.why: Scarry's profound philosophical and political analysis of pain's world-destroying character.

books.title: The Story of Pain
books.author: Joanna Bourke
books.why: A historical and philosophical account of how pain has been understood and experienced across different periods.

completion.name: Pan-Africanism
completion.thinkerBio: W.E.B. Du Bois (1868–1963) organized the first Pan-African Congress in 1919 and articulated double consciousness; Marcus Garvey (1887–1940) founded the Universal Negro Improvement Association and developed a separatist Pan-Africanism focused on economic self-sufficiency.
completion.quote: The problem of the twentieth century is the problem of the color line.

related.name: Negritude
related.note: Negritude is the cultural and philosophical dimension of Pan-Africanism — both assert African solidarity and the value of African heritage against colonialism.

related.name: Colonialism and Postcolonialism
related.note: Pan-Africanism is the political response to colonialism — the demand for African political self-determination.

related.name: Race and Political Philosophy
related.note: Du Bois's double consciousness names the psychological condition that makes Pan-African solidarity philosophically necessary.

related.name: Cosmopolitanism and Obligations
related.note: Pan-Africanism asserts the particular solidarity of African and diasporic peoples against cosmopolitanism's claim to universal human community.

books.title: The Souls of Black Folk
books.author: W.E.B. Du Bois
books.why: Du Bois's masterwork — double consciousness, the veil, and the philosophical dimensions of Black American experience.

books.title: The Philosophy and Opinions of Marcus Garvey
books.author: Marcus Garvey
books.why: Garvey's separatist Pan-Africanism — the call for Black economic self-sufficiency and return to Africa.

books.title: Pan-Africanism: A Brief History
books.author: Hakim Adi
books.why: A scholarly introduction to the Pan-African movement and its philosophical dimensions.

completion.name: Panpsychism
completion.thinkerBio: David Chalmers (b. 1966) at NYU articulated the hard problem that motivates panpsychism; Philip Goff (b. 1981) at Durham has written the most accessible contemporary defence in Galileo's Error (2019).
completion.quote: Consciousness is a fundamental feature of the world, alongside mass, charge, and spin.

related.name: Hard Problem of Consciousness
related.note: Panpsychism avoids the emergence of consciousness from non-conscious matter by making consciousness fundamental rather than emergent.

related.name: Substance Dualism
related.note: Both are non-physicalist; panpsychism makes consciousness fundamental and physical rather than a separate immaterial substance.

related.name: Qualia
related.note: Panpsychism takes the irreducibility of qualia seriously as requiring revision of our conception of physical reality.

related.name: Process Philosophy
related.note: Whitehead's process philosophy is a form of panpsychism — each actual occasion has an experiential dimension.

books.title: Galileo's Error
books.author: Philip Goff
books.why: The most accessible and rigorous contemporary case for panpsychism, arguing consciousness was excluded from physics by a historical mistake.

books.title: Consciousness Explained
books.author: Daniel Dennett
books.why: The major opposition — Dennett's eliminativist account that panpsychists must engage with.

books.title: Mind and the Cosmic Order
books.author: Charles Pinter
books.why: Overview of panpsychist traditions from Leibniz through Whitehead to contemporary philosophy.

completion.name: Paradigm Shift
completion.thinkerBio: Thomas Kuhn (1922–1996) was an American historian and philosopher of science whose The Structure of Scientific Revolutions (1962) is one of the most cited academic books of the twentieth century; he taught at MIT and Princeton.
completion.quote: Normal science does not aim at novelties of fact or theory and, when successful, finds none.

related.name: Falsifiability
related.note: Kuhn showed that actual science protects paradigms from falsification — Popper's model describes an ideal that real science doesn't follow.

related.name: Theory-Ladenness of Observation
related.note: Theory-ladenness explains why paradigm shifts are so difficult — scientists in different paradigms literally see different things.

related.name: Scientific Revolutions Reconsidered
related.note: Lakatos's sophisticated falsificationism and Feyerabend's anarchism are both responses to the challenges posed by Kuhn's historical account.

related.name: Social Studies of Science
related.note: Kuhn's account of paradigms as social phenomena opened the door to sociological approaches to scientific knowledge production.

books.title: The Structure of Scientific Revolutions
books.author: Thomas Kuhn
books.why: The source — still the most important book in twentieth-century philosophy of science.

books.title: The Essential Tension
books.author: Thomas Kuhn
books.why: Kuhn's essays clarifying his account of paradigms, normal science, and scientific progress.

books.title: Criticism and the Growth of Knowledge
books.author: Lakatos and Musgrave (eds.)
books.why: The 1965 debate between Popper, Kuhn, Lakatos, and Feyerabend — essential for understanding the paradigm shift debate.

completion.name: Perception and Illusion
completion.thinkerBio: A.J. Ayer (1910-1989) developed the argument from illusion supporting sense-datum theory; John McDowell (b. 1942) at Pittsburgh developed disjunctivism as the most influential contemporary challenge to indirect realist theories of perception.
completion.quote: Veridical perception and hallucination are different in kind, not merely in cause -- there is no common factor shared between them that representational theories assume.

related.name: Skepticism
related.note: The argument from illusion historically motivated indirect realism, which in turn generates skeptical worries about whether sense data accurately represent external objects.

related.name: Phenomenology of Perception
related.note: Both McDowell's disjunctivism and Merleau-Ponty's phenomenology resist reducing perception to an inner representational intermediary between mind and world.

related.name: Qualia
related.note: Debates about perception and illusion bear directly on whether qualia are inner mental objects (sense data) or features of direct perceptual contact with the world.

related.name: Functionalism
related.note: Disjunctivism challenges purely functionalist accounts of perception by insisting that veridical perceptual states are categorically different from non-veridical ones.

books.title: The Problem of Perception
books.author: A.D. Smith
books.why: A comprehensive contemporary survey of theories of perception including sense-datum theory and disjunctivism.

books.title: Mind and World
books.author: John McDowell
books.why: McDowell's foundational development of disjunctivism and his critique of the Myth of the Given.

books.title: The Foundations of Empirical Knowledge
books.author: A.J. Ayer
books.why: Ayer's classic statement of sense-datum theory and the argument from illusion.

completion.name: Personal Identity
completion.thinkerBio: John Locke (1632–1704) formulated the memory criterion in An Essay Concerning Human Understanding (1689); Derek Parfit (1942–2017) transformed the debate in Reasons and Persons (1984) by arguing that personal identity is indeterminate and not what matters in survival.
completion.quote: Personal identity consists not in the identity of substance, but in the identity of consciousness.

related.name: Identity Over Time
related.note: Personal identity over time is the specific application of the general problem of persistence through change.

related.name: The Ship of Theseus
related.note: The Ship of Theseus is the general puzzle of which personal identity is the most urgent and practically significant version.

related.name: Anatta
related.note: Buddhist anatta and Parfit's no-self conclusion arrive at structurally similar results through very different routes.

related.name: Narrative Identity
related.note: Narrative identity theory is a response to Parfit — even if there is no metaphysical self, persons constitute identity through the stories they tell.

books.title: Reasons and Persons
books.author: Derek Parfit
books.why: The most important work on personal identity — Parfit's argument that identity is indeterminate and not what matters.

books.title: Personal Identity
books.author: John Perry (ed.)
books.why: The essential anthology including Locke, Hume, Williams, and contemporary papers.

books.title: Self and World
books.author: Quassim Cassam
books.why: Contemporary account connecting metaphysical questions about personal identity to phenomenology and epistemology.

completion.name: Phenomenal Consciousness and the Physical
completion.thinkerBio: Joseph Levine introduced the explanatory gap; Frank Jackson (b. 1943) developed the knowledge argument through Mary's room.
completion.quote: Even if pain is identical to C-fiber firing, we cannot explain why C-fiber firing feels like pain rather than nothing at all.

related.name: Hard Problem of Consciousness
related.note: The explanatory gap is closely related to but distinct from the hard problem, focusing on inexplicability rather than in-principle impossibility.

related.name: Qualia
related.note: Jackson's knowledge argument is the paradigm case pressing the explanatory gap through phenomenal qualia.

related.name: The Hard Problem Revisited
related.note: Type-B physicalism responds to the explanatory gap while accepting physicalism, illustrating the range of contemporary positions.

books.title: Purple Haze
books.author: Joseph Levine
books.why: Levine's foundational development of the explanatory gap.

books.title: There's Something About Mary
books.author: Peter Ludlow (ed.)
books.why: The essential anthology on Jackson's knowledge argument.

books.title: The Conscious Mind
books.author: David Chalmers
books.why: Connects the explanatory gap to Chalmers's broader hard problem.

completion.name: Phenomenology of Perception
completion.thinkerBio: Maurice Merleau-Ponty (1908-1961) wrote Phenomenology of Perception (1945) as his major work, developing a sustained analysis of embodiment that became foundational for both continental philosophy and contemporary cognitive science.
completion.quote: The body is our general medium for having a world.

related.name: Embodied Cognition
related.note: Embodied cognition science draws directly on Merleau-Ponty's account of the body as the pre-reflective vehicle of perception and action.

related.name: Existential Phenomenology
related.note: Phenomenology of Perception is the foundational text of existential phenomenology -- perception understood through lived, embodied existence.

related.name: Substance Dualism
related.note: Merleau-Ponty's account directly challenges the Cartesian picture of the mind as an unextended thinking substance that merely inhabits a body.

related.name: Intersubjectivity
related.note: Merleau-Ponty grounds intersubjectivity in bodily resonance and intercorporeality rather than in theoretical inference about other minds.

books.title: Phenomenology of Perception
books.author: Maurice Merleau-Ponty
books.why: The primary source -- Merleau-Ponty's comprehensive analysis of embodied perception and action.

books.title: Merleau-Ponty's Phenomenology
books.author: Komarine Romdenh-Romluc
books.why: The clearest contemporary scholarly guide to Merleau-Ponty's difficult major work.

books.title: The Visible and the Invisible
books.author: Maurice Merleau-Ponty
books.why: Merleau-Ponty's unfinished late work extending his phenomenology toward a more radical ontology of flesh and reversibility.

completion.name: Phenomenology of Spirit
completion.thinkerBio: G.W.F. Hegel (1770-1831) wrote the Phenomenology of Spirit in 1807, tracing the development of consciousness from bare sense-certainty through increasingly complex forms culminating in Absolute Knowledge.
completion.quote: The True is thus the Bacchanalian revel in which no member is not drunk; yet because each member collapses as soon as he drops out, the revel is just as much transparent and simple repose.

related.name: Dialectics
related.note: The Phenomenology of Spirit is dialectics demonstrated rather than merely described -- consciousness moving through contradiction toward Absolute Knowledge.

related.name: Idealism
related.note: Hegel's absolute idealism is the metaphysical framework within which the Phenomenology's dialectical history of consciousness operates.

related.name: Alienation
related.note: Hegel introduced alienation as a necessary stage in Spirit's self-development through externalization and eventual reconciliation.

related.name: Recognition and Misrecognition
related.note: The master-slave dialectic in the Phenomenology is the founding text for the entire subsequent philosophy of recognition.

books.title: The Phenomenology of Spirit
books.author: G.W.F. Hegel
books.why: The primary source -- Hegel's dialectical journey of consciousness culminating in Absolute Knowledge.

books.title: Hegel's Phenomenology of Spirit
books.author: Robert Stern
books.why: The clearest contemporary guide through Hegel's notoriously difficult text, chapter by chapter.

books.title: Reading Hegel's Phenomenology
books.author: John Russon
books.why: A rigorous philosophical commentary illuminating the systematic structure of Hegel's argument.

completion.name: Philosophy of Action
completion.thinkerBio: G.E.M. Anscombe (1919-2001) founded contemporary philosophy of action in Intention (1957); Donald Davidson (1917-2003) developed the influential causal theory holding that reasons are the causes of intentional actions.
completion.quote: An action is intentional when it makes sense to ask why the agent performed it, and reasons can be given as the answer.

related.name: Free Will vs Determinism
related.note: The causal theory of action connects directly to the free will debate -- if reasons are causes, intentional action may be compatible with a fully deterministic universe.

related.name: Speech Act Theory
related.note: Both philosophy of action and speech act theory analyze the structure of intentional human doings, whether physical actions or linguistic performances.

related.name: Wu Wei
related.note: The causal theory of action presupposes deliberate reasons guiding behavior, in contrast to the Taoist ideal of spontaneous, non-deliberate action.

related.name: Wang Yangming's Mind-Only Philosophy
