related.note: Both Davidsonian action theory and Wang Yangming's unity of knowing and acting investigate the tight connection between mental states and intentional behavior.

books.title: Intention
books.author: G.E.M. Anscombe
books.why: Anscombe's foundational text establishing contemporary philosophy of action as a distinct field.

books.title: Essays on Actions and Events
books.author: Donald Davidson
books.why: Davidson's collected papers developing the causal theory of action and reasons as causes.

books.title: Action, Reason, and Purpose
books.author: Myles Brand (ed.)
books.why: An essential anthology covering the major positions in contemporary philosophy of action.

completion.name: Philosophy of Biology
completion.thinkerBio: Ernst Mayr (1904–2005) was a German-American biologist and philosopher whose work on species concepts and teleology shaped the field; Elliott Sober (b. 1948) at Wisconsin has been the dominant philosopher of biology for four decades, connecting formal evolutionary theory to philosophy of causation.
completion.quote: The species problem is the most important and most difficult problem in taxonomic biology.

related.name: Scientific Explanation
related.note: Philosophy of biology debates what form of explanation is appropriate for biological phenomena — functional, causal-mechanical, or evolutionary.

related.name: Reductionism
related.note: The reducibility of biology to physics and chemistry is one of philosophy of biology's central questions.

related.name: Essentialism
related.note: Evolutionary biology challenges biological essentialism — species lack fixed essential natures because they are historical lineages.

related.name: Environmental Ethics
related.note: Philosophy of biology underlies environmental ethics — what species, ecosystems, and biodiversity are determines what environmental protection requires.

books.title: The Growth of Biological Thought
books.author: Ernst Mayr
books.why: Mayr's magisterial history of biology as science — rich with philosophical analysis of species, teleology, and evolution.

books.title: Evidence and Evolution
books.author: Elliott Sober
books.why: Sober's analysis of the logic of evolutionary evidence — connecting philosophy of biology to epistemology.

books.title: The Philosophy of Biology
books.author: David Hull and Michael Ruse (eds.)
books.why: The essential anthology covering species, selection, function, and reduction in philosophy of biology.

completion.name: Philosophy of Chemistry
completion.thinkerBio: Jan van Brakel (b. 1943) and Eric Scerri (b. 1953) are the leading contemporary philosophers of chemistry, examining whether chemical kinds and explanations are reducible to physics or possess genuine explanatory autonomy.
completion.quote: The periodic table reveals deep regularities in atomic structure, but the chemical organization it embodies may not be fully derivable from quantum mechanics alone.

related.name: Reductionism
related.note: Philosophy of chemistry directly tests reductionism -- whether chemical kinds and explanations reduce fully to quantum mechanical description.

related.name: Philosophy of Biology
related.note: Both philosophy of chemistry and philosophy of biology examine whether their respective sciences possess genuine explanatory autonomy from physics.

related.name: Essentialism
related.note: Scientific essentialism about natural kinds is tested rigorously by the periodic table, where elements appear to have genuinely essential atomic properties.

related.name: Emergence
related.note: Van Brakel argues chemical properties like wetness or acidity may be irreducibly emergent at the macroscopic, human-scale level.

books.title: The Periodic Table: Its Story and Its Significance
books.author: Eric Scerri
books.why: Scerri's authoritative account of the periodic table's philosophical and scientific significance.

books.title: Philosophy of Chemistry: Synthesis of a New Discipline
books.author: Davis Baird, Eric Scerri, and Lee McIntyre (eds.)
books.why: The foundational anthology establishing philosophy of chemistry as a distinct discipline.

books.title: Philosophy of Chemistry
books.author: Jaap van Brakel
books.why: Van Brakel's systematic philosophical treatment of chemical autonomy and the limits of reduction.

completion.name: Philosophy of Fiction
completion.thinkerBio: Kendall Walton (b. 1939) developed the make-believe theory of fiction; Gregory Currie (b. 1953) developed the simulation-based account of fictional understanding.
completion.quote: Fictional works are props in games of make-believe -- our emotional responses to characters are quasi-emotions generated within the imaginative game.

related.name: Fictional Entities
related.note: The philosophy of fiction addresses questions about reference and truth that the metaphysics of fictional entities investigates ontologically.

related.name: Interpretation
related.note: Debates about fictional truth and reference directly bear on how fictional texts should be interpreted.

related.name: Imagination
related.note: Walton's make-believe theory grounds fictional engagement in the exercise of imagination as a distinctive cognitive capacity.

books.title: Mimesis as Make-Believe
books.author: Kendall Walton
books.why: Walton's foundational make-believe theory of fiction and representation.

books.title: The Nature of Fiction
books.author: Gregory Currie
books.why: Currie's simulation-based alternative account of fictional understanding.

books.title: Fictional Points of View
books.author: Peter Lamarque
books.why: A comprehensive philosophical treatment of narrative fiction and perspective.

completion.name: Philosophy of Mathematics
completion.thinkerBio: Gottlob Frege (1848-1925) developed logicism, attempting to reduce mathematics to logic; Kurt Godel (1906-1978) proved the incompleteness theorems that transformed the philosophical understanding of mathematical truth and proof.
completion.quote: Any sufficiently powerful consistent formal system contains true statements that cannot be proved within that system.

related.name: Formal Logic
related.note: Frege's logicist program and Godel's incompleteness theorems both emerged from the formalization of logic that grounds mathematical philosophy.

related.name: The Problem of Universals
related.note: Mathematical Platonism about numbers and sets is a special case of the broader debate about the existence of abstract universals.

related.name: A Priori / A Posteriori
related.note: Mathematical knowledge is the paradigm case of a priori knowledge, raising deep questions about how we can know truths independent of experience.

related.name: Abstract Objects
related.note: Whether numbers and sets exist as abstract objects independent of minds is the central ontological question in philosophy of mathematics.

books.title: Introduction to Mathematical Philosophy
books.author: Bertrand Russell
books.why: Russell's classic accessible introduction to the logicist foundations of mathematics.

books.title: Godel, Escher, Bach
books.author: Douglas Hofstadter
books.why: An accessible and profound exploration of Godel's incompleteness theorems and their philosophical implications.

books.title: Philosophy of Mathematics: Selected Readings
books.author: Paul Benacerraf and Hilary Putnam (eds.)
books.why: The essential anthology covering logicism, formalism, intuitionism, and Platonism in philosophy of mathematics.

completion.name: Philosophy of Medicine
completion.thinkerBio: Georges Canguilhem (1904-1995) challenged the statistical model of disease in The Normal and the Pathological; the evidence-based medicine movement raised further philosophical questions about clinical judgment.
completion.quote: The distinction between normal and pathological is not merely statistical deviation -- health is the flexibility to establish new biological norms.

related.name: Philosophy of Psychiatry
related.note: Both philosophy of medicine and philosophy of psychiatry examine the conceptual foundations of health, disease, and disorder.

related.name: Scientific Knowledge and Values
related.note: Debates about evidence-based medicine directly engage questions about the role of values and clinical judgment alongside statistical evidence.

related.name: Naturalism
related.note: Whether medical concepts like disease can be fully naturalized without evaluative content is a central question in philosophy of medicine.

books.title: The Normal and the Pathological
books.author: Georges Canguilhem
books.why: Canguilhem's foundational challenge to the purely statistical model of disease.

books.title: Philosophy of Medicine
books.author: Alex Broadbent
books.why: A clear contemporary introduction to the philosophy of medicine.

books.title: Evidence-Based Medicine and the Search for a Science of Clinical Care
books.author: Jeanne Daly
books.why: A critical philosophical examination of the evidence-based medicine movement.

completion.name: Philosophy of Psychiatry
completion.thinkerBio: Christopher Boorse (b. 1943) developed the biostatistical account of disease as deviation from statistically normal functioning; Jerome Wakefield (b. 1948) developed the harmful dysfunction analysis requiring both biological dysfunction and resulting harm.
completion.quote: Disorder requires both a failure of naturally selected function and harm to the person -- capturing the dual biological and evaluative nature of mental illness.

related.name: Naturalism
related.note: Boorse's biostatistical account attempts a fully naturalistic, value-neutral definition of disease grounded purely in statistical deviation from normal function.

related.name: The Unconscious
related.note: Philosophy of psychiatry engages directly with psychoanalytic and cognitive models of unconscious processes in explaining the etiology of mental disorder.

related.name: Pain and Suffering
related.note: Both philosophy of psychiatry and the philosophy of pain examine how subjective suffering interacts with biological dysfunction in determining what counts as pathological.

related.name: Epistemic Injustice
related.note: Psychiatric diagnosis can produce distinctive forms of epistemic injustice when patients' own understanding of their experience is discounted relative to clinical authority.

books.title: What Is Disease?
books.author: James Humber and Robert Almeder (eds.)
books.why: An anthology including Boorse's foundational biostatistical theory and critical responses.

books.title: The Loss of Sadness
books.author: Allan Horwitz and Jerome Wakefield
books.why: Wakefield's application of the harmful dysfunction analysis to the medicalization of normal sadness as depression.

books.title: Philosophy of Psychiatry: A Companion
books.author: K.W.M. Fulford, Tim Thornton, and George Graham
books.why: The comprehensive scholarly companion covering all major positions in philosophy of psychiatry.

completion.name: Philosophy of Technology
completion.thinkerBio: Martin Heidegger (1889-1976) analyzed technology as a mode of revealing in 'The Question Concerning Technology'; Jacques Ellul (1912-1994) developed a sociological analysis of technique.
completion.quote: Modern technology discloses nature as standing reserve -- resources stockpiled for human exploitation rather than encountered as they are.

related.name: The Question of Being
related.note: Heidegger's analysis of technology is part of his broader later engagement with the question of Being and modes of disclosure.

related.name: Artificial Intelligence and Mind
related.note: Contemporary philosophy of technology engages directly with the philosophical implications of AI and automation.

related.name: Naturalism
related.note: Ellul's critique of instrumental technique challenges purely naturalistic, efficiency-oriented approaches to organizing social life.

books.title: The Question Concerning Technology
books.author: Martin Heidegger
books.why: Heidegger's foundational essay on technology as a mode of revealing.

books.title: The Technological Society
books.author: Jacques Ellul
books.why: Ellul's sociological and philosophical analysis of the totalization of technique.

books.title: Technics and Time, 1
books.author: Bernard Stiegler
books.why: A contemporary continental development connecting technology to temporality and human origin.

completion.name: Philosophy of the Future
completion.thinkerBio: Derek Parfit (1942-2017) developed the non-identity problem challenging standard approaches to obligations toward future generations; Nick Bostrom (b. 1973) developed longtermism and the analysis of existential risk grounded in the astronomical scale of potential future populations.
completion.quote: If there are vast numbers of possible future people, the value of ensuring they exist and flourish dwarfs the value of any policy focused only on currently existing people.

related.name: Population Ethics
related.note: Population ethics and longtermism are directly connected -- the number of future people who could exist makes policies affecting future populations enormously significant.

related.name: Moral Uncertainty
related.note: Longtermism operates under profound moral and empirical uncertainty about the far future, requiring careful application of moral uncertainty frameworks.

related.name: Cosmopolitanism and Obligations
related.note: Longtermism extends cosmopolitan obligation across time as well as space -- distance in time, like distance in space, is held to be morally irrelevant.

related.name: Artificial Intelligence and Mind
related.note: Existential risk from advanced AI is one of the central concrete concerns motivating contemporary longtermist philosophy and policy.

books.title: The Precipice
books.author: Toby Ord
books.why: Ord's comprehensive philosophical and empirical treatment of existential risk and longtermist ethics.

books.title: What We Owe the Future
books.author: William MacAskill
books.why: MacAskill's accessible and influential systematic case for longtermism.

books.title: Reasons and Persons
books.author: Derek Parfit
books.why: Contains Parfit's foundational non-identity problem, central to the philosophical foundations of longtermist ethics.

completion.name: Photography and Visual Culture
completion.thinkerBio: Susan Sontag (1933-2004) analyzed photography as a form of acquisition and possession in On Photography (1977); Roland Barthes (1915-1980) developed a phenomenology of photographic experience through studium and punctum in Camera Lucida (1980).
completion.quote: The photograph always represents something that has been -- the person photographed is both alive in the photograph and dead, since time has passed.

related.name: The Spectacle
related.note: Both photography theory and Debord's spectacle examine how the proliferation of images transforms authentic experience into mediated representation.

related.name: The Uncanny
related.note: Barthes's account of photography as 'flat death' -- presence and absence simultaneously -- connects to the uncanny's structure of the familiar made strange.

related.name: The Gaze
related.note: Photography theory and the gaze both examine how visual representation structures relationships of looking, power, and objectification.

related.name: Interpretation
related.note: Barthes's punctum -- the detail that escapes intended meaning to create personal response -- connects to broader debates about authorial intention and interpretation.

books.title: On Photography
books.author: Susan Sontag
books.why: Sontag's foundational philosophical analysis of photography's role in acquisition, consumption, and the documentation of experience.

books.title: Camera Lucida
books.author: Roland Barthes
books.why: Barthes's foundational phenomenology of photographic experience through studium and punctum.

books.title: Ways of Seeing
books.author: John Berger
books.why: Berger's influential and accessible analysis of visual culture, looking, and power that complements Sontag and Barthes.

completion.name: Political Epistemology
completion.thinkerBio: David Estlund (b. 1956) developed epistemic proceduralism defending democracy on epistemic grounds; Jason Brennan (b. 1979) developed the epistocracy challenge, arguing that voter incompetence undermines simple democratic justification.
completion.quote: Democratic procedures, when functioning well, tend to track morally correct political outcomes better than alternatives, because they aggregate diverse perspectives.

related.name: Deliberative Democracy
related.note: Deliberative democracy has significant epistemic dimensions -- political epistemology examines whether and how the quality of public reasoning tracks correct outcomes.

related.name: Democracy
related.note: Political epistemology provides epistemic grounds for preferring democracy to alternatives, complementing purely procedural justifications.

related.name: Social Epistemology
related.note: Political epistemology is the specific application of social epistemology's insights about collective knowledge production to democratic institutions.

related.name: Calibration and Epistemic Rationality
related.note: Both political epistemology and calibration theory examine whether and how collective belief-forming processes track truth and accuracy.

books.title: Democratic Authority
books.author: David Estlund
books.why: Estlund's foundational development of epistemic proceduralism as a defence of democratic legitimacy.

books.title: Against Democracy
books.author: Jason Brennan
books.why: Brennan's provocative epistocracy challenge to standard democratic justifications.

books.title: The Myth of the Rational Voter
books.author: Bryan Caplan
books.why: Caplan's empirical case for systematic voter irrationality that motivates the epistocracy debate.

completion.name: Political Feminism
completion.thinkerBio: Mary Wollstonecraft (1759-1797) founded political feminism with A Vindication of the Rights of Woman (1792); John Stuart Mill (1806-1873) extended liberal political principles to women's subjection in The Subjection of Women (1869).
completion.quote: The same liberal principles that ground the rights of men ground the rights of women -- there is no principled basis for exclusion.

related.name: Liberalism
related.note: Political feminism extends liberal principles of equal rights and opportunity systematically to women, exposing the inconsistency of restricting them by sex.

related.name: The Second Sex
related.note: De Beauvoir's existentialist analysis of women's internalized subordination develops Mill's earlier observation about manufactured consent to subjection.

related.name: Feminist Political Philosophy
related.note: Contemporary feminist political philosophy builds on the foundational liberal feminist case while extending it to structural and intersectional concerns.

related.name: Intersectionality
related.note: Intersectionality challenges classical liberal feminism's relatively narrow focus on gender alone, without sufficient attention to race and class.

books.title: A Vindication of the Rights of Woman
books.author: Mary Wollstonecraft
books.why: The founding text -- the case that women's apparent inferiority results from inadequate education, not natural incapacity.

books.title: The Subjection of Women
books.author: John Stuart Mill
books.why: Mill's systematic liberal argument that women's legal subordination is wrong for the same reasons any ascribed status inequality is wrong.

books.title: Justice, Gender, and the Family
books.author: Susan Moller Okin
books.why: Okin's foundational late-twentieth-century extension of liberal feminist critique to the structure of the family.

completion.name: Political Legitimacy
completion.thinkerBio: Max Weber (1864-1920) developed the classic typology of legitimate authority -- traditional, charismatic, and legal-rational; contemporary political philosophers including David Estlund examine what normative conditions make political authority genuinely legitimate.
completion.quote: Political legitimacy concerns whether a government has the right to rule, distinct from citizens' obligation to obey or the moral acceptability of specific laws.

related.name: Political Obligation
related.note: Legitimacy and obligation are distinct -- a government can be legitimate without every citizen having an obligation to obey every law.

related.name: Social Contract
related.note: Social contract theory is the most influential account of political legitimacy.

related.name: Sovereignty
related.note: Sovereignty concerns the location of ultimate authority; legitimacy concerns whether its exercise is normatively justified.

related.name: Political Epistemology
related.note: Epistemic theories of legitimacy hold it depends partly on whether procedures reliably track correct outcomes.

books.title: Economy and Society
books.author: Max Weber
books.why: Weber's foundational sociological typology of legitimate authority.

books.title: Democratic Authority
books.author: David Estlund
books.why: Estlund's contemporary treatment of what makes democratic authority legitimate.

books.title: On Political Legitimacy
books.author: Arthur Isak Applbaum
books.why: A comprehensive contemporary survey of competing theories of political legitimacy.

completion.name: Political Obligation
completion.thinkerBio: T.H. Green (1836-1882) grounded political obligation in social membership; H.L.A. Hart (1907-1992) and John Rawls developed fair play theories.
completion.quote: Citizens who benefit from a cooperative scheme governed by law are obligated to do their fair share by complying with it.

related.name: Social Contract
related.note: The social contract is the standard account of political obligation -- we are bound because we have consented.

related.name: Civil Disobedience
related.note: Civil disobedience is the most philosophically interesting test of political obligation's limits.

related.name: Political Legitimacy
related.note: Legitimacy and obligation are distinct but related -- a government can be legitimate without every citizen being obligated to obey every law.

books.title: Lectures on the Principles of Political Obligation
books.author: T.H. Green
books.why: Green's foundational social membership theory of political obligation.

books.title: Are There Any Natural Rights?
books.author: H.L.A. Hart, in Philosophical Review
books.why: Hart's foundational fair play theory of obligation.

books.title: A Theory of Justice
books.author: John Rawls
books.why: Rawls's development of fair play as grounding political obligation.

completion.name: Population Ethics
completion.thinkerBio: Derek Parfit (1942-2017) introduced the repugnant conclusion in Reasons and Persons (1984); David Benatar (b. 1966) developed anti-natalism in Better Never to Have Been (2006).
completion.quote: For any population of very happy people, there is a larger population of people with lives barely worth living whose existence would be better.

related.name: Utilitarianism
related.note: The repugnant conclusion follows from applying utilitarian aggregation to population size.

related.name: Philosophy of the Future
related.note: Population ethics and longtermism are connected -- the number of future people who could exist makes policies affecting future populations enormously significant.

related.name: Moral Uncertainty
related.note: Population ethics is a domain of deep moral uncertainty.

related.name: Bioethics
related.note: Population ethics extends bioethical reasoning about individual reproductive choices to collective policy questions.

books.title: Reasons and Persons
books.author: Derek Parfit
books.why: Part IV contains Parfit's introduction of the repugnant conclusion.

books.title: Better Never to Have Been
books.author: David Benatar
books.why: Benatar's anti-natalist argument.

books.title: The Repugnant Conclusion
books.author: Jesper Ryberg and Torbjorn Tannsjo (eds.)
books.why: The essential anthology of responses to Parfit's repugnant conclusion.

completion.name: Possible Language Games
completion.thinkerBio: Ludwig Wittgenstein (1889-1951) raised the rule-following paradox in the Philosophical Investigations; Saul Kripke (1940-2022) developed the influential skeptical interpretation.
completion.quote: No finite set of past applications determines what a rule requires in new cases.

related.name: Language Games
related.note: This entry develops the rule-following paradox within Wittgenstein's broader language games framework.

related.name: Meaning as Use
related.note: The rule-following paradox complicates any simple picture of meaning as determined by use.

related.name: Formal Logic
related.note: The rule-following paradox challenges the idea that formal rules alone can determine correct application without a social community.

books.title: Wittgenstein on Rules and Private Language
books.author: Saul Kripke
books.why: Kripke's influential 'skeptical solution' to the rule-following paradox.

books.title: Philosophical Investigations
books.author: Ludwig Wittgenstein
books.why: The primary source raising the rule-following paradox.

books.title: Rule-Following and Meaning
books.author: Alexander Miller and Crispin Wright (eds.)
books.why: A comprehensive anthology on the rule-following debate.

completion.name: Possible Worlds
completion.thinkerBio: Saul Kripke (b. 1940) introduced possible worlds semantics for modal logic in the late 1950s; David Lewis (1941–2001) at Princeton developed modal realism — the radical claim that possible worlds are literally real concrete universes — in On the Plurality of Worlds (1986).
completion.quote: I believe, and so do you, that things could have been different in countless ways.

related.name: Counterfactuals
related.note: Lewis's possible worlds semantics was explicitly developed to give rigorous truth conditions for counterfactual conditionals.

related.name: Essentialism
related.note: Kripke's essentialism is grounded in possible worlds — essential properties are those a thing has in every possible world in which it exists.

related.name: Modality and Necessity
related.note: Possible worlds provide the formal semantic framework within which metaphysical necessity and possibility are analyzed.

related.name: Four-Dimensionalism
related.note: Lewis's modal realism and his four-dimensionalism about persistence are parts of the same comprehensive metaphysical system.

books.title: On the Plurality of Worlds
books.author: David Lewis
books.why: Lewis's full defence of modal realism — the most ambitious and systematic metaphysical theory of the twentieth century.

books.title: Naming and Necessity
books.author: Saul Kripke
books.why: Kripke's lectures that transformed philosophy of language and modal metaphysics — accessible and foundational.

books.title: The Philosophy of Philosophy
books.author: Timothy Williamson
books.why: Williamson's account of philosophical methodology through modal epistemology and the limits of possible worlds reasoning.

completion.name: Postmodern Ethics
completion.thinkerBio: Zygmunt Bauman (1925-2017) developed postmodern ethics drawing on Emmanuel Levinas (1906-1995), arguing that the bureaucratic administration of modern morality enabled rather than prevented the Holocaust.
completion.quote: Morality cannot be institutionalized without loss -- the face of the Other makes an infinite demand that no system or procedure can fully discharge.

related.name: Hermeneutics of Suspicion
related.note: Both postmodern ethics and the hermeneutics of suspicion challenge the adequacy of systematic, rule-governed approaches to morality and meaning.

related.name: Categorical Imperative
related.note: Postmodern ethics rejects Kantian systematic moral rules in favor of the immediate, pre-theoretical responsibility generated by encountering the face of the Other.

related.name: Biopolitics and Bare Life
related.note: Both postmodern ethics and Agamben's analysis of bare life examine how bureaucratic and administrative systems can produce or enable extreme violence.

related.name: Care Ethics
related.note: Both postmodern ethics and care ethics ground moral responsibility in concrete, particular relationships rather than in abstract universal principles.

books.title: Postmodern Ethics
books.author: Zygmunt Bauman
books.why: Bauman's foundational development of an ethics grounded in immediate responsibility rather than systematic rules.

books.title: Totality and Infinity
books.author: Emmanuel Levinas
books.why: Levinas's foundational philosophical text on the ethical demand made by the face of the Other.

books.title: Modernity and the Holocaust
books.author: Zygmunt Bauman
books.why: Bauman's sociological and philosophical analysis of how modern rationality and bureaucracy enabled the Holocaust.

completion.name: Power and Domination
completion.thinkerBio: Robert Dahl (1915-2014) at Yale developed the pluralist analysis of power as the ability to prevail in observable conflicts; Steven Lukes (b. 1941) developed the influential three-dimensional analysis adding agenda-setting and preference-shaping to overt conflict.
completion.quote: A has power over B to the extent that he can get B to do something B would not otherwise do.

related.name: Hegemony
related.note: Lukes's third dimension of power -- shaping preferences themselves -- converges with Gramsci's account of how hegemony operates through manufactured consent.

related.name: Power-Knowledge
related.note: Foucault's power-knowledge goes further than Lukes -- power is not merely possessed and exercised but is everywhere, productive of subjects and truths.

related.name: Republicanism
related.note: Republican non-domination provides the most precise political analysis of what domination consists in and what institutions are needed to prevent it.

related.name: Civil Disobedience
related.note: Civil disobedience theory analyzes resistance as a response to structural power -- not just individual injustice but systemic domination.

books.title: Power: A Radical View
books.author: Steven Lukes
books.why: The foundational text of the three-dimensional view of power -- overt conflict, agenda-setting, and preference-shaping.

books.title: Who Governs?
books.author: Robert Dahl
books.why: Dahl's pluralist empirical study and theoretical foundation for analyzing power as observable influence over decisions.

books.title: Discipline and Punish
books.author: Michel Foucault
books.why: Foucault's analysis of disciplinary power that goes beyond Lukes's framework to examine power as productive and pervasive.

completion.name: Power-Knowledge
completion.thinkerBio: Michel Foucault (1926-1984) developed power-knowledge across Discipline and Punish and his Collège de France lectures, arguing power and knowledge directly imply one another.
completion.quote: Power produces knowledge; power and knowledge directly imply one another -- there is no power relation without a correlative field of knowledge.

related.name: Panopticon
related.note: The panopticon is power-knowledge made concrete -- surveillance generates knowledge that extends disciplinary control.

related.name: Biopower
related.note: Biopower is a specific application of power-knowledge -- medicine and demography generate knowledge while exercising power over populations.

related.name: Colonialism and Postcolonialism
related.note: Said's Orientalism applies Foucauldian power-knowledge to show how scholarly representation functioned as colonial power.

books.title: Discipline and Punish
books.author: Michel Foucault
books.why: Foucault's foundational analysis connecting disciplinary power to knowledge production.

books.title: Power/Knowledge
books.author: Michel Foucault (ed. Colin Gordon)
books.why: Foucault's interviews and essays directly addressing the power-knowledge relationship.

books.title: Foucault
books.author: Gary Gutting
books.why: A clear introduction to Foucault's philosophy including power-knowledge.

completion.name: Practical Reason
completion.thinkerBio: Bernard Williams (1929-2003) developed internalism about reasons, requiring connection to an agent's existing motivational set; Derek Parfit (1942-2017) defended an objective list account of reasons that can apply regardless of an agent's actual desires.
completion.quote: An agent has a reason to act only if the action could be motivated by their existing subjective motivational set through sound deliberation.

related.name: Moral Motivation
related.note: The internalism-externalism debate about practical reasons directly extends the broader Hume-Kant debate about the sources of moral motivation.

related.name: Categorical Imperative
related.note: Parfit's externalist objective list reasons echo Kant's view that genuine moral reasons can bind regardless of an agent's actual desires or motivational set.

related.name: Hedonism
related.note: Parfit's challenge to internalism through the case of the future-Tuesday-indifferent agent connects directly to broader debates about hedonism and rational self-interest.

related.name: Norm and Value
related.note: Both Korsgaard's constructivism and the practical reason debate investigate what grounds the normative force of reasons for action.

books.title: Moral Luck
books.author: Bernard Williams
books.why: Williams's collected essays including his foundational development of internalism about reasons for action.

books.title: On What Matters (Vols 1 & 2)
books.author: Derek Parfit
books.why: Parfit's late masterwork defending an objective list account of reasons against internalist alternatives.

books.title: Weighing Reasons
books.author: John Broome
books.why: Broome's careful analytic distinction between explanatory and normative reasons, clarifying much confusion in the practical reason debate.

completion.name: Pragmatics
completion.thinkerBio: H.P. Grice (1913-1988) founded contemporary pragmatics through conversational implicature; Dan Sperber and Deirdre Wilson developed relevance theory as the leading successor framework.
completion.quote: Speakers communicate more than they literally say, and hearers infer this by assuming cooperative conversational behavior.

related.name: Implicature and Pragmatic Inference
related.note: This entry introduces the general field that the implicature entry develops in technical detail.

related.name: Relevance and Communication
related.note: Relevance theory is the most influential contemporary development within pragmatics.

related.name: Speech Act Theory
related.note: Both pragmatics and speech act theory examine how utterances communicate beyond literal semantic content.

books.title: Studies in the Way of Words
books.author: H.P. Grice
books.why: The foundational collection establishing contemporary pragmatics.

books.title: Pragmatics
books.author: Stephen Levinson
books.why: The standard comprehensive pragmatics textbook.

books.title: Relevance: Communication and Cognition
books.author: Sperber and Wilson
books.why: The leading contemporary unified theory of pragmatics.

completion.name: Pragmatism
completion.thinkerBio: Charles Sanders Peirce (1839–1914) introduced the pragmatic maxim; William James (1842–1910) extended it to truth; John Dewey (1859–1952) developed it into a comprehensive philosophy of inquiry, education, and democracy — together they constitute classical American pragmatism.
completion.quote: The true is only the expedient in the way of our thinking, just as the right is only the expedient in the way of our behaving.

related.name: Naturalized Epistemology
related.note: Quine's naturalized epistemology is a neo-pragmatist position — epistemology as part of empirical science rather than a priori first philosophy.

related.name: Coherentism
related.note: Both treat knowledge as a web of interconnected beliefs evaluated holistically rather than derived from certain foundations.

related.name: The Nature of Truth
related.note: James's pragmatist account of truth as what works is one of the main alternatives to correspondence and coherence theories.

related.name: Language Games
related.note: Wittgenstein's meaning-as-use and Dewey's instrumentalism both reject the idea that thought represents a mind-independent reality rather than coordinating action.

books.title: Pragmatism
books.author: William James
books.why: The most accessible statement of classical pragmatism — James's eight lectures on truth and meaning.

books.title: Consequences of Pragmatism
books.author: Richard Rorty
books.why: The best introduction to neo-pragmatism — Rorty's essays connecting Peirce, James, and Dewey to analytic philosophy.

books.title: Experience and Nature
books.author: John Dewey
books.why: Dewey's most systematic work — his naturalistic account of experience, meaning, and inquiry.

completion.name: Presentism and the Block Universe
completion.thinkerBio: Presentism holds that only the present moment exists; the block universe view, associated with the implications of special relativity, holds that past, present, and future all exist equally within a four-dimensional spacetime manifold.
completion.quote: In the block universe, there is no privileged now -- the distinction between past and future is a feature of one's position within spacetime, not of reality itself.

related.name: The Reality of Time
related.note: This entry develops the specific metaphysical positions -- presentism versus the block universe -- that organize debates about the reality of temporal passage.

related.name: Four-Dimensionalism
related.note: The block universe view is closely connected to four-dimensionalism about persistence, treating time as a fourth dimension analogous to the three spatial dimensions.

related.name: The Arrow of Time
related.note: Both presentism debates and the arrow of time investigate whether temporal asymmetry and passage are objective features of physical reality.

related.name: Identity Over Time
related.note: Whether presentism or the block universe is correct bears directly on how we should understand identity and persistence through time.

books.title: Real Time II
books.author: D.H. Mellor
books.why: Mellor's foundational defence of the B-theory and the block universe against presentist alternatives.

books.title: The Reality of Tense and Temporal Experience
books.author: L. Nathan Oaklander
books.why: A contemporary defence of presentism and the dynamic A-theory of time against block universe objections.

books.title: Time
books.author: Robin Le Poidevin and Murray MacBeath (eds.)
books.why: A comprehensive anthology covering the presentism versus block universe debate from multiple angles.

completion.name: Process Philosophy
completion.thinkerBio: Alfred North Whitehead (1861–1947) was a British mathematician who developed process metaphysics in Process and Reality (1929) after collaborating with Russell on Principia Mathematica; he taught at Harvard from 1924 until his death.
completion.quote: The art of progress is to preserve order amid change, and to preserve change amid order.

related.name: Panpsychism
related.note: Whitehead's process philosophy is a form of panpsychism — each actual occasion has an experiential dimension.

related.name: Dependent Origination
related.note: Both Buddhist dependent origination and Whiteheadian process philosophy take events rather than substances as fundamental.

related.name: Causation
related.note: Process philosophy reconceives causation as the creative advance of actual occasions rather than a relation between persistent substances.

related.name: Identity Over Time
related.note: Process philosophy denies the persistent substance whose identity over time is in question — what we call things are stable processes.

books.title: Process and Reality
books.author: Alfred North Whitehead
books.why: The foundational text — notoriously difficult but irreplaceable for understanding process metaphysics.

books.title: Adventures of Ideas
books.author: Alfred North Whitehead
books.why: Whitehead's most accessible major work — his philosophical vision applied to civilization, beauty, and ideas.

books.title: Whitehead's Radically Different Postmodern Philosophy
books.author: David Ray Griffin
books.why: The best secondary introduction to process philosophy's systematic implications.

completion.name: Properties and Tropes
completion.thinkerBio: D.C. Williams (1899-1983) introduced tropes as abstract particulars in 1953; Keith Campbell (b. 1938) developed a systematic trope ontology treating ordinary objects as bundles of mutually compresent tropes.
completion.quote: Tropes are both particular and qualitative -- each instance of redness is its own particular trope, resembling but not identical to other red tropes.

related.name: The Problem of Universals
related.note: Trope theory denies shared universals -- resembling particulars replace universals as the fundamental constituents of property instantiation.

related.name: Substance and Accident
related.note: Trope theory eliminates the need for bare particulars as substrata bearing properties.

related.name: Bundle Theory
related.note: Trope theory is the most developed contemporary form of bundle theory.

related.name: Essentialism
related.note: Trope theorists explain essential properties as a special subset of tropes whose loss would destroy identity.

books.title: Abstract Particulars
books.author: Keith Campbell
books.why: Campbell's foundational systematic development of trope theory.

books.title: The Elements of Being
books.author: D.C. Williams
books.why: Williams's original foundational paper introducing tropes.

books.title: Tropes: Properties, Objects, and Mental Causation
books.author: Anna-Sofia Maurin
books.why: A rigorous contemporary survey of trope theory.

completion.name: Property Rights and Libertarianism
completion.thinkerBio: Robert Nozick (1938-2002) developed entitlement theory and the minimal state argument in Anarchy, State, and Utopia (1974), grounding libertarian political philosophy in the inviolability of historically just acquisitions and transfers.
completion.quote: A distribution is just if and only if it arises from just acquisitions and just voluntary transfers -- historical process, not distributive pattern, determines justice.

related.name: Rights
related.note: Nozick's entitlement theory is the most systematic attempt to derive political philosophy entirely from inviolable individual property rights.

related.name: Justice as Fairness
related.note: Nozick explicitly rejects Rawlsian patterned distributive justice -- redistribution to satisfy any particular pattern, including the difference principle, violates individual entitlements.

related.name: Socialism
related.note: Libertarian entitlement theory and socialist critiques of capitalist property relations represent opposite poles on the legitimacy of unequal property holdings.

related.name: Anarchism
related.note: Both libertarianism and anarchism share skepticism of expansive state power, though libertarianism defends a minimal state that anarchism rejects entirely.

books.title: Anarchy, State, and Utopia
books.author: Robert Nozick
books.why: Nozick's foundational text developing entitlement theory and the case for the minimal state.

books.title: The Examined Life
books.author: Robert Nozick
books.why: Nozick's later, more reflective philosophical essays revisiting and complicating his earlier libertarian commitments.

books.title: Reading Nozick
books.author: Jeffrey Paul (ed.)
books.why: A comprehensive anthology of critical responses to Nozick's libertarian political philosophy.

completion.name: Propositional Attitudes
completion.thinkerBio: Bertrand Russell (1872-1970) and Gottlob Frege (1848-1925) developed the foundational analysis of belief, desire, and other attitudes toward propositions.
completion.quote: John believes Hesperus is visible and John believes Phosphorus is visible can differ in truth value, though Hesperus is Phosphorus.

related.name: Frege's Sense and Reference
related.note: Frege used sense and reference to explain the opacity of propositional attitude contexts.

related.name: Language of Thought
related.note: Fodor's language of thought treats propositional attitudes as relations to mentalese symbols.

related.name: The Intentionality of Consciousness
related.note: Propositional attitudes are the paradigm case of intentional, content-bearing mental states.

books.title: The Frege Reader
books.author: Michael Beaney (ed.)
books.why: Contains Frege's foundational treatment of sense, reference, and attitude reports.

books.title: Frege's Puzzle
books.author: Nathan Salmon
books.why: A rigorous contemporary treatment of propositional attitude puzzles.

books.title: Propositional Attitudes
books.author: C. Anthony Anderson and Joseph Owens (eds.)
books.why: An essential anthology on the semantics of attitude reports.

completion.name: Punishment
completion.thinkerBio: Immanuel Kant (1724-1804) is the paradigmatic retributivist; Jeremy Bentham (1748-1832) the paradigmatic consequentialist on punishment.
completion.quote: Judicial punishment can never be used merely as a means to promote some other good; it must be inflicted because the criminal has committed a crime.

related.name: Rights
related.note: Rights-based punishment theories hold proportional punishment recognizes criminals as responsible agents.

related.name: Utilitarianism
related.note: Consequentialist punishment is justified by future effects; retributivism by past desert alone.

related.name: Civil Disobedience
related.note: Willingness to accept punishment connects to the legitimacy of law-governed institutions.

books.title: Punishment and Responsibility
books.author: H.L.A. Hart
books.why: The essential mixed-theory analysis of punishment.

books.title: Why Punish? How Much?
books.author: Michael Moore
books.why: The most rigorous contemporary retributivist defence.

books.title: Are Prisons Obsolete?
books.author: Angela Davis
books.why: The most important challenge to punitive incarceration.

completion.name: Qualia
completion.thinkerBio: Thomas Nagel (b. 1937) at NYU introduced the what-it's-like formulation in 'What Is It Like to Be a Bat?' (1974); Frank Jackson (b. 1943) developed the knowledge argument through Mary's room in 'Epiphenomenal Qualia' (1982) — Jackson later recanted but the argument remains foundational.
completion.quote: What it is like to see red — the redness of red — is not captured by any physical description of the visual system.

related.name: Hard Problem of Consciousness
related.note: Qualia are the specific phenomenal properties whose existence generates the hard problem.

related.name: Functionalism
related.note: Inverted and absent qualia arguments show that functional organization does not fix phenomenal character.

related.name: Chinese Room
related.note: The Chinese Room and qualia arguments both challenge the sufficiency of functional organization for genuine mental states.

related.name: Embodied Cognition
related.note: Embodied cognition theorists argue qualia are not inner mental objects but features of embedded, embodied engagement with the world.

books.title: Mortal Questions
books.author: Thomas Nagel
books.why: Contains 'What Is It Like to Be a Bat?' — the essay that put qualia at the center of analytic philosophy of mind.

books.title: Philosophy of Mind: Classical and Contemporary Readings
books.author: David Chalmers (ed.)
books.why: The essential anthology including Jackson's knowledge argument and all major responses.

books.title: There's Something About Mary
books.author: Peter Ludlow (ed.)
books.why: The anthology focused specifically on the knowledge argument and its implications for physicalism.

completion.name: Race and Political Philosophy
completion.thinkerBio: Charles Mills (1951-2021) developed the racial contract theory; W.E.B. Du Bois (1868-1963) developed double consciousness as its phenomenological complement.
completion.quote: The Racial Contract is the actual normative agreement that structured colonial societies while claiming universal principles.

related.name: Social Contract
related.note: Mills argues the canonical social contract tradition was systematically racially exclusive in application despite universal claims.

related.name: Pan-Africanism
related.note: Du Bois's double consciousness grounds the psychological complement to Mills's structural racial contract analysis.

related.name: Intersectionality and Political Theory
related.note: Collins's controlling images directly extends Mills's racial contract theory to cultural representation.

books.title: The Racial Contract
books.author: Charles Mills
books.why: Mills's foundational argument that the social contract tradition was racially exclusive.

books.title: The Souls of Black Folk
books.author: W.E.B. Du Bois
books.why: Du Bois's foundational development of double consciousness.

books.title: Race and the Enlightenment
books.author: Emmanuel Eze (ed.)
books.why: Documents the racist views embedded in canonical political philosophy.

completion.name: Recognition and Misrecognition
completion.thinkerBio: Axel Honneth (b. 1949) developed the philosophy of recognition drawing on Hegel's master-slave dialectic in The Struggle for Recognition (1992); Nancy Fraser (b. 1947) challenged the primacy of recognition over redistribution.
completion.quote: Misrecognition damages self-confidence, self-respect, and self-esteem -- denial of recognition in love, law, or solidarity produces distinctive psychological pathologies.

related.name: Phenomenology of Spirit
related.note: The master-slave dialectic in Hegel's Phenomenology is the founding text for the entire subsequent philosophy of recognition.

related.name: Multiculturalism
related.note: Taylor's politics of recognition is the philosophical foundation for multiculturalism -- misrecognition damages minority self-understanding.

related.name: Alienation and Reification
related.note: Honneth's recent reconstruction of reification theory returns to a phenomenology of prior recognition as the condition for any objective knowledge.

related.name: Justice and Equality
related.note: Fraser argues recognition cannot fully replace redistribution as the currency of justice -- both dimensions are co-primary and irreducible.

books.title: The Struggle for Recognition
books.author: Axel Honneth
books.why: Honneth's foundational development of recognition theory drawing on and extending Hegel's master-slave dialectic.

books.title: Redistribution or Recognition?
books.author: Nancy Fraser and Axel Honneth
books.why: The direct philosophical exchange between Fraser and Honneth on the relative primacy of recognition and redistribution.

books.title: Multiculturalism: Examining the Politics of Recognition
books.author: Charles Taylor et al.
books.why: Taylor's foundational application of recognition theory to multicultural politics.

completion.name: Reductionism
completion.thinkerBio: Ernest Nagel (1901-1985) developed the formal model of theory reduction; Jerry Fodor (1935-2017) challenged it through multiple realizability.
completion.quote: Chemistry reduces to physics; biology reduces to chemistry -- or does it?

related.name: Multiple Realizability
related.note: Multiple realizability is the central argument against strict psychophysical reduction.

related.name: Philosophy of Chemistry
related.note: Philosophy of chemistry directly tests whether chemical kinds reduce to quantum mechanics.

related.name: Complexity and Self-Organization
related.note: Complexity science challenges reductionism by showing complex behavior isn't derivable from component properties alone.

books.title: The Structure of Science
books.author: Ernest Nagel
books.why: Nagel's foundational formal model of scientific reduction.

books.title: Special Sciences
books.author: Jerry Fodor, in Synthese
books.why: Fodor's foundational argument for the autonomy of special sciences.

books.title: Philosophy of Mind
books.author: Jaegwon Kim
books.why: Clear textbook treatment of reductionism and its challenges.

completion.name: Reference and Rigid Designation
completion.thinkerBio: Saul Kripke (1940-2022) developed rigid designation in Naming and Necessity, transforming philosophy of language and metaphysics.
completion.quote: Names are rigid designators -- they refer to the same individual in all possible worlds in which that individual exists.

related.name: Names and Descriptions
related.note: Rigid designation is Kripke's direct challenge to Russell's description theory of names.

related.name: Essentialism
related.note: Rigid designation rehabilitated essentialism -- essential properties hold across all possible worlds.

related.name: Semantic Externalism
related.note: Putnam extended rigid designation from names to natural kind terms like 'water.'

books.title: Naming and Necessity
books.author: Saul Kripke
books.why: The foundational text developing rigid designation.

books.title: Reference and Existence
books.author: Saul Kripke
books.why: Kripke's later extension of his theory of reference.

books.title: Frege's Puzzle
books.author: Nathan Salmon
books.why: A rigorous development of direct reference theory's implications.

completion.name: Relevance and Communication
completion.thinkerBio: Dan Sperber (b. 1942) and Deirdre Wilson (b. 1941) developed relevance theory in Relevance: Communication and Cognition (1986), replacing Grice's multiple conversational maxims with a single cognitive principle of optimal relevance.
completion.quote: Every act of ostensive communication communicates a presumption of its own optimal relevance -- the greatest cognitive effects for the least processing effort.

related.name: Pragmatics
related.note: Relevance theory replaces Grice's four conversational maxims with a single unified cognitive principle explaining the full range of pragmatic phenomena.

related.name: Metaphor
related.note: Relevance theory explains metaphor as a case of optimal relevance achieved through loose, non-literal language rather than as a special rhetorical device requiring separate treatment.

related.name: Speech Act Theory
related.note: Both relevance theory and speech act theory investigate how utterances communicate more than their literal semantic content through the exploitation of pragmatic principles.

related.name: Theory of Mind
related.note: Relevance theory presupposes sophisticated theory of mind capacities -- interpreting an utterance requires inferring the speaker's intended meaning from their presumed communicative intention.

books.title: Relevance: Communication and Cognition
books.author: Dan Sperber and Deirdre Wilson
books.why: The foundational text developing relevance theory as a unified cognitive account of communication.

books.title: Meaning and Relevance
books.author: Deirdre Wilson and Dan Sperber
books.why: A more recent collection of essays extending and refining relevance theory across a range of linguistic phenomena.

books.title: Studies in the Way of Words
books.author: H.P. Grice
books.why: Grice's foundational essays on conversational implicature that relevance theory was developed to unify and explain.

completion.name: Reliabilism
completion.thinkerBio: Alvin Goldman (b. 1938) at Rutgers introduced process reliabilism in 'A Causal Theory of Knowing' (1967) and developed it fully in subsequent decades; he remains the most influential figure in externalist epistemology.
completion.quote: S knows that p if S's belief that p was produced by a reliable cognitive process.

related.name: The Gettier Problem
related.note: Reliabilism was developed partly to solve the Gettier problem -- reliability, not mere justification, is the additional condition required for knowledge.

related.name: Virtue Epistemology
related.note: Virtue epistemology can be understood as a refinement of reliabilism -- knowledge requires reliable processes that are also exercises of the agent's intellectual virtue.

related.name: Foundationalism
related.note: Reliabilism is externalist about justification, unlike traditional internalist foundationalism, which requires the believer to have access to their justifying reasons.

related.name: Naturalized Epistemology
related.note: Both reliabilism and naturalized epistemology ground epistemic norms in facts about cognitive processes studied by empirical psychology.

books.title: Epistemology and Cognition
books.author: Alvin Goldman
books.why: Goldman's mature systematic statement of reliabilism integrated with cognitive science.

books.title: Liaisons: Philosophy Meets the Cognitive and Social Sciences
books.author: Alvin Goldman
books.why: Goldman's later essays extending reliabilist epistemology into social epistemology.

books.title: Knowledge in a Social World
books.author: Alvin Goldman
books.why: Goldman's extension of reliabilist epistemology to social and political contexts.

completion.name: Reproductive Rights
completion.thinkerBio: Judith Jarvis Thomson (1929-2020) developed the violinist thought experiment defending abortion rights even granting fetal personhood; contemporary feminist philosophers including Susan Sherwin connect reproductive autonomy to broader bodily integrity and political equality.
completion.quote: Even granting that the fetus has a right to life, this does not establish that it has a right to use the woman's body -- bodily autonomy is a distinct and weighty consideration.

related.name: Feminist Political Philosophy
related.note: Reproductive autonomy is among the central concerns of contemporary feminist political philosophy, grounding bodily integrity as a matter of political equality.

related.name: Bioethics
related.note: Reproductive rights is a central application domain within bioethics, connecting to broader debates about autonomy, personhood, and bodily integrity.

related.name: Rights
related.note: Thomson's violinist argument is a sophisticated application of rights theory, distinguishing the right to life from the right to use another's body.

related.name: Embodiment and Feminist Philosophy
related.note: Both reproductive rights and feminist philosophy of embodiment center the lived, material body as a site of both vulnerability and political contestation.

books.title: A Defense of Abortion
books.author: Judith Jarvis Thomson, in The Rights and Wrongs of Abortion
books.why: Thomson's foundational violinist thought experiment defending abortion rights independent of the fetal personhood debate.

books.title: No Longer Patient
books.author: Susan Sherwin
books.why: Sherwin's feminist bioethics connecting reproductive autonomy to broader structures of medical and social power.

books.title: Killing in the Name of Identity
books.author: Cynthia Gorney
books.why: A journalistic but philosophically engaged account of the abortion debate's social and political dimensions.

completion.name: Republican Theory of Freedom
completion.thinkerBio: Quentin Skinner (b. 1940) at Cambridge reconstructed the neo-Roman republican tradition through intellectual history; Philip Pettit's systematic philosophical development complements Skinner's historical recovery of the tradition.
completion.quote: Freedom consists not merely in the absence of interference but in the absence of dependence on the arbitrary will of another.

related.name: Republicanism
related.note: This entry develops the historical intellectual recovery that complements Pettit's systematic philosophical reconstruction of republican freedom.

related.name: Liberalism
related.note: Republican freedom as non-domination is explicitly developed against the liberal conception of freedom as mere non-interference.

related.name: Sovereignty
related.note: Neo-Roman republicanism's concern with avoiding dependence on arbitrary will connects directly to debates about the proper limits of sovereign power.

related.name: Civil Disobedience
related.note: Republican freedom grounds a robust justification for resisting laws that constitute or enable domination, even when formally legal.

books.title: Liberty Before Liberalism
books.author: Quentin Skinner
books.why: Skinner's foundational intellectual history recovering the neo-Roman republican conception of liberty.

books.title: Republicanism: A Theory of Freedom and Government
books.author: Philip Pettit
books.why: The systematic philosophical companion to Skinner's historical recovery of republican freedom.

books.title: Machiavelli: A Very Short Introduction
books.author: Quentin Skinner
books.why: Skinner traces the republican tradition from its classical origins through Machiavelli's development of it.

completion.name: Republicanism
completion.thinkerBio: Philip Pettit (b. 1945) at Princeton reconstructed the republican tradition in Republicanism (1997); Hannah Arendt (1906–1975) developed a contrasting republican emphasis on political action and the public realm in The Human Condition (1958).
completion.quote: Freedom is not the absence of interference — it is the absence of arbitrary power over one's life.

related.name: Liberalism
related.note: Liberalism defines freedom as non-interference; republicanism argues this is insufficient — a non-interfering master still dominates.

related.name: Democracy
related.note: Non-domination requires democratic institutions that prevent any person or group from holding arbitrary power over others.

related.name: Power and Domination
related.note: Republicanism's concept of non-domination provides the most precise political analysis of what domination consists in and what institutions prevent it.

related.name: Civil Disobedience
related.note: Civil disobedience against tyranny is the republican paradigm of political action — principled resistance to domination.

books.title: Republicanism
books.author: Philip Pettit
books.why: The foundational text reconstructing republican political philosophy — freedom as non-domination.

books.title: The Human Condition
books.author: Hannah Arendt
books.why: Arendt's account of political action as freedom — the public realm as the space of human plurality and new beginnings.

books.title: Domination and the Arts of Resistance
books.author: James Scott
books.why: The empirical complement to republican theory — how dominated groups create and maintain resistance.

completion.name: Resoluteness
completion.thinkerBio: Martin Heidegger (1889-1976) developed resoluteness as the existential structure of authentic existence in Being and Time.
completion.quote: Resoluteness is the mode of owning one's thrownness from the standpoint of being-toward-death.

related.name: Authenticity
related.note: Resoluteness is the specific existential structure of authentic existence.

related.name: Thrownness
related.note: Resoluteness is the appropriate response to thrownness -- owning one's situation from genuine freedom.

related.name: Being-toward-death
related.note: Authentic resoluteness requires confronting being-toward-death.

books.title: Being and Time
books.author: Martin Heidegger
books.why: The source developing resoluteness within the existential analytic.

books.title: Heidegger and Unconcealment
books.author: Mark Wrathall
books.why: A clear guide to resoluteness and authenticity in Heidegger.

books.title: Heidegger: A Very Short Introduction
books.author: Michael Inwood
books.why: An accessible entry point to Heidegger's existential structures.

completion.name: Rights
completion.thinkerBio: John Locke (1632-1704) grounded natural rights in self-ownership; Ronald Dworkin (1931-2013) developed rights as 'trumps' over utilitarian calculation.
completion.quote: Rights are not goals to be maximized -- they are trumps that individuals hold against collective decisions.

related.name: Categorical Imperative
related.note: Kantian rights derive from the humanity formula's claim that persons create inviolable claims.

related.name: Social Contract
related.note: Social contract theory grounds rights as pre-political entitlements governments exist to protect.

related.name: Global Justice
related.note: Debates ask whether natural rights generate obligations across borders.

books.title: Two Treatises of Government
books.author: John Locke
books.why: The foundational natural rights text.

books.title: Taking Rights Seriously
books.author: Ronald Dworkin
books.why: Dworkin's influential rights-as-trumps account.

books.title: Anarchy, State, and Utopia
books.author: Robert Nozick
books.why: The most systematic libertarian rights theory.

completion.name: Sage Philosophy
completion.thinkerBio: Henry Odera Oruka (1944–1995) was a Kenyan philosopher at the University of Nairobi who developed sage philosophy as a methodology for recovering African individual philosophical thought; his Sage Philosophy (1990) demonstrated individual critical reflection in traditional sages outside academic institutions.
completion.quote: African sages philosophize on the nature of reality, on morality, on God and the good life — not as collective belief but as individual critical reflection.

related.name: Decolonizing Knowledge
related.note: Sage philosophy is a method of decolonizing knowledge — recovering philosophical thought that colonial systems denied existed.

related.name: Epistemic Injustice
related.note: The denial that African individuals engage in philosophy is a form of epistemic injustice — refusing the status of knower based on cultural identity.

related.name: African Logic and Reasoning
related.note: Both sage philosophy and African logic demonstrate that rigorous individual philosophical reasoning exists in African traditions.

related.name: Ordinary Language Philosophy
related.note: Both sage philosophy and ordinary language philosophy find philosophical wisdom in informal, non-academic discourse.

books.title: Sage Philosophy
books.author: Henry Odera Oruka
books.why: Oruka's foundational work — the methodology of sage philosophy and interviews with African philosophical sages.

books.title: African Philosophy: An Introduction
books.author: Richard Wright (ed.)
books.why: An anthology covering ethnophilosophy, sage philosophy, and professional African philosophy.

books.title: Contemporary African Philosophy
books.author: Leonard Harris (ed.)
books.why: Essays by African philosophers representing different methodological approaches including sage philosophy.

completion.name: Samkhya Dualism
completion.thinkerBio: Kapila (traditional founder, dates uncertain) and Ishvarakrishna (c. fourth century CE), author of the Samkhyakarika, developed the classical Samkhya system positing a strict dualism between pure consciousness (Purusha) and material nature (Prakriti).
completion.quote: Liberation consists in the discriminative knowledge that distinguishes Purusha from Prakriti, recognizing that consciousness was never truly bound by matter.

related.name: Substance Dualism
related.note: Samkhya's Purusha-Prakriti dualism and Cartesian substance dualism both posit two fundamentally distinct kinds of reality, consciousness and matter, though through different arguments.

related.name: Yoga Philosophy
related.note: Patanjali's Yoga philosophy accepts the Samkhya metaphysical framework and adds a systematic practical methodology and theistic element for achieving liberation.

related.name: Moksha
related.note: Samkhya's account of liberation through discriminative knowledge (viveka) is one of the classical Hindu paths to moksha alongside devotion and selfless action.

related.name: The Bhagavad Gita and Action
related.note: The Bhagavad Gita's metaphysical backdrop draws significantly on Samkhya's account of self, matter, and the path to liberation through discriminative knowledge.

books.title: Samkhya: A Dualist Tradition in Indian Philosophy
books.author: Gerald Larson
books.why: The definitive scholarly study of Samkhya philosophy, its history, and its metaphysical commitments.

books.title: Classical Samkhya
books.author: Gerald Larson
books.why: Larson's detailed analysis of the Samkhyakarika and the systematic development of Samkhya cosmology.

books.title: The Yoga Sutras of Patanjali
books.author: trans. Edwin Bryant
books.why: Essential context -- Patanjali's Yoga accepts and builds directly on the Samkhya metaphysical framework.

completion.name: Scientific Explanation
completion.thinkerBio: Carl Hempel (1905–1997) developed the deductive-nomological model in 'Studies in the Logic of Explanation' (1948) with Paul Oppenheim; Wesley Salmon (1925–2001) at Pittsburgh proposed the causal-mechanical alternative in Scientific Explanation and the Causal Structure of the World (1984).
completion.quote: To explain a phenomenon is to subsume it under a general law — to show that it had to happen given the conditions and the laws.

related.name: Laws of Nature
related.note: Scientific explanation on the DN model requires laws of nature — the nomological component that gives the explanandum its necessity.

related.name: Causation in Science
related.note: Salmon's causal-mechanical account makes causation the core of explanation — to explain is to exhibit the mechanism.

related.name: Reductionism
related.note: Reductive explanation is one important form — explaining higher-level phenomena by deriving them from lower-level theories and laws.

related.name: Emergence
related.note: Emergence poses a challenge — emergent properties may not be explicable by any combination of laws and initial conditions.

books.title: Aspects of Scientific Explanation
books.author: Carl Hempel
books.why: The primary source — Hempel's definitive statement of the DN model and its variants.

books.title: Scientific Explanation and the Causal Structure of the World
books.author: Wesley Salmon
books.why: Salmon's causal-mechanical alternative to the DN model — the most influential contemporary account.

books.title: The Explanation of Behaviour
books.author: Charles Taylor
books.why: Taylor's analysis of teleological explanation in biology and psychology — a challenge to purely mechanical accounts.

completion.name: Scientific Knowledge and Values
completion.thinkerBio: Heather Douglas (b. 1972) developed the indirect role for values in scientific reasoning under uncertainty; Helen Longino (b. 1944) developed contextual empiricism.
completion.quote: When evidence is inconclusive, scientists must weigh the consequences of error in each direction, requiring value judgments about which errors are worse.

related.name: Feminist Philosophy of Science
related.note: Longino's contextual empiricism directly grounds feminist philosophy of science's account of value-laden background assumptions.

related.name: Scientific Realism
related.note: If values partially constitute scientific reasoning, this complicates simple realist pictures of value-free scientific truth-tracking.

related.name: Theory-Ladenness of Observation
related.note: Douglas's indirect role for values extends theory-ladenness to show values, not just theories, shape scientific judgment under uncertainty.

books.title: Science, Policy, and the Value-Free Ideal
books.author: Heather Douglas
books.why: Douglas's foundational argument for the indirect role of values in science.

books.title: The Fate of Knowledge
books.author: Helen Longino
books.why: Longino's contextual empiricism grounding value-laden scientific objectivity.

books.title: Is Science Value Free?
books.author: Hugh Lacey
books.why: A comprehensive philosophical treatment of the value-free ideal debate.

completion.name: Scientific Method
completion.thinkerBio: Francis Bacon (1561-1626) developed inductivism; Karl Popper (1902-1994) replaced it with hypothetico-deductive conjecture and refutation.
completion.quote: Science proceeds by bold conjecture and rigorous attempted refutation, not by accumulation of confirming instances.

related.name: Falsifiability
related.note: Popper's hypothetico-deductivism is his alternative to Baconian inductivism as an account of scientific method.

related.name: Paradigm Shift
related.note: Kuhn showed actual scientific practice doesn't follow any single universal method.

related.name: Scientific Revolutions Reconsidered
related.note: Lakatos and Feyerabend both develop and critique the idea of a single scientific method.

books.title: Novum Organum
books.author: Francis Bacon
books.why: Bacon's foundational inductivist account of scientific method.

books.title: The Logic of Scientific Discovery
books.author: Karl Popper
books.why: Popper's replacement of induction with conjecture and refutation.

books.title: Against Method
books.author: Paul Feyerabend
books.why: The most radical challenge to the idea of a single scientific method.

completion.name: Scientific Realism
completion.thinkerBio: Hilary Putnam (1926–2016) formulated the no-miracles argument for scientific realism; Richard Boyd (b. 1942) developed a sophisticated causal scientific realism; Bas van Fraassen (b. 1941) at Princeton produced the most rigorous contemporary anti-realist alternative in The Scientific Image (1980).
completion.quote: The only philosophy that doesn't make the success of science a miracle is realism.

related.name: Underdetermination
related.note: Underdetermination challenges scientific realism — if multiple incompatible theories are always empirically equivalent, realism is unjustified.

related.name: Inference to Best Explanation
related.note: The no-miracles argument is an IBE — the best explanation of scientific success is that our theories are approximately true.

related.name: Falsifiability
related.note: Scientific realism requires that successful theories be falsifiable — if there is genuine truth to track, theories must make risky testable predictions.

related.name: Social Studies of Science
related.note: Social constructivism challenges scientific realism — scientific facts are constructed through social practices, not discovered in mind-independent reality.

books.title: The Scientific Image
books.author: Bas van Fraassen
books.why: The most rigorous contemporary alternative to scientific realism — constructive empiricism.

books.title: Scientific Realism
books.author: Jarrett Leplin (ed.)
books.why: The essential anthology covering the main positions in the scientific realism debate.

books.title: Representing and Intervening
books.author: Ian Hacking
books.why: Hacking's entity realism — a balanced philosophical treatment of observation, experiment, and theoretical commitment.

completion.name: Scientific Revolutions Reconsidered
completion.thinkerBio: Imre Lakatos (1922-1974) developed sophisticated falsificationism through the concept of scientific research programmes; Paul Feyerabend (1924-1994) developed epistemological anarchism, arguing no universal scientific method has ever actually governed scientific progress.
completion.quote: Anything goes -- every methodological rule that has been proposed has been violated during actual scientific revolutions that nonetheless produced genuine progress.

related.name: Paradigm Shift
related.note: Lakatos's sophisticated falsificationism and Feyerabend's anarchism are both responses to the historical and philosophical challenges posed by Kuhn's account of paradigm shifts.

related.name: Falsifiability
related.note: Feyerabend's epistemological anarchism directly challenges Popper's falsifiability criterion by showing that successful science has routinely violated it.

related.name: The Demarcation Problem
related.note: Lakatos's progressive versus degenerative research programmes represents the most historically sophisticated refinement of the demarcation criterion.

related.name: Theory-Ladenness of Observation
related.note: Feyerabend's radical conclusions partly follow from taking theory-ladenness to its logical extreme, denying any neutral standard for evaluating competing paradigms.

books.title: The Methodology of Scientific Research Programmes
books.author: Imre Lakatos
books.why: Lakatos's foundational development of sophisticated falsificationism and research programme methodology.

books.title: Against Method
books.author: Paul Feyerabend
books.why: Feyerabend's foundational and provocative development of epistemological anarchism.

books.title: Criticism and the Growth of Knowledge
books.author: Imre Lakatos and Alan Musgrave (eds.)
books.why: The famous 1965 colloquium where Popper, Kuhn, Lakatos, and Feyerabend debated the structure of scientific change.

completion.name: Self-Consciousness and Apperception
completion.thinkerBio: Immanuel Kant (1724-1804) developed the transcendental unity of apperception; Johann Gottlieb Fichte (1762-1814) radicalized it into the self-positing act of the I.
completion.quote: The 'I think' must be able to accompany all my representations -- a formal condition for the unity of experience.

related.name: Cogito Ergo Sum
related.note: Kant's transcendental apperception is the Kantian transformation of the Cartesian cogito.

related.name: The Self as Construction
related.note: Both apperception debates and contemporary self-construction debates ask what unifies subjective experience.

related.name: Idealism
related.note: Fichte's self-positing I grounds his development of German idealism after Kant.

books.title: Critique of Pure Reason
books.author: Immanuel Kant
books.why: The primary source developing transcendental apperception.

books.title: Science of Knowledge
books.author: J.G. Fichte
books.why: Fichte's radicalization of apperception into self-positing.

books.title: Self-Consciousness
books.author: Sebastian Rodl
books.why: A rigorous contemporary treatment connecting Kantian apperception to analytic philosophy of mind.

completion.name: Self-Determination
completion.thinkerBio: Self-determination as a political and legal principle developed through twentieth-century decolonization movements; its philosophical articulation draws on Wilsonian internationalism, Leninist anti-imperialism, and the UN Charter's codification of the principle.
completion.quote: All peoples have the right to self-determination -- by virtue of that right they freely determine their political status.

related.name: Nationalism
related.note: Self-determination is the political principle that operationalizes nationalist claims to nationhood into rights to sovereign political organization.

related.name: Colonialism and Postcolonialism
related.note: Self-determination became the central legal and political principle driving decolonization movements across Africa, Asia, and the Caribbean.

related.name: Sovereignty
related.note: Self-determination grounds claims to sovereignty for peoples seeking independence from colonial or otherwise illegitimate rule.

related.name: Pan-Africanism
related.note: Pan-Africanism's demand for African self-determination was among the most consequential applications of the principle in twentieth-century politics.

books.title: Self-Determination and National Minorities
books.author: Margaret Moore
books.why: A rigorous philosophical analysis of competing accounts of the right to self-determination.

books.title: National Self-Determination and Secession
books.author: Margaret Moore (ed.)
books.why: An anthology examining the philosophical justification and limits of self-determination claims.

books.title: The Wretched of the Earth
books.author: Frantz Fanon
books.why: Fanon's account of the struggle for self-determination from the perspective of anticolonial revolution.

completion.name: Semantic Externalism
completion.thinkerBio: Hilary Putnam (1926-2016) introduced the Twin Earth thought experiment in 'The Meaning of Meaning' (1975); Saul Kripke's earlier work on rigid designation provided the foundational framework that Putnam extended to natural kind terms.
