completion.quote: Meanings just ain't in the head -- the reference of natural kind terms is partly constituted by the actual nature of things in the world, not by mental representations alone.

related.name: Reference and Rigid Designation
related.note: Putnam's semantic externalism extends Kripke's rigid designation theory from proper names to natural kind terms like 'water.'

related.name: Essentialism
related.note: Semantic externalism supports scientific essentialism -- the actual underlying nature of a kind, not surface appearance, determines what a term refers to.

related.name: The Intentionality of Consciousness
related.note: Externalism challenges traditional internalist accounts of intentionality, which locate the content of mental states entirely within the head.

related.name: Language of Thought
related.note: If meaning depends partly on external facts about the world, this complicates Fodor's picture of thought as purely internal symbol manipulation in a language of thought.

books.title: Mind, Language and Reality
books.author: Hilary Putnam
books.why: Contains Putnam's foundational essay 'The Meaning of Meaning' introducing semantic externalism and the Twin Earth thought experiment.

books.title: Naming and Necessity
books.author: Saul Kripke
books.why: Kripke's foundational work on rigid designation that provides the framework Putnam extends to natural kind terms.

books.title: Externalism and Self-Knowledge
books.author: Peter Ludlow and Norah Martin (eds.)
books.why: An anthology examining the implications of semantic externalism for self-knowledge and philosophy of mind.

completion.name: Shintoism and Nature
completion.thinkerBio: Shinto is Japan's indigenous religious tradition, characterized by reverence for kami -- the sacred forces present in natural phenomena, ancestral spirits, and exceptional persons.
completion.quote: The sacred is not transcendent but present within the world -- in mountains, rivers, forests, and human communities.

related.name: Taoism and Nature
related.note: Both Shinto and Taoism ground ethics in reverence for and alignment with natural process rather than domination over it.

related.name: Environmental Ethics
related.note: Shinto's immanent sacred grounds an ethics of attentiveness toward nature that parallels contemporary environmental philosophy.

related.name: Process Philosophy
related.note: Shinto's sense of the sacred as immanent within dynamic natural process has affinities with process metaphysics.

books.title: Shinto: The Kami Way
books.author: Sokyo Ono
books.why: An accessible foundational introduction to Shinto belief and practice.

books.title: A Popular Dictionary of Shinto
books.author: Brian Bocking
books.why: A reference resource for Shinto concepts and their philosophical dimensions.

books.title: The Essence of Shinto
books.author: Motohisa Yamakage
books.why: A contemporary practitioner's philosophical account of Shinto's relationship to nature.

completion.name: Situation
completion.thinkerBio: Jean-Paul Sartre and Simone de Beauvoir (1908-1986) developed situation as the concrete context within which freedom is exercised, refined especially by de Beauvoir.
completion.quote: Freedom is always situated -- exercised within and against material and social constraints, not in an abstract vacuum.

related.name: Freedom and Responsibility
related.note: Situation refines Sartrean freedom by attending to material and social constraint.

related.name: Embodied Freedom
related.note: Embodied freedom develops situation specifically through the lens of bodily comportment.

related.name: The Second Sex
related.note: De Beauvoir applies situation to analyze how patriarchal structures constrain women's freedom.

books.title: The Ethics of Ambiguity
books.author: Simone de Beauvoir
books.why: De Beauvoir's fullest philosophical development of situated freedom.

books.title: The Second Sex
books.author: Simone de Beauvoir
books.why: Situation applied concretely to women's condition under patriarchy.

books.title: Being and Nothingness
books.author: Jean-Paul Sartre
books.why: Sartre's original development of situated freedom within facticity.

completion.name: Skepticism
completion.thinkerBio: Pyrrho of Elis (c. 360–270 BCE) founded ancient skepticism; René Descartes (1596–1650) developed modern skepticism as a method; David Hume (1711–1776) made the most trenchant modern case for its inescapability in matters of induction and causation.
completion.quote: If you would be a real seeker after truth, it is necessary that at least once in your life you doubt, as far as possible, all things.

related.name: Cogito Ergo Sum
related.note: The cogito is Descartes's proposed escape — the one truth that survives even the evil demon hypothesis.

related.name: Foundationalism
related.note: Skepticism motivates foundationalism — if no belief is certain, we must find something certain or skepticism wins.

related.name: Epistemic Humility
related.note: Both begin from recognition of the limits of human knowledge, though humility leads to calibrated confidence while skepticism leads to suspension of judgment.

related.name: The Gettier Problem
related.note: Gettier cases extend skeptical pressure into ordinary contexts — even justified true belief falls short of knowledge.

books.title: The Skeptics
books.author: R.J. Hankinson
books.why: Comprehensive history of ancient skepticism from Pyrrho through the Academic skeptics.

books.title: Skepticism and the Veil of Perception
books.author: Michael Huemer
books.why: A contemporary direct realist response to Cartesian skepticism that takes the challenge seriously.

books.title: Knowledge and Its Limits
books.author: Timothy Williamson
books.why: Williamson's externalist approach — treating knowledge as primitive — offers a contemporary response to skeptical challenges.

completion.name: Social Contract
completion.thinkerBio: Thomas Hobbes (1588–1679) grounded the contract in fear of violent death; John Locke (1632–1704) grounded it in natural rights; Jean-Jacques Rousseau (1712–1778) grounded it in the general will — together they represent the three major versions of social contract theory.
completion.quote: Man is born free, and everywhere he is in chains.

related.name: Original Position
related.note: Rawls's original position is the most rigorous modern transformation of the social contract tradition — a thought experiment about the standpoint of fairness.

related.name: Liberalism
related.note: Classical liberalism is built on the Lockean social contract — governments formed to protect pre-political rights can be resisted when they violate them.

related.name: Anarchism
related.note: Anarchism holds that no contract could legitimately authorize a state — voluntary agreement can ground only voluntary cooperation, not coercive authority.

related.name: Political Obligation
related.note: The social contract is the standard theory of political obligation — we are bound to obey because we have consented, actually or hypothetically.

books.title: Leviathan
books.author: Thomas Hobbes
books.why: Hobbes's full argument for sovereign authority from the state of nature — the most rigorous defence of absolute government.

books.title: Two Treatises of Government
books.author: John Locke
books.why: Locke's argument for limited government and the right of revolution — the foundational liberal text.

books.title: The Social Contract
books.author: Jean-Jacques Rousseau
books.why: Rousseau's general will and the idea that genuine political freedom requires participating in collective self-legislation.

completion.name: Social Epistemology
completion.thinkerBio: Alvin Goldman (b. 1938) founded contemporary social epistemology with Knowledge in a Social World (1999); Helen Longino (b. 1944) developed a parallel feminist social epistemology of science emphasizing communal critical norms.
completion.quote: Most of what any individual knows, they know because they trust others, not because they verified it themselves.

related.name: Testimony
related.note: Testimony is the central case studied by social epistemology -- the social transmission of belief through trust rather than direct observation.

related.name: Standpoint Epistemology
related.note: Both fields examine how social structures and power relations shape the production and distribution of knowledge.

related.name: Epistemic Injustice
related.note: Epistemic injustice extends social epistemology to the fair distribution of epistemic credibility and conceptual resources.

related.name: Paradigm Shift
related.note: Kuhn's account of paradigms as social phenomena anticipates social epistemology's emphasis on community structure in knowledge production.

books.title: Knowledge in a Social World
books.author: Alvin Goldman
books.why: The foundational systematic text of contemporary social epistemology.

books.title: The Fate of Knowledge
books.author: Helen Longino
books.why: Longino's social epistemology of science -- objectivity as a property of communities, not individuals.

books.title: Social Epistemology: Essential Readings
books.author: Alvin Goldman and Dennis Whitcomb (eds.)
books.why: The essential anthology covering the full range of contemporary social epistemology.

completion.name: Social Ontology
completion.thinkerBio: John Searle (b. 1932) developed the theory of institutional facts and status functions in The Construction of Social Reality (1995); Raimo Tuomela (1940-2020) developed a complementary account focused on collective acceptance.
completion.quote: Social entities are constituted by collective agreements of the form 'X counts as Y in context C' -- money is paper that counts as currency.

related.name: Speech Act Theory
related.note: Searle's account of institutional facts is built directly on the speech act framework.

related.name: Collective Intentionality
related.note: Social ontology requires collective intentionality -- shared intentions that cannot be reduced to aggregated individual intentions.

related.name: Power and Domination
related.note: Both social ontology and theories of power examine how social structures are constructed and maintained.

related.name: Feminist Metaphysics
related.note: Haslanger's social ontology of gender applies the broader framework to the specific case of gender.

books.title: The Construction of Social Reality
books.author: John Searle
books.why: Searle's foundational text on institutional facts and status functions.

books.title: The Philosophy of Sociality
books.author: Raimo Tuomela
books.why: Tuomela's development of collective acceptance and we-intentions.

books.title: Making the Social World
books.author: John Searle
books.why: Searle's later, more accessible synthesis of his social ontology.

completion.name: Social Studies of Science
completion.thinkerBio: Bruno Latour (1947-2022) and Steve Woolgar (b. 1950) founded laboratory ethnography in Laboratory Life (1979); Harry Collins (b. 1943) developed the study of tacit knowledge in scientific practice through decades of fieldwork on gravitational wave detection.
completion.quote: Scientific facts are constructed through networks of human and non-human actors -- they become robust as more elements are enrolled to support them.

related.name: Paradigm Shift
related.note: Kuhn's account of paradigms as social phenomena opened the door to the sociological approaches Latour and Collins developed further.

related.name: Theory-Ladenness of Observation
related.note: Theory-ladenness supports the constructivist insight that what scientists observe is shaped by social and theoretical context, not given neutrally.

related.name: Scientific Realism
related.note: Social constructivism challenges scientific realism -- facts are stabilized through networks of actors, not simply discovered in mind-independent reality.

related.name: Power-Knowledge
related.note: Both Foucauldian power-knowledge and actor-network theory examine how knowledge production is inseparable from networks of power and social practice.

books.title: Laboratory Life
books.author: Bruno Latour and Steve Woolgar
books.why: The foundational ethnographic study treating scientists as an anthropologist would study an unfamiliar culture.

books.title: We Have Never Been Modern
books.author: Bruno Latour
books.why: Latour's mature actor-network theory and his critique of the nature-culture divide.

books.title: Changing Order
books.author: Harry Collins
books.why: Collins's foundational study of tacit knowledge and replication in experimental science.

completion.name: Socialism
completion.thinkerBio: Karl Marx (1818-1883) offered the most influential theoretical framework for socialism through his analysis of surplus value and exploitation; Robert Owen (1771-1858) and Charles Fourier (1772-1837) developed earlier utopian socialist visions of cooperative community.
completion.quote: From each according to his ability, to each according to his needs.

related.name: Alienation
related.note: Marx's critique of alienation under capitalism is the foundation of his argument that socialism would supersede capitalism's structural contradictions.

related.name: Dialectics
related.note: Marx's materialist dialectic explains socialism as the historical successor to capitalism, arising from capitalism's internal contradictions.

related.name: Justice and Equality
related.note: Socialist theories of justice extend egalitarianism to argue that structural features of capitalist property relations are themselves sources of injustice.

related.name: Hegemony
related.note: Gramsci's hegemony explains why socialist revolution did not occur as predicted -- capitalist culture maintains consent that mere economic analysis could not anticipate.

books.title: Capital: Volume 1
books.author: Karl Marx
books.why: Marx's foundational analysis of capitalist exploitation through the theory of surplus value.

books.title: The Communist Manifesto
books.author: Karl Marx and Friedrich Engels
books.why: The most accessible and historically consequential statement of socialist political theory.

books.title: Socialism: Utopian and Scientific
books.author: Friedrich Engels
books.why: Engels's account of the development from utopian socialism (Owen, Fourier) to Marxist scientific socialism.

completion.name: Sovereignty
completion.thinkerBio: Jean Bodin (1530-1596) developed the modern concept of sovereignty during the French Wars of Religion in Six Books of the Commonwealth (1576); Carl Schmitt (1888-1985) radicalized it in the twentieth century through his theory of the sovereign as the one who decides on the exception.
completion.quote: Sovereign is he who decides on the exception.

related.name: Social Contract
related.note: Hobbes's social contract theory grounds the necessity of absolute sovereignty as the only alternative to the chaos of the state of nature.

related.name: Biopower
related.note: Foucault explicitly contrasted sovereign power, which decides life and death, with biopower, which administers and optimizes life.

related.name: Biopolitics and Bare Life
related.note: Agamben draws directly on Schmitt's theory of sovereignty to analyze how modern states produce bare life through the state of exception.

related.name: Republicanism
related.note: Republican theory's concern with non-domination challenges Schmittian sovereignty's celebration of decisionistic, potentially arbitrary executive power.

books.title: On Sovereignty
books.author: Jean Bodin
books.why: The foundational early modern text developing the concept of indivisible sovereign authority.

books.title: Political Theology
books.author: Carl Schmitt
books.why: Schmitt's radical twentieth-century theory of sovereignty as the decision on the state of exception.

books.title: Homo Sacer
books.author: Giorgio Agamben
books.why: Agamben's development of Schmittian sovereignty into a theory of bare life and the camp as the paradigm of modern politics.

completion.name: Speech Act Revisited
completion.thinkerBio: Rae Langton (b. 1961) and Rebecca Kukla (b. 1971) developed revisionist extensions of Austinian speech act theory examining how speech constitutes social hierarchies through directive utterances and the silencing of marginalized speakers.
completion.quote: The same words function differently as commands, requests, or suggestions depending on the social position of speaker and listener -- speech acts are shaped by social testure.

related.name: Speech Act Theory
related.note: This entry develops Austin's original framework in explicitly political and feminist directions, examining how speech constitutes social hierarchy.

related.name: Feminist Philosophy of Language
related.note: Langton's silencing thesis is the central case study developed at length in feminist philosophy of language's application of speech act theory.

related.name: Speech and Power
related.note: Both this entry and the broader topic of speech and power examine how language constitutes rather than merely describes social and political reality.

related.name: Power and Domination
related.note: Kukla's social testures and Lukes's three-dimensional power both examine how power operates through structures that shape interaction without requiring explicit coercion.

books.title: Sexual Solipsism
books.author: Rae Langton
books.why: Langton's collected essays developing her revisionist speech act analysis of pornography, objectification, and silencing.

books.title: Performative Force, Convention, and Discursive Injustice
books.author: Rebecca Kukla, in Hypatia
books.why: Kukla's influential paper on social testures, included alongside related work in Langton's collection for accessibility.

books.title: How to Do Things with Words
books.author: J.L. Austin
books.why: The foundational text that Langton and Kukla's revisionist extensions build upon and politically transform.

completion.name: Speech Act Theory
completion.thinkerBio: J.L. Austin (1911–1960) developed speech act theory at Oxford in How to Do Things with Words (1962, posthumous); John Searle (b. 1932) at Berkeley systematized and extended the theory, connecting it to intentionality, social ontology, and the nature of consciousness.
completion.quote: To say something is to do something.

related.name: Pragmatics
related.note: Grice's conversational implicature theory is the pragmatic development of Austin's insight that utterances do more than state facts.

related.name: Social Ontology
related.note: Searle's account of how institutional facts are constituted by status function declarations is built on the speech act framework.

related.name: Feminist Philosophy of Language
related.note: Langton's silencing thesis and analysis of pornography as a speech act develop Austin's performative framework in feminist directions.

related.name: Speech and Power
related.note: Both Habermas and Butler build on Austin's insight that language constitutes as well as describes social reality.

books.title: How to Do Things with Words
books.author: J.L. Austin
books.why: The foundational text — Austin's development of locutionary, illocutionary, and perlocutionary acts.

books.title: Speech Acts
books.author: John Searle
books.why: Searle's systematic development of Austin's framework with a full taxonomy of illocutionary acts.

books.title: The Construction of Social Reality
books.author: John Searle
books.why: Searle's account of how speech acts constitute institutional reality — money, government, marriage.

completion.name: Speech and Power
completion.thinkerBio: Jurgen Habermas (b. 1929) developed communicative action as a normative ideal aimed at mutual understanding; Judith Butler (b. 1956) developed performativity, arguing speech constitutes and enacts power rather than merely communicating about it.
completion.quote: Speech does not merely communicate about power -- the performative utterance that creates a social fact demonstrates that language is never merely instrumental.

related.name: Speech Act Theory
related.note: Both Habermas's communicative action and Butler's performativity build on Austin's insight that language constitutes social reality.

related.name: Gender Performativity
related.note: Butler's account of speech and power is the general framework from which gender performativity is derived.

related.name: Deliberative Democracy
related.note: Habermas's communicative action grounds his broader normative theory of deliberative democracy.

related.name: Feminist Philosophy of Language
related.note: Langton's silencing thesis is a specific application of how speech constitutes relations of power.

books.title: The Theory of Communicative Action
books.author: Jurgen Habermas
books.why: Habermas's foundational account of communication aimed at mutual understanding rather than strategic power.

books.title: Excitable Speech
books.author: Judith Butler
books.why: Butler's foundational development of how speech constitutes and enacts power.

books.title: Gender Trouble
books.author: Judith Butler
books.why: Butler's broader performativity framework grounding her account of speech and power.

completion.name: Standpoint Epistemology
completion.thinkerBio: Nancy Hartsock (1943–2015) developed standpoint epistemology from a Marxist feminist framework in 'The Feminist Standpoint' (1983); Sandra Harding (b. 1935) extended it into a comprehensive feminist philosophy of science in The Science Question in Feminism (1986).
completion.quote: The standpoint of the oppressed provides not merely a different perspective but an epistemically superior vantage point on certain features of social reality.

related.name: Feminist Philosophy of Science
related.note: Standpoint epistemology is the foundational claim of feminist philosophy of science — marginalized standpoints generate questions and knowledge mainstream science misses.

related.name: Epistemic Injustice
related.note: Epistemic injustice is the harm done when marginalized standpoints are denied epistemic credibility — the negative side of what standpoint theory identifies.

related.name: Social Epistemology
related.note: Standpoint epistemology is a specific contribution to social epistemology — it shows how social position systematically shapes epistemic access.

related.name: Intersectionality
related.note: Intersectionality complexifies standpoint epistemology — standpoints are intersectional, compounding different epistemic advantages and disadvantages.

books.title: Whose Science? Whose Knowledge?
books.author: Sandra Harding
books.why: Harding's comprehensive development of standpoint epistemology for feminist philosophy of science.

books.title: The Feminist Standpoint Theory Reader
books.author: Sandra Harding (ed.)
books.why: An anthology collecting the foundational texts of standpoint epistemology from Hartsock through Collins.

books.title: The Science Question in Feminism
books.author: Sandra Harding
books.why: Harding's earlier but more accessible introduction to feminist epistemology and standpoint theory.

completion.name: State of Nature
completion.thinkerBio: Thomas Hobbes (1588-1679) depicted the state of nature as a war of all against all in Leviathan; John Locke (1632-1704) and Jean-Jacques Rousseau (1712-1778) developed sharply different and more benign accounts grounding their contrasting political conclusions.
completion.quote: In the state of nature, life is solitary, poor, nasty, brutish, and short -- the absence of common power generates constant insecurity.

related.name: Social Contract
related.note: The state of nature is the pre-political condition against which the social contract is measured.

related.name: Anarchism
related.note: Anarchism implicitly denies Hobbes's pessimistic state of nature, holding voluntary cooperation can sustain order.

related.name: Liberalism
related.note: Locke's more benign state of nature grounds his liberal argument for limited rather than absolute government.

related.name: Sovereignty
related.note: Hobbes's state of nature directly grounds his argument for absolute sovereign authority.

books.title: Leviathan
books.author: Thomas Hobbes
books.why: Hobbes's foundational and most pessimistic depiction of the state of nature.

books.title: Two Treatises of Government
books.author: John Locke
books.why: Locke's more benign state of nature, already governed by natural law and rights.

books.title: Discourse on Inequality
books.author: Jean-Jacques Rousseau
books.why: Rousseau's account of natural goodness corrupted by civilization.

completion.name: Stoic Ethics
completion.thinkerBio: Zeno of Citium (334-262 BCE) founded Stoicism; Epictetus (c. 50-135 CE), Seneca (4 BCE-65 CE), and Marcus Aurelius (121-180 CE) produced the most influential Roman Stoic texts, emphasizing virtue, reason, and acceptance of what is beyond one's control.
completion.quote: Some things are within our power, while others are not. Within our power are opinion, motivation, desire -- in a word, everything that is our own doing.

related.name: Eudaimonia
related.note: Stoics held virtue alone sufficient for eudaimonia; Aristotle required external goods including health, friendship, and fortune for genuine flourishing.

related.name: Wu Wei
related.note: Both Stoic amor fati and Taoist wu wei counsel aligning oneself with the natural order rather than straining against it, though from different metaphysical starting points.

related.name: The Bhagavad Gita and Action
related.note: Both the Gita's nishkama karma and Stoic ethics counsel performing one's duty fully while remaining detached from outcomes outside one's control.

related.name: Dukkha
related.note: Both Stoicism and Buddhism identify attachment to externals as a primary source of suffering, though their prescriptions and metaphysics differ significantly.

books.title: Meditations
books.author: Marcus Aurelius
books.why: The most personally compelling Stoic text -- a Roman emperor's private philosophical journal on virtue and acceptance.

books.title: Discourses and Selected Writings
books.author: Epictetus
books.why: The foundational Stoic text on the dichotomy of control and the practical cultivation of equanimity.

books.title: A Guide to the Good Life
books.author: William Irvine
books.why: A clear contemporary introduction to Stoic philosophy and its practical application to modern life.

completion.name: Structuralism
completion.thinkerBio: Ferdinand de Saussure (1857–1913) established structural linguistics in Course in General Linguistics (1916); Claude Lévi-Strauss (1908–2009) applied the structural method to anthropology — together they define the structuralist movement that transformed the humanities.
completion.quote: In language there are only differences, with no positive terms.

related.name: Deconstruction
related.note: Derrida extends Saussure's insight that meaning is differential — then shows the structure itself is unstable.

related.name: Hermeneutics
related.note: Structuralism seeks universal structural laws beneath surface diversity; hermeneutics insists on the irreducibility of historical particularity.

related.name: Language Games
related.note: Both Wittgenstein and Saussure ground meaning in difference and use rather than in a direct relation between names and things.

related.name: The Rhizome
related.note: Deleuze and Guattari's rhizome concept is an explicit critique of arborescent, hierarchical structuralism.

books.title: Course in General Linguistics
books.author: Ferdinand de Saussure
books.why: The foundational text of structural linguistics — Saussure's account of the sign, difference, and the arbitrary nature of language.

books.title: Structural Anthropology
books.author: Claude Lévi-Strauss
books.why: The application of structural method to myth, kinship, and culture — defines the high point of structuralism.

books.title: The Linguistic Turn
books.author: Richard Rorty (ed.)
books.why: The essential anthology situating structuralism within the broader linguistic turn in twentieth-century philosophy.

completion.name: Substance Dualism
completion.thinkerBio: René Descartes (1596–1650) was a French philosopher and mathematician who inaugurated modern philosophy through radical doubt; his Meditations on First Philosophy (1641) remains the canonical statement of the mind-body problem.
completion.quote: I think, therefore I am.

related.name: Hard Problem of Consciousness
related.note: The hard problem is the modern successor to Cartesian dualism — both hold that subjective experience cannot be explained in purely physical terms.

related.name: Functionalism
related.note: Functionalism holds mental states are defined by causal roles realizable in multiple substrates — a direct challenge to the Cartesian immaterial mind.

related.name: Panpsychism
related.note: Both are non-physicalist positions that take consciousness seriously as irreducible to physical description.

related.name: Embodied Cognition
related.note: Embodied cognition directly challenges Descartes's picture of the mind as an unextended thinking substance that merely inhabits the body.

books.title: Meditations on First Philosophy
books.author: René Descartes
books.why: The primary source — Descartes's six meditations argue from radical doubt to substance dualism.

books.title: The Conscious Mind
books.author: David Chalmers
books.why: The most rigorous contemporary defence of non-physicalism — takes the intuitions behind dualism seriously.

books.title: Philosophy of Mind
books.author: Jaegwon Kim
books.why: The clearest textbook introduction to the mind-body problem and its major positions.

completion.name: Substance and Accident
completion.thinkerBio: Aristotle (384-322 BCE) developed the substance-accident distinction in the Categories and Metaphysics; it became central to medieval Scholastic philosophy and remains foundational for debates about the nature of objects and properties.
completion.quote: Substance is that which is not predicated of anything else, but of which everything else is predicated.

related.name: The Problem of Universals
related.note: The substance-accident distinction underlies debates about universals -- accidents are the properties substances instantiate.

related.name: Essentialism
related.note: Essential properties are those a substance cannot lose without ceasing to exist; accidental properties can change without destroying identity.

related.name: Identity Over Time
related.note: A substance persisting through accidental change while remaining numerically the same thing is the classical model of identity over time.

related.name: Process Philosophy
related.note: Whitehead's process philosophy denies that substance is the fundamental category -- events and processes replace substances as the basic units of reality.

books.title: Categories
books.author: Aristotle
books.why: The primary source -- Aristotle's original taxonomy of substance and the nine categories of accident.

books.title: Aristotle's Metaphysics
books.author: Aristotle (trans. Hugh Lawson-Tancred)
books.why: Aristotle's mature development of substance theory in Metaphysics Books Z, H, and Theta.

books.title: Substance: Its Nature and Existence
books.author: Joshua Hoffman and Gary Rosenkrantz
books.why: The most thorough contemporary analytic treatment of substance ontology.

completion.name: Supererogation
completion.thinkerBio: J.O. Urmson (1915–2012) introduced the concept in a 1958 paper showing that standard moral theories lack a category for saintly or heroic acts beyond duty; Joel Feinberg (1926–2004) and David Heyd subsequently developed its systematic philosophical analysis.
completion.quote: Some acts go beyond what duty requires — they are not obligatory, yet they are genuinely praiseworthy.

related.name: Utilitarianism
related.note: Utilitarianism collapses supererogation — if you can produce more good, you are simply obligated to do so, leaving no space for acts beyond duty.

related.name: Virtue Ethics
related.note: Virtue ethics has natural space for supererogation through the concept of the morally excellent character that exceeds minimal duty.

related.name: Autonomy and Paternalism
related.note: Agent-relative prerogatives — the permission to give special weight to one's own projects — preserve supererogation against utilitarian collapse.

related.name: Cosmopolitanism and Obligations
related.note: Singer's utilitarian argument eliminates supererogation in global aid — what most people call charity becomes obligatory on his reasoning.

books.title: Doing Good Better
books.author: William MacAskill
books.why: Engages directly with whether giving is supererogatory or obligatory — the most practically important contemporary version of the debate.

books.title: The Demands of Morality
books.author: Liam Murphy
books.why: Murphy's cooperative principle attempts to set limits on moral demand — a direct engagement with supererogation's theoretical context.

books.title: Practical Ethics
books.author: Peter Singer
books.why: Singer's demanding utilitarian position that eliminates the supererogatory/obligatory distinction for affluent people.

completion.name: Taoism and Nature
completion.thinkerBio: Laozi and Zhuangzi developed Taoism's philosophy of nature as the primary manifestation of spontaneous, self-organizing cosmic order.
completion.quote: Nature is the most transparent expression of the way things naturally unfold when not distorted by human interference.

related.name: The Tao
related.note: Taoist nature philosophy is grounded in the broader metaphysics of the Tao as cosmic principle.

related.name: Wu Wei
related.note: Wu wei is the practical corollary of Taoist nature philosophy -- acting in alignment with natural process.

related.name: Environmental Ethics
related.note: Taoism's critique of domination over nature has made it a resource for contemporary environmental philosophy.

books.title: Tao Te Ching
books.author: Laozi (trans. Ursula K. Le Guin)
books.why: The primary source for Taoist nature philosophy.

books.title: The Complete Works of Zhuangzi
books.author: trans. Burton Watson
books.why: Zhuangzi's deeper literary and philosophical development of natural spontaneity.

books.title: Nature in Asian Traditions of Thought
books.author: J. Baird Callicott and Roger Ames (eds.)
books.why: Comparative philosophical essays connecting Taoist and other Asian nature philosophies to environmental ethics.

completion.name: Testimony
completion.thinkerBio: David Hume (1711-1776) gave the classic reductionist treatment of testimony in An Enquiry Concerning Human Understanding; C.A.J. Coady (b. 1936) defended anti-reductionism in Testimony: A Philosophical Study (1992), arguing testimony is a basic, irreducible source of knowledge.
completion.quote: Testimony is the default-and-challenge structure of human knowledge transmission -- we believe what others tell us unless given specific reason for doubt.

related.name: Epistemic Injustice
related.note: Testimonial injustice is the systematic deflation of a speaker's credibility based on social identity -- a central application of testimony theory.

related.name: Standpoint Epistemology
related.note: Both testimony theory and standpoint epistemology examine how social position affects the reception and credibility of knowledge claims.

related.name: Social Epistemology
related.note: Testimony is the paradigm case studied by social epistemology -- most human knowledge is acquired through trusting others rather than direct observation.

related.name: Skepticism
related.note: Hume's reductionist account of testimony treats it skeptically -- testimonial belief requires independent inductive evidence for the reliability of the source.

books.title: Testimony: A Philosophical Study
books.author: C.A.J. Coady
books.why: The foundational contemporary text defending anti-reductionism about testimonial knowledge.

books.title: Epistemic Injustice
books.author: Miranda Fricker
books.why: Fricker's extension of testimony theory to the ethics of credibility and social power.

books.title: An Enquiry Concerning Human Understanding
books.author: David Hume
books.why: Hume's classical reductionist account of testimony, requiring independent evidence of source reliability.

completion.name: The Absurd
completion.thinkerBio: Albert Camus (1913–1960) was an Algerian-French novelist and philosopher who developed the absurd in The Myth of Sisyphus (1942) and extended it politically in The Rebel (1951); he won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1957 and died in a car accident at 46.
completion.quote: There is but one truly serious philosophical problem, and that is suicide.

related.name: Nihilism
related.note: Both begin from the recognition that the universe provides no inherent meaning, but Camus responds with revolt rather than Nietzsche's value-creation.

related.name: Authenticity
related.note: Camus's revolt is a form of authenticity — living without hope or despair, fully aware of the absurd, refusing both faith and suicide.

related.name: Eternal Recurrence
related.note: Both Nietzsche's eternal recurrence and Camus's absurd revolt demand affirmation of life without transcendent justification.

related.name: Absurdism and Revolt
related.note: The Myth of Sisyphus introduces the absurd; The Rebel develops its political implications — revolt as the basis for a non-totalitarian politics.

books.title: The Myth of Sisyphus
books.author: Albert Camus
books.why: The foundational text of absurdism — Camus's argument that Sisyphus must be imagined happy.

books.title: The Stranger
books.author: Albert Camus
books.why: The literary embodiment of the absurd — best read alongside the Myth.

books.title: The Rebel
books.author: Albert Camus
books.why: Camus's political philosophy — revolt as the basis for a non-totalitarian politics.

completion.name: The Aesthetic Experience
completion.thinkerBio: John Dewey (1859-1952) developed his pragmatist aesthetics in Art as Experience (1934), arguing that aesthetic quality is continuous with all meaningful experience rather than a separate, elevated category restricted to fine art.
completion.quote: Art is not the possession of the few who are recognized writers, painters, musicians; it is the authentic expression of any and all individuality.

related.name: Aesthetic Disinterestedness
related.note: Dewey rejects the Kantian model of disinterested contemplation in favor of aesthetic experience as engaged, consummatory activity continuous with ordinary life.

related.name: Aesthetic Experience and Flow
related.note: Csikszentmihalyi's empirical concept of flow provides a scientific complement to Dewey's philosophical account of consummatory aesthetic engagement.

related.name: Pragmatism
related.note: Dewey's aesthetics is the application of his broader pragmatist philosophy of experience and inquiry to the specific domain of art.

related.name: Environmental Aesthetics
related.note: Both Dewey's aesthetics and environmental aesthetics challenge the restriction of aesthetic value to fine art objects viewed from a distance.

books.title: Art as Experience
books.author: John Dewey
books.why: Dewey's foundational pragmatist aesthetics -- the case for continuity between aesthetic and ordinary experience.

books.title: Experience and Nature
books.author: John Dewey
books.why: Dewey's broader philosophical framework of experience that grounds his specific aesthetic theory.

books.title: Flow: The Psychology of Optimal Experience
books.author: Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi
books.why: The empirical psychological complement to Dewey's philosophical account of consummatory experience.

completion.name: The Anthropic Cosmological Principle
completion.thinkerBio: John Barrow (1952-2020) and Frank Tipler (b. 1947) developed the comprehensive treatment of the anthropic principle in their 1986 book of the same name.
completion.quote: The observed values of physical constants constrain the times and locations at which observers can exist.

related.name: The Anthropic Principle
related.note: This entry develops the fuller cosmological treatment that the introductory anthropic principle entry summarizes.

related.name: Possible Worlds
related.note: The multiverse response to fine-tuning applies possible-worlds-style reasoning to physical cosmology.

related.name: Inference to Best Explanation
related.note: Whether the anthropic principle explains fine-tuning or merely restates a selection effect is a question of explanatory adequacy.

books.title: The Anthropic Cosmological Principle
books.author: Barrow and Tipler
books.why: The definitive comprehensive treatment across physics and philosophy.

books.title: Universes
books.author: John Leslie
books.why: Leslie's philosophical analysis including the firing squad analogy.

books.title: A Fortunate Universe
books.author: Lewis and Barnes
books.why: A rigorous contemporary treatment of cosmic fine-tuning.

completion.name: The Anthropic Principle
completion.thinkerBio: Brandon Carter (b. 1942) introduced the weak and strong anthropic principles in 1973; John Barrow (1952-2020) and Frank Tipler (b. 1947) developed the most comprehensive philosophical and physical analysis in The Anthropic Cosmological Principle (1986).
completion.quote: We should not be surprised that the universe permits the existence of life, because if it did not, we would not be here to observe it.

related.name: Possible Worlds
related.note: The multiverse response to fine-tuning applies possible-worlds-style reasoning to cosmology -- we necessarily find ourselves in a world permitting observers.

related.name: The Anthropic Cosmological Principle
related.note: This entry surveys the broader cosmological development of the anthropic principle beyond its initial formulation.

related.name: Inference to Best Explanation
related.note: Whether the anthropic principle explains fine-tuning or merely restates the selection effect is a live question of explanatory adequacy.

related.name: Scientific Realism
related.note: Debates about the anthropic principle parallel debates about scientific realism -- whether unfalsifiable multiverse theories constitute genuine science.

books.title: The Anthropic Cosmological Principle
books.author: John Barrow and Frank Tipler
books.why: The definitive comprehensive treatment of the anthropic principle across physics and philosophy.

books.title: Universes
books.author: John Leslie
books.why: Leslie's philosophical analysis including the famous firing squad analogy for fine-tuning.

books.title: A Fortunate Universe
books.author: Geraint Lewis and Luke Barnes
books.why: A rigorous contemporary scientific and philosophical treatment of cosmic fine-tuning.

completion.name: The Arrow of Time
completion.thinkerBio: Ludwig Boltzmann (1844-1906) explained the thermodynamic arrow of time through statistical mechanics; Huw Price (b. 1953) developed the perspectival account holding that temporal asymmetry reflects our own epistemic position rather than an objective feature of physical reality.
completion.quote: The apparent asymmetry of time reflects our own asymmetric epistemic position -- we remember the past but not the future because we are in causal contact only with the past.

related.name: The Reality of Time
related.note: The arrow of time is closely connected to debates about whether temporal passage is objectively real or merely a feature of our perspective within the block universe.

related.name: Causation
related.note: The direction of causation is typically aligned with the thermodynamic arrow of time, raising deep questions about whether causal asymmetry is itself fundamental or derived.

related.name: Time and Existence
related.note: Both the physics of time's arrow and existentialist analysis of lived temporality address fundamental questions about the nature and direction of temporal experience.

related.name: Naturalism
related.note: Price's perspectival account exemplifies a thoroughly naturalistic approach, explaining apparent temporal asymmetry through our physical and epistemic situation within the universe.

books.title: Time's Arrow and Archimedes' Point
books.author: Huw Price
books.why: Price's foundational development of the perspectival account of temporal asymmetry.

books.title: From Eternity to Here
books.author: Sean Carroll
books.why: An accessible and rigorous contemporary physics-based treatment of the arrow of time and entropy.

books.title: The Direction of Time
books.author: Hans Reichenbach
books.why: Reichenbach's classic philosophical treatment connecting entropy, causation, and temporal direction.

completion.name: The Bhagavad Gita and Action
completion.thinkerBio: The Bhagavad Gita, embedded in the epic Mahabharata, is traditionally attributed to the sage Vyasa and dates to roughly the second century BCE to second century CE; it remains the single most influential text in Hindu philosophy and practice.
completion.quote: You have the right to perform your prescribed duties, but you are not entitled to the fruits of your actions.

related.name: Dharma
related.note: The Gita's central teaching is fulfilling one's dharma without attachment to results -- nishkama karma is the ideal of dharmic action.

related.name: Karma
related.note: The Gita develops the most philosophically sophisticated treatment of karma through the doctrine of action performed without attachment to outcomes.

related.name: Stoic Ethics
related.note: Both the Gita's nishkama karma and Stoic ethics counsel performing one's duty fully while remaining detached from outcomes outside one's control.

related.name: Moksha
related.note: The Gita synthesizes the major paths to moksha -- knowledge, devotion, and selfless action -- as complementary rather than competing routes.

books.title: The Bhagavad Gita
books.author: trans. Barbara Stoler Miller
books.why: The primary source -- an elegant and philosophically attentive translation of the foundational text.

books.title: The Bhagavad Gita: A New Translation
books.author: trans. Stephen Mitchell
books.why: A literary translation with an accessible introduction connecting the Gita's themes to contemporary spiritual concerns.

books.title: The Bhagavad Gita for Daily Living
books.author: Eknath Easwaran
books.why: A practical commentary connecting the Gita's philosophy of action to everyday ethical life.

completion.name: The Binding Problem
completion.thinkerBio: Anne Treisman (1935-2018) developed feature integration theory proposing attention binds separately processed features into unified objects; Francis Crick contributed to neuroscientific approaches investigating the neural mechanisms underlying perceptual unity.
completion.quote: How does the brain combine separately processed features -- color, shape, motion, location -- into the unified experience of a single object?

related.name: Attention and Consciousness
related.note: Treisman's feature integration theory proposes attention is the mechanism that binds separately processed features into unified perceptual objects.

related.name: Hard Problem of Consciousness
related.note: Solving the neural binding problem does not explain why unified neural representations are accompanied by unified subjective experience, connecting directly to the hard problem.

related.name: Phenomenology of Perception
related.note: Merleau-Ponty's account of unified embodied perception anticipates the binding problem's concern with how disparate sensory information becomes a single experienced object.

related.name: Functionalism
related.note: Functionalist accounts of binding treat perceptual unity as a matter of appropriate functional integration across cognitive subsystems.

books.title: The Binding Problem
books.author: Alexandra Cleeremans (ed.)
books.why: A comprehensive scientific and philosophical anthology on the binding problem and perceptual unity.

books.title: The Astonishing Hypothesis
books.author: Francis Crick
books.why: Crick's neuroscientific approach connecting binding to broader questions about the neural basis of consciousness.

books.title: Feature Integration Theory
books.author: Anne Treisman, in Cognitive Psychology
books.why: Treisman's foundational original development of feature integration theory.

completion.name: The Capabilities Approach
completion.thinkerBio: Amartya Sen (b. 1933) introduced the capabilities approach as an alternative to welfare economics in the 1980s; Martha Nussbaum (b. 1947) developed it into a comprehensive philosophical theory of justice and human development with a specific list of central capabilities.
completion.quote: Development is the expansion of human capabilities -- the freedoms people have to achieve the kinds of lives they have reason to value.

related.name: Eudaimonia
related.note: Nussbaum's capabilities approach is the most systematic contemporary attempt to operationalize Aristotelian eudaimonia for development policy and political philosophy.

related.name: Consequentialism and Its Limits
related.note: Sen developed the capabilities approach partly to preserve consequentialist insights while avoiding strict utilitarian welfare aggregation's limits.

related.name: Feminist Political Philosophy
related.note: Nussbaum applies the capabilities approach specifically to women's global flourishing, addressing gendered barriers to capability development.

related.name: Desire-Satisfaction Theory
related.note: The capabilities approach rejects pure desire-satisfaction as a measure of wellbeing, insisting that some capabilities are objectively valuable regardless of whether they are desired.

books.title: Development as Freedom
books.author: Amartya Sen
books.why: Sen's foundational and accessible statement of the capabilities approach to development and freedom.

books.title: Creating Capabilities
books.author: Martha Nussbaum
books.why: Nussbaum's systematic philosophical development of the capabilities approach with her specific list of central capabilities.

books.title: Inequality Reexamined
books.author: Amartya Sen
books.why: Sen's more technical philosophical treatment connecting capabilities to debates about equality and justice.

completion.name: The Causal Theory of Reference
completion.thinkerBio: Saul Kripke (1940-2022) and Hilary Putnam (1926-2016) developed the causal-historical account of how names and natural kind terms secure their reference.
completion.quote: Reference is fixed through an initial baptism and a causal-historical chain of transmission, not through descriptive knowledge.

related.name: Reference and Rigid Designation
related.note: The causal theory explains how rigid designation is secured through baptism and transmission.

related.name: Semantic Externalism
related.note: Putnam's semantic externalism extends the causal theory to natural kind terms.

related.name: Names and Descriptions
related.note: The causal theory replaces Russellian descriptivism about how names refer.

books.title: Naming and Necessity
books.author: Saul Kripke
books.why: The foundational development of the causal theory for names.

books.title: Mind, Language and Reality
books.author: Hilary Putnam
books.why: Putnam's extension to natural kind terms via Twin Earth.

books.title: Meaning and Reference
books.author: A.W. Moore (ed.)
books.why: An anthology situating the causal theory within broader reference debates.

completion.name: The Concept of De
completion.thinkerBio: De is discussed throughout both classical Confucian texts including the Analects and classical Daoist texts including the Tao Te Ching, representing one of the most semantically rich and philosophically contested terms in classical Chinese thought.
completion.quote: The Tao gives birth to all things; de nurtures them, develops them, and brings them to maturity.

related.name: The Tao
related.note: De is the particular expression of the Tao in each individual thing -- the Tao is universal, de is its specific manifestation.

related.name: Confucian Ren
related.note: In Confucian usage, de is cultivated moral virtue closely related to ren -- the charismatic power that attracts others without coercion.

related.name: Wu Wei
related.note: A person of great de acts without effort because they are perfectly aligned with the natural order rather than straining against it through deliberate force.

related.name: Virtue Ethics
related.note: Confucian de and Aristotelian virtue both name the cultivated excellence of character that gives a person their distinctive moral and social efficacy.

books.title: Tao Te Ching
books.author: Laozi (trans. Ursula K. Le Guin)
books.why: The title itself -- 'Classic of the Way and Its Virtue' -- places de at the center of the text's philosophical concern.

books.title: The Analects
books.author: Confucius (trans. Edward Slingerland)
books.why: Confucius's development of de as cultivated moral virtue and its political significance.

books.title: Early Chinese Religion
books.author: John Lagerwey and Marc Kalinowski (eds.)
books.why: Scholarly essays tracing the historical development of the concept of de across early Chinese religious and philosophical texts.

completion.name: The Demarcation Problem
completion.thinkerBio: Karl Popper (1902-1994) proposed falsifiability as the criterion of demarcation between science and pseudoscience; Imre Lakatos (1922-1974) developed the more historically sophisticated criterion of progressive versus degenerative research programmes.
completion.quote: What distinguishes genuine science from pseudoscience, metaphysics, and non-science?

related.name: Falsifiability
related.note: Falsifiability is Popper's original proposed solution to the demarcation problem, though it has been substantially refined by subsequent philosophers of science.

related.name: Paradigm Shift
related.note: Kuhn's account of normal science and paradigm shifts complicates simple demarcation criteria by showing how science protects its core commitments.

related.name: Scientific Revolutions Reconsidered
related.note: Lakatos's sophisticated falsificationism and Feyerabend's epistemological anarchism represent opposing responses to the demarcation problem's difficulties.

related.name: Underdetermination
related.note: If theory choice is always underdetermined by evidence, sharp demarcation between science and non-science becomes correspondingly harder to draw precisely.

books.title: The Logic of Scientific Discovery
books.author: Karl Popper
books.why: Popper's foundational proposal of falsifiability as the criterion of scientific demarcation.

books.title: The Methodology of Scientific Research Programmes
books.author: Imre Lakatos
books.why: Lakatos's sophisticated falsificationism -- the most historically informed refinement of the demarcation criterion.

books.title: But Is It Science?
books.author: Robert Pennock and Michael Ruse (eds.)
books.why: An anthology applying the demarcation problem directly to the creationism-evolution controversy.

completion.name: The Digital and Virtual Aesthetics
completion.thinkerBio: Walter Benjamin (1892-1940) anticipated digital aesthetics through his analysis of mechanical reproduction in 1935; Lev Manovich (b. 1960) developed the systematic theory of new media's distinctive formal features in The Language of New Media (2001).
completion.quote: Reproduction destroys the aura of the original -- but this loss creates new possibilities, including art created with reproduction itself as its medium.

related.name: Mimesis
related.note: Digital aesthetics raises new questions about representation and reproduction that extend the ancient debate about mimesis into entirely new technical contexts.

related.name: The Ontology of Art
related.note: Digital works pose new ontological puzzles -- whether a digital work can have an original, and what constitutes the identity of an infinitely reproducible artwork.

related.name: Formalism in Art
related.note: Manovich's analysis of new media's distinctive formal features extends formalist aesthetics into the specifically computational domain.

related.name: The Spectacle
related.note: Both digital aesthetics and Debord's spectacle theory examine how new media technologies transform the nature of representation and authentic experience.

books.title: The Work of Art in the Age of Mechanical Reproduction
books.author: Walter Benjamin
books.why: Benjamin's foundational essay anticipating digital aesthetics through analysis of reproduction's effect on aura and authenticity.

books.title: The Language of New Media
books.author: Lev Manovich
books.why: Manovich's systematic theoretical account of the distinctive formal features of digital and new media art.

books.title: Aesthetics and the Philosophy of Art
books.author: Noel Carroll
books.why: A comprehensive contemporary survey that situates digital aesthetics within the broader landscape of philosophical aesthetics.

completion.name: The Eternal Return as Ethics
completion.thinkerBio: Friedrich Nietzsche (1844-1900) developed eternal recurrence as an ethical test in The Gay Science and Thus Spoke Zarathustra.
completion.quote: Can you will that you have done what you have done, forever?

related.name: Eternal Recurrence
related.note: This entry develops the ethical criterion the metaphysical doctrine of eternal recurrence generates.

related.name: Authenticity
related.note: Only an authentically lived life can pass the test of eternal recurrence.

related.name: Nihilism
related.note: The eternal return functions as Nietzsche's test for whether one can create meaning without cosmic backing.

books.title: The Gay Science
books.author: Friedrich Nietzsche
books.why: Eternal recurrence's first appearance as ethical test.

books.title: Thus Spoke Zarathustra
books.author: Friedrich Nietzsche
books.why: The fullest development as a test of amor fati.

books.title: Nietzsche on Truth and Philosophy
books.author: Maudemarie Clark
books.why: Rigorous analysis of eternal recurrence as ethics rather than cosmology.

completion.name: The Gaze
completion.thinkerBio: Jean-Paul Sartre (1905-1980) analyzed the ontological threat of the Other's gaze in Being and Nothingness; Jacques Lacan (1901-1981) reworked the gaze psychoanalytically; Laura Mulvey (b. 1941) applied it to film theory through the concept of the male gaze.
completion.quote: When another consciousness looks at me, I am transformed from a free subject who constitutes the world into a mere object in their world.

related.name: Bad Faith
related.note: Sartre's analysis of the Other's gaze connects to bad faith -- we often adopt the Other's objectifying view of us as our fixed, essential nature.

related.name: Embodiment and Feminist Philosophy
related.note: Mulvey's male gaze in film theory extends feminist philosophy of embodiment by showing how visual media structurally position women as objects of looking.

related.name: The Spectacle
related.note: Both the gaze and Debord's spectacle analyze how visual culture structures power relations through the organization of looking and representation.

related.name: The Unconscious
related.note: Lacan's psychoanalytic reworking of the gaze connects directly to his broader structural reformulation of the unconscious through the scopic drive.

books.title: Being and Nothingness
books.author: Jean-Paul Sartre
books.why: Sartre's foundational phenomenological analysis of the Other's look and its ontologically threatening character.

books.title: Visual Pleasure and Narrative Cinema
books.author: Laura Mulvey, in Visual and Other Pleasures
books.why: Mulvey's foundational feminist film theory essay introducing the concept of the male gaze.

books.title: The Four Fundamental Concepts of Psycho-Analysis
books.author: Jacques Lacan
books.why: Lacan's seminar developing the gaze as a structural feature of the scopic drive and the subject's relation to the visual field.

completion.name: The Gettier Problem
completion.thinkerBio: Edmund Gettier (b. 1927) published his three-page paper 'Is Justified True Belief Knowledge?' in Analysis in 1963, launching one of the most productive research programs in analytic epistemology; he taught at the University of Massachusetts Amherst for his entire career.
completion.quote: It is possible for a person to have justified true belief and yet fail to have knowledge.

related.name: Reliabilism
related.note: Goldman developed reliabilism partly in response to Gettier — knowledge requires reliably produced true belief, not just justified true belief.

related.name: Virtue Epistemology
related.note: Virtue epistemology responds to Gettier by requiring that knowledge succeed through the exercise of intellectual virtue, not just lucky coincidence.

related.name: Skepticism
related.note: Gettier cases show that justification and truth can align by luck in ways that fall short of knowledge, pressing skeptical questions into ordinary contexts.

related.name: Epistemic Injustice
related.note: Testimonial injustice produces systematic Gettier-like failures — speakers are denied knowledge attribution due to deflated credibility rather than faulty evidence.

books.title: Epistemology: An Anthology
books.author: Sosa, Kim, Fantl, McGrath (eds.)
books.why: Contains the original Gettier paper alongside all major responses — the essential collection for the JTB debate.

books.title: Knowledge and Its Limits
books.author: Timothy Williamson
books.why: Williamson's proposal that knowledge is primitive and unanalyzable — the most influential contemporary response to the failures of JTB analysis.

books.title: Theory of Knowledge
books.author: Roderick Chisholm
books.why: The classic attempt at a complete epistemological theory including multiple responses to the Gettier problem.

completion.name: The Grotesque
completion.thinkerBio: Mikhail Bakhtin (1895-1975) distinguished classical from Romantic grotesque in Rabelais and His World; Philip Thomson developed the analysis of unresolved comic-horrific tension.
completion.quote: The grotesque combines the comic and the horrifying without resolving the tension into either pure comedy or pure horror.

related.name: The Uncanny
related.note: Both the grotesque and the uncanny explore aesthetic categories built on transgression and the disturbance of comfortable boundaries.

related.name: Katharsis
related.note: The grotesque resists the clean emotional resolution that katharsis theory attributes to well-formed tragic or comic works.

related.name: Humor and Comedy
related.note: The grotesque combines comic elements with horror, complicating simple incongruity theories of humor.

books.title: Rabelais and His World
books.author: Mikhail Bakhtin
books.why: Bakhtin's foundational analysis of the carnivalesque grotesque.

books.title: The Grotesque in Art and Literature
books.author: Wolfgang Kayser
books.why: Kayser's classical study of the Romantic grotesque as sinister and estranging.

books.title: The Grotesque
books.author: Philip Thomson
books.why: Thomson's focused analysis of the grotesque's unresolved comic-horrific structure.

completion.name: The Grue Paradox
completion.thinkerBio: Nelson Goodman (1906-1998) introduced the grue paradox in Fact, Fiction, and Forecast (1955), showing the problem of induction runs deeper than Hume supposed by raising the question of which predicates are genuinely projectible.
completion.quote: All observed emeralds are both green and grue -- what principled reason do we have for projecting 'green' rather than 'grue' into the future?

related.name: The Problem of Induction
related.note: Goodman's grue paradox shows the problem of induction runs deeper than Hume supposed.

related.name: Essentialism
related.note: The grue paradox challenges naive essentialism by showing natural-seeming predicates may not correspond to genuine natural kinds.

related.name: Naturalized Epistemology
related.note: Goodman's pragmatic entrenchment solution is a naturalistic answer to projectibility.

related.name: Inference to Best Explanation
related.note: Both address what justifies preferring some hypotheses when multiple fit available evidence equally well.

books.title: Fact, Fiction, and Forecast
books.author: Nelson Goodman
books.why: Goodman's foundational text introducing the grue paradox.

books.title: The Problem of Induction and Its Solution
books.author: Wesley Salmon
books.why: Salmon's survey of attempted solutions to induction.

books.title: Ways of Worldmaking
books.author: Nelson Goodman
books.why: Goodman's later constructivist epistemology.

completion.name: The Hard Problem Revisited
completion.thinkerBio: David Chalmers (b. 1966) continues to develop responses to the hard problem; Keith Frankish (b. 1953) developed illusionism, arguing phenomenal consciousness as traditionally conceived is itself an introspective illusion.
completion.quote: Illusionism holds that phenomenal consciousness as traditionally understood does not exist -- introspection systematically misrepresents the character of our mental states.

related.name: Hard Problem of Consciousness
related.note: This entry surveys the contemporary landscape of responses including illusionism and Russellian monism.

related.name: Qualia
related.note: Illusionism directly denies the existence of qualia as traditionally conceived.

related.name: Panpsychism
related.note: Panpsychism and illusionism represent opposite extreme responses to the hard problem.

related.name: Functionalism
related.note: Illusionism is compatible with and often motivated by functionalist commitments.

books.title: Illusionism
books.author: Keith Frankish (ed.)
books.why: The essential anthology developing and debating illusionism.

books.title: The Character of Consciousness
books.author: David Chalmers
books.why: Chalmers's comprehensive treatment of contemporary responses to the hard problem.

books.title: Consciousness Explained
books.author: Daniel Dennett
books.why: Dennett's eliminativist groundwork that illusionism develops.

completion.name: The Intentionality of Consciousness
completion.thinkerBio: Franz Brentano (1838-1917) revived intentionality from medieval scholasticism as the defining mark of the mental in Psychology from an Empirical Standpoint (1874); Edmund Husserl (1859-1938), his student, made intentionality the foundation of phenomenology.
completion.quote: Every mental phenomenon is characterized by what the Scholastics called the intentional inexistence of an object.

related.name: Cogito Ergo Sum
related.note: Both the cogito and Brentano's intentionality make the first-person perspective philosophically foundational for understanding the mental.

related.name: Chinese Room
related.note: Searle's Chinese Room argument depends on the claim that genuine understanding requires intrinsic intentionality, which mere symbol manipulation cannot supply.

related.name: Phenomenology of Perception
related.note: Husserlian intentionality is the foundation that Merleau-Ponty's existential phenomenology of embodied perception builds upon and transforms.

related.name: Functionalism
related.note: Naturalizing intentionality in purely functional or causal terms remains one of the deepest challenges for physicalist philosophy of mind.

books.title: Psychology from an Empirical Standpoint
books.author: Franz Brentano
books.why: The foundational text reviving intentionality as the defining mark of the mental.

books.title: Logical Investigations
books.author: Edmund Husserl
books.why: Husserl's foundational development of intentionality into the systematic method of phenomenology.

books.title: Intentionality
books.author: John Searle
books.why: Searle's contemporary analytic treatment of intentionality, connecting it to speech acts and philosophy of mind.

completion.name: The Language of Thought
completion.thinkerBio: Jerry Fodor (1935-2017) developed the language of thought hypothesis in The Language of Thought (1975), proposing that cognition occurs in a compositional mental symbol system he called mentalese.
completion.quote: Thinking is systematic and productive because it occurs in a compositional, language-like system of mental representation -- mentalese.

related.name: Chinese Room
related.note: Searle's Chinese Room directly challenges Fodor's hypothesis -- syntax alone, however systematically organized, may not be sufficient to generate genuine semantic content.

related.name: Semantic Externalism
related.note: If meaning depends partly on external facts about the world, this complicates Fodor's picture of thought as purely internal symbol manipulation.

related.name: Functionalism
related.note: The language of thought hypothesis is the most developed functionalist cognitive architecture, treating mental states as computational relations to mentalese symbols.

related.name: Imagination and Mental Imagery
related.note: If mental imagery is genuinely depictive rather than propositional, this challenges Fodor's claim that all cognition operates through a language-like symbolic medium.

books.title: The Language of Thought
books.author: Jerry Fodor
books.why: Fodor's foundational text introducing the language of thought hypothesis and mentalese.

books.title: Concepts: Where Cognitive Science Went Wrong
books.author: Jerry Fodor
books.why: Fodor's later defence of an atomistic, classical theory of concepts against prototype-based alternatives.

books.title: Connectionism and the Mind
books.author: William Bechtel and Adele Abrahamsen
books.why: The clearest accessible overview of the classical versus connectionist debate that the language of thought hypothesis sits at the center of.

completion.name: The Leap of Faith
completion.thinkerBio: Soren Kierkegaard (1813-1855) developed the leap of faith through the Abraham story in Fear and Trembling (1843).
completion.quote: Faith begins precisely where thought stops -- it cannot be justified from the outside.

related.name: Despair
related.note: The leap of faith is Kierkegaard's proposed resolution to the despair of the self's incoherent relation to itself.

related.name: Existential Communication
related.note: The leap cannot be communicated directly; Kierkegaard's indirect strategy is designed to provoke rather than describe it.

related.name: The Absurd
related.note: Camus explicitly rejects the leap of faith as philosophical suicide, an evasion of the absurd rather than its confrontation.

books.title: Fear and Trembling
books.author: Soren Kierkegaard
books.why: The primary source developing the leap of faith through Abraham.

books.title: Concluding Unscientific Postscript
books.author: Soren Kierkegaard
books.why: Kierkegaard's fuller theoretical treatment of faith and subjectivity.

books.title: Kierkegaard's Writings
books.author: Hong and Hong (trans.)
books.why: The definitive scholarly translation series.

completion.name: The Liar's Paradox
completion.thinkerBio: The paradox is attributed to Epimenides the Cretan (sixth century BCE); Alfred Tarski (1901–1983) provided the most influential formal response through the hierarchy of metalanguages in 'The Concept of Truth in Formalized Languages' (1933).
completion.quote: This sentence is false.

related.name: Formal Logic
related.note: The Liar's Paradox reveals the limits of naive truth theories — Tarski's response requires hierarchical metalanguages to avoid the contradiction.

related.name: Vagueness
related.note: Both the Liar's Paradox and vagueness challenge the classical principle of bivalence — every proposition is either true or false.

related.name: The Nature of Truth
related.note: The paradox motivates deflationary and hierarchical truth theories by revealing the limits of correspondence and naive truth.

related.name: Logical Consequence
related.note: Paraconsistent logics — which tolerate the Liar without explosion — represent alternative accounts of logical consequence.

books.title: Paradoxes
books.author: R.M. Sainsbury
books.why: The best accessible philosophical treatment of the Liar and other paradoxes of self-reference.

books.title: Logic and Structure
books.author: Dirk van Dalen
books.why: Includes formal treatment of the Liar's Paradox and its implications for logic.

books.title: Truth and the Absence of Fact
books.author: Hartry Field
books.why: Field's essays on truth and the Liar — the most technically sophisticated contemporary engagement.

completion.name: The Mahayana Bodhisattva Ideal
completion.thinkerBio: Shantideva (eighth century CE) developed the most philosophically sophisticated defence of the bodhisattva ideal in the Bodhicaryavatara.
completion.quote: The suffering of others is as real as one's own suffering -- the arbitrary preference for one's own welfare rests on the illusion of a fixed self.

related.name: Buddhist Ethics
related.note: The bodhisattva ideal extends Buddhist ethical concern from personal liberation to universal compassion.

related.name: Anatta
related.note: Shantideva's argument for universal compassion depends directly on the doctrine of no-self undermining self-preference.

related.name: Utilitarianism
related.note: The bodhisattva's impartial compassion has structural affinities with utilitarian impartiality, though grounded in different metaphysics.

books.title: A Guide to the Bodhisattva's Way of Life
books.author: Shantideva
books.why: The primary source developing the bodhisattva ideal philosophically.

books.title: Altruism and Reality
books.author: Paul Williams
books.why: A rigorous philosophical analysis of Shantideva's argument for universal compassion.

books.title: Mahayana Buddhism
books.author: Paul Williams
books.why: Comprehensive scholarly context for the bodhisattva ideal within Mahayana thought.

completion.name: The Meaning of Life
completion.thinkerBio: Susan Wolf (b. 1952) at UNC Chapel Hill developed her two-condition theory of meaning in Meaning in Life and Why It Matters (2010); Thomas Nagel's earlier essay 'The Absurd' identified the structural source of meaninglessness in the collision of seriousness and cosmic insignificance.
completion.quote: Meaning arises from active engagement in projects of worth, combining subjective attraction with genuine objective value -- neither alone suffices.

related.name: The Absurd
related.note: Both Wolf's meaning theory and Camus's confrontation with the absurd address what makes life worth living, though through very different philosophical methods.

related.name: Eudaimonia
related.note: Wolf's meaningful life and Aristotle's eudaimonia are related but distinct -- eudaimonia is the objective good life; meaningful life adds the subjective engagement condition.

related.name: Hedonism
related.note: Wolf explicitly denies that meaning reduces to either pleasure or pure subjective satisfaction -- it requires engagement with what is genuinely, objectively valuable.

related.name: Nihilism
related.note: Wolf's account of meaning is a direct contemporary analytic answer to the nihilist challenge -- it specifies what makes life meaningful without requiring cosmic or religious backing.

books.title: Meaning in Life and Why It Matters
books.author: Susan Wolf
books.why: Wolf's systematic and highly influential account of meaning as active engagement in projects of genuine worth.

books.title: Mortal Questions
books.author: Thomas Nagel
books.why: Contains 'The Absurd,' Nagel's foundational essay on the structural source of meaninglessness in human self-reflection.

books.title: The Meaning of Life
books.author: E.D. Klemke (ed.)
books.why: The essential anthology covering the full range of philosophical views on life's meaning from existentialism to analytic philosophy.

completion.name: The Measurement Problem
completion.thinkerBio: John von Neumann (1903-1957) formalized the collapse postulate in quantum mechanics; Hugh Everett (1930-1982) proposed the many-worlds interpretation in his 1957 doctoral thesis as an alternative that avoids collapse entirely.
completion.quote: When a measurement is made, we observe a definite outcome -- the measurement problem asks how this emerges from an indefinite quantum superposition.

related.name: Scientific Realism
related.note: Different interpretations of quantum mechanics represent fundamentally different metaphysical pictures of reality, making the measurement problem a central test case for scientific realism.

related.name: Possible Worlds
related.note: Everett's many-worlds interpretation multiplies ontology by treating every possible outcome as realized in a distinct branch, echoing modal realism's commitment to the reality of alternative possibilities.

related.name: Underdetermination
related.note: The choice between quantum interpretations is famously underdetermined by experimental evidence -- it is a philosophical rather than purely empirical choice.

related.name: Causation
related.note: Quantum mechanics challenges classical assumptions about determinism and causal structure that ground much of the traditional philosophical analysis of causation.

books.title: Quantum Theory and Measurement
books.author: John Wheeler and Wojciech Zurek (eds.)
books.why: The essential historical anthology collecting foundational papers on the quantum measurement problem.

books.title: The Many-Worlds Interpretation of Quantum Mechanics
books.author: Hugh Everett, in DeWitt and Graham (eds.)
books.why: Everett's original thesis proposing the many-worlds interpretation, included in the foundational anthology.

books.title: Quantum Mechanics and Experience
books.author: David Albert
books.why: The clearest accessible philosophical introduction to the measurement problem and the major interpretive options.

completion.name: The Mind-Body Problem
completion.thinkerBio: Rene Descartes (1596-1650) gave the mind-body problem its canonical modern formulation through substance dualism; the problem has generated every major position in contemporary philosophy of mind from physicalism to panpsychism.
completion.quote: How can an unextended thinking substance causally interact with an extended physical body?

related.name: Substance Dualism
related.note: Substance dualism is Descartes's original answer to the mind-body problem -- mind and body are two distinct kinds of substance.

related.name: Functionalism
related.note: Functionalism is a contemporary physicalist answer -- mental states are functional roles realized by physical states, dissolving the interaction problem.

related.name: Panpsychism
related.note: Panpsychism offers another answer -- consciousness is fundamental and physical, avoiding both Cartesian dualism and physicalist reduction.

related.name: Hard Problem of Consciousness
related.note: The hard problem is the contemporary articulation of why the mind-body problem resists straightforward physicalist resolution.

books.title: Meditations on First Philosophy
books.author: Rene Descartes
books.why: The text that gave the mind-body problem its canonical modern formulation.

books.title: Philosophy of Mind
books.author: Jaegwon Kim
books.why: The clearest comprehensive textbook surveying every major position on the mind-body problem.

books.title: The Mind-Body Problem
books.author: Richard Warner and Tadeusz Szubka (eds.)
books.why: An anthology of classic and contemporary papers spanning the full range of positions.

completion.name: The Nature of Truth
completion.thinkerBio: Alfred Tarski (1901-1983) developed the semantic theory of truth; William James (1842-1910) developed the pragmatist alternative treating truth as what works.
completion.quote: 'Snow is white' is true if and only if snow is white -- Tarski's schema captures the formal structure any adequate truth theory must satisfy.

related.name: Meaning and Truth Conditions
related.note: Truth-conditional semantics presupposes a substantive theory of what truth itself consists in.

related.name: Pragmatism
